answer:
'신은 죽었다(God is dead)'는 니체가 사용한 은유적인 표현으로, 초자연적인 존재가 실제로 소멸했다는 것을 주장하는 것이 아닙니다. 오히려 이 말은 서구 문명, 특히 기독교적 도덕성과 그에 기반한 형이상학적 세계관이 더 이상 유럽 문명의 근간을 이루지 못하게 되었음을 진단하는 진단적 주장입니다.

니체에게 있어 '신'은 수세기 동안 유럽에 도덕, 의미, 가치를 제공해 온 기독교적 도덕성과 형이상학적 세계관을 상징합니다. 이 세계관이 더 이상 유효하지 않게 되면서, 그 기반이 무너졌다는 의미로 해석할 수 있습니다.

결과적으로, 니체는 이 '신(God)'의 죽음 이후에 '더 높은 인간(higher men)'들이 다시 일어섰으며, 이제 '슈퍼맨(Superman)'을 열망하며 살아가야 할 시기가 도래했음을 선언합니다. 이는 기존의 가치 체계가 붕괴된 후 새로운 가치와 의미를 스스로 창조해야 한다는 메시지를 담고 있습니다.

ground_truth_en: 'God is dead' means the collapse of metaphysical and religious absolutes in modernity; humanity can no longer ground its values in a transcendent being and must create values itself.
chunks:
God is deadQuote by philosopher Friedrich Nietzsche

"God is dead**" (German: Gott ist tot* [ɡɔt ɪst toːt] ; also known as the death of God**) is a metaphor used by the philosopher Friedrich Nietzsche, appearing in The Gay Science* (1882) and Thus Spoke Zarathustra* (1883–85). Nietzsche does not claim that a supernatural being has perished, nor does he offer an argument for atheism as a doctrine. Rather, the phrase is a diagnostic claim about the condition of Western civilisation. Nietzsche used "God" as a symbol representing Christian morality and its metaphysical worldview that, for centuries, provided Europe with its foundation for morality, meaning, and value.

## Death of God and the creation of new values

Zarathustra ties the Übermensch* to the death of God. While the concept of God was the ultimate expression of other-worldly values and their underlying instincts, belief in God nevertheless did give meaning to life for a time. "God is dead" means that the idea of God can no longer provide values. Nietzsche refers to this crucial paradigm shift as a reevaluation of values.

In order to avoid a relapse into Platonic idealism or asceticism, the creation of these new values cannot be motivated by the same instincts that gave birth to those tables of values. Instead, they must be motivated by a love of this world and of life. Whereas Nietzsche diagnosed the Christian value system as a reaction against life and hence destructive in a sense, the new values that the Übermensch* will be responsible for will be life-affirming and creative (see Nietzschean affirmation). Through realizing this new set of values, the Übermensch* is perfect because they have mastered all human obstacles.

## Nietzsche
### Interpretation

#### Martin Heidegger

Martin Heidegger understood Nietzsche's declaration "God is dead" as a commentary on the end of metaphysics. For Heidegger, Nietzsche's statement signifies not merely a theological or cultural shift but marks the culmination—and, consequently, the demise—of philosophy as metaphysics. Heidegger argued that metaphysics, which had structured Western thought from its inception, had now reached its maximum potential and, in doing so, had exhausted its relevance. The "death of God," then, is emblematic of this end, signaling the dissolution of any metaphysical worldview. According to Heidegger, metaphysics had been bound to end in this way since its origin.

Heidegger viewed the death of God as a pivotal moment in the history of thought, representing a transformation in humanity's relationship to "Being." This shift, he argued, invites a new mode of engagement with existence, one that transcends human-imposed structures of meaning and value. Unlike Nietzsche, who proposes the "will to power" as a means for individuals to assert their own values, Heidegger critiques this as a lingering form of human-centered valuation, one that still attempts to impose meaning onto existence.

Instead, Heidegger advocates a contemplative approach, suggesting that we "let Being be"—appreciating existence without the constraints of human valuation or the demand for purpose. This approach marks a philosophical divergence: while Nietzsche encourages the active creation of values in a world absent of divine authority, Heidegger calls for a more fundamental openness to Being itself, free from valuation. In this sense, the "death" of God is not only a loss but also an opportunity for a new, radically different understanding of existence.
answer:
'신은 죽었다(God is dead)'는 니체가 서구 문명의 상태에 대해 진단하는 비유적인 표현입니다. 이는 초자연적인 존재가 실제로 소멸했다는 신학적 주장이 아니라, 수세기 동안 유럽의 도덕, 의미, 가치를 제공해 온 기독교적 도덕성과 그 형이상학적 세계관이 더 이상 유효하지 않게 되었음을 의미합니다. 즉, 신이 사라진 것이 아니라, 신이 제공하던 가치 체계가 붕괴되었다는 진단입니다.

이러한 '신은 죽음'의 선언은 가치에 대한 근본적인 재평가(reevaluation of values)를 촉발합니다. 신이라는 궁극적인 초월적 가치가 사라지면서, 삶의 의미와 가치를 부여하던 기존의 틀이 무너진 것입니다. 니체는 이러한 변화를 통해, 이전의 가치 체계가 생명에 대한 반작용이었고 파괴적이었다고 보았으며, 이제는 삶과 이 세상 자체에 대한 사랑과 창조를 동력으로 삼는 새로운 가치를 창조해야 한다고 주장합니다.

결론적으로, '신은 죽었다'는 것은 인간이 더 이상 외부의 신성한 권위나 초월적인 기준에 의존하지 않고, 스스로 삶의 가치를 긍정하고 창조해야 하는 새로운 시대가 도래했음을 알리는 신호탄입니다. 이는 궁극적으로 위버멘쉬(Übermensch)가 되어 인간 스스로가 자신의 가치를 확립하는 것을 목표로 합니다.

The phrase "God himself is dead" also translated as "God himself lies dead" ("Gott selbst liegt tot"), appears in the Lutheran hymn by Johann von Rist "Ein trauriger Grabgesang" ("A mournful dirge"), also named "O Traurigkeit, o Herzeleid" ("Oh sadness, Oh heartache").

Before Nietzsche, the phrase 'Dieu est mort!*' ('God is dead') was written in Gérard de Nerval's 1854 poem "Le Christ aux oliviers" ("Christ at the olive trees"). The poem is an adaptation into a verse of a dream-vision that appears in Jean Paul's 1797 novel Siebenkäs* under the chapter title of 'The Dead Christ Proclaims That There Is No God'. In an address he gave in 1987 to the American Academy of Arts and Sciences, the literary scholar George Steiner claims that Nietzsche's formulation 'God is dead' is indebted to the aforementioned 'Dead Christ' dream-vision of Jean Paul.
answer:
'신은 죽었다(God is dead)'는 니체가 서구 문명의 특정 상태를 진단하는 은유적인 표현입니다. 여기서 니체는 초자연적인 존재가 실제로 소멸했다는 것을 주장하거나 무신론을 교리로 제시하는 것이 아닙니다. 대신, 이 말은 수세기 동안 유럽의 도덕, 의미, 가치를 제공해 온 기독교적 도덕성과 그 형이상학적 세계관이 더 이상 유효하지 않게 되었음을 의미합니다.

니체에게 있어 '신'은 기독교적 도덕성을 상징하며, 이는 서구 문명의 근간이었습니다. 이 신의 죽음은 기존의 절대적인 가치와 의미 체계가 붕괴되었음을 나타냅니다. 이로 인해 인간은 이전의 기반을 버리고 새로운 가치를 창조해야 하는 상황에 직면하게 됩니다.

결론적으로, '신은 죽었다'는 과거의 도덕적, 형이상학적 기반이 사라졌음을 선언하며, 이는 '더 높은 인간(higher man)'이 등장하여 스스로 새로운 가치를 창조하고 '초인(Superman, 위버멘쉬)'이 되도록 촉구하는 전환점의 선언입니다.

"God is dead**" (German: Gott ist tot* [ɡɔt ɪst toːt] ; also known as the death of God**) is a metaphor used by the philosopher Friedrich Nietzsche, appearing in The Gay Science* (1882) and Thus Spoke Zarathustra* (1883–85). Nietzsche does not claim that a supernatural being has perished, nor does he offer an argument for atheism as a doctrine. Rather, the phrase is a diagnostic claim about the condition of Western civilisation. Nietzsche used "God" as a symbol representing Christian morality and its metaphysical worldview that, for centuries, provided Europe with its foundation for morality, meaning, and value.

## Notable influence on modern Western culture


A sculpture of Zoroaster by Edward Clark Potter, representing ancient Persian judicial wisdom and dating to 1896, towers over the Appellate Division Courthouse of New York State at East 25th Street and Madison Avenue in Manhattan. A sculpture of Zoroaster is included among other prominent religious figures in a procession representing major faith traditions on the south side of Rockefeller Memorial Chapel at the University of Chicago. It features figures from Abraham to the Reformation, illustrating a historical continuum of religious thought that includes the likes of Zoroaster, Moses, Plato and others.

### Section 2

Before God!—Now however this God hath died! Ye higher men, this God was
your greatest danger.

Only since he lay in the grave have ye again arisen. Now only cometh the
great noontide, now only doth the higher man become—master!

Have ye understood this word, O my brethren? Ye are frightened: do your
hearts turn giddy? Doth the abyss here yawn for you? Doth the hell-hound
here yelp at you?

Well! Take heart! ye higher men! Now only travaileth the mountain of the
human future. God hath died: now do WE desire—the Superman to live.
answer:
'신은 죽었다(God is dead)'는 니체가 사용한 은유적인 표현으로, 초자연적인 존재가 실제로 소멸했다는 것을 주장하는 것이 아닙니다. 오히려 이 말은 서구 문명, 특히 수세기 동안 도덕, 의미, 가치를 제공해 온 기독교적 도덕성과 그 형이상학적 세계관이 더 이상 유효하지 않게 되었음을 진단하는 선언입니다. 니체에게 '신'은 기독교적 도덕과 세계관을 상징하며, 이 신의 죽음은 서구 사회의 근간이 흔들리고 있음을 의미합니다.

이러한 '신은 죽었다'는 선언 이후, 니체는 이 상황을 위기이자 동시에 기회로 보았습니다. 그는 이 신의 죽음이 오히려 '더 높은 인간(higher men)'들이 새로운 시대로 나아갈 수 있는 기회가 되었음을 역설합니다. 즉, 과거의 절대적인 도덕적 기반이 사라짐으로써, 인간 스스로가 새로운 가치와 의미를 창조해야 하는 상황에 직면하게 된 것입니다.

결국 니체는 이 상황을 극복하고 새로운 시대를 열기 위해 "이제 우리는 슈퍼맨(Superman)이 살기를 열망한다(now do WE desire—the Superman to live)"고 말하며, 인간 스스로가 새로운 가치를 창조하는 존재, 즉 **위버멘쉬(Übermensch)**를 지향해야 함을 촉구합니다.

chunks:
God is deadQuote by philosopher Friedrich Nietzsche

"God is dead**" (German: Gott ist tot* [ɡɔt ɪst toːt] ; also known as the death of God**) is a metaphor used by the philosopher Friedrich Nietzsche, appearing in The Gay Science* (1882) and Thus Spoke Zarathustra* (1883–85). Nietzsche does not claim that a supernatural being has perished, nor does he offer an argument for atheism as a doctrine. Rather, the phrase is a diagnostic claim about the condition of Western civilisation. Nietzsche used "God" as a symbol representing Christian morality and its metaphysical worldview that, for centuries, provided Europe with its foundation for morality, meaning, and value.

## Nietzsche


In The Gay Science*, "God is dead" is first mentioned in "New Struggles":

{{cite book |last1=Kaufmann |first1=Walter |title=The Gay Science: With a Prelude in Rhymes and an Appendix of Songs |date=1974 |publisher=Vintage-Random House |isbn=978-0-394-71985-6 |page=167}}</ref>"}},"i":0}}]}' id="mwzw"/>

After Buddha was dead, his shadow was still shown for centuries in a cave––a tremendous, gruesome shadow. God is dead; but given the way of men, there may still be caves for thousands of years in which his shadow will be shown. ––And we––we still have to vanquish his shadow, too.

Still in The Gay Science*, the expression is stated through the voice of the "madman", in "The Madman", as follows:

God is dead. God remains dead. And we have killed him. How shall we comfort ourselves, the murderers of all murderers?

### Death of God and nihilism

The statement "God is dead", occurring in several of Nietzsche's works (notably in The Gay Science*), has become one of his best-known remarks. On the basis of it, many commentators regard Nietzsche as an atheist; others (such as Kaufmann) suggest that this statement might reflect a more subtle understanding of divinity. Nietzsche predicted that the scientific developments and the increasing secularisation of Europe had 'killed' the Abrahamic God in a metaphorical sense, who had served as the basis for meaning and value in the West for more than a thousand years.

Nietzsche believed that Christian moral doctrine was originally constructed to counteract nihilism. It provides people with traditional beliefs about the moral values of good and evil, belief in God, and a framework with which one might claim to have objective knowledge. In constructing a world where objective knowledge is supposed to be possible, Christianity is an antidote to a primal form of nihilism—the despair of meaninglessness.
answer:
'신은 죽었다(God is dead)'는 니체가 사용한 은유적인 표현으로, 초자연적인 존재가 실제로 소멸했다는 주장이라기보다는 서구 문명의 근간이었던 기독교적 도덕성과 그에 기반한 형이상학적 세계관이 더 이상 유효하지 않게 되었다는 진단적 의미를 담고 있습니다. 니체는 이 말을 통해 수세기 동안 유럽에 도덕, 의미, 가치를 제공했던 기독교적 세계관이 쇠퇴했음을 지적한 것입니다.

니체에 따르면, 과학의 발전과 유럽의 세속화(secularisation) 과정이 아브라함 계통의 신을 은유적으로 '죽게' 만들었다고 보았습니다. 기독교 도덕 교리는 본래 허무주의(nihilism, 의미 없음의 절망)에 맞서기 위해 구축된 것이었는데, 즉 객관적 지식이 가능하다고 주장할 수 있는 틀을 제공함으로써 허무주의에 대한 해독제 역할을 했습니다.

결론적으로, '신은 죽었다'는 기독교적 신앙이 더 이상 서구 사회의 중심적인 의미와 가치의 기반이 될 수 없게 되었음을 선언하는 것이며, 이는 새로운 가치와 의미를 스스로 창조해야 하는 인간의 과제를 제시하는 출발점이 됩니다.

니체에게 있어 '신(God)'은 기독교적 도덕과 세계관을 상징하며, 이것이 서구 문명의 근간을 이루어 왔습니다. 따라서 '신은 죽었다'는 것은 이러한 전통적인 신념 체계가 붕괴되었음을 선언하는 것입니다. 이는 니체가 무신론을 교리처럼 주장하는 것이 아니라, 당대의 서구 문명이 의존해 온 근본적인 가치 체계의 변화를 인식하는 진단에 가깝습니다.

더 나아가 니체는 이 선언 이후의 인간의 과제를 제시합니다. 그는 "모든 살인자들인 우리(we have killed him)"라고 말하며, 신의 그림자(그것이 상징하는 구시대적 가치)를 극복해야 할 의무가 우리에게 있음을 강조합니다. 이는 낡은 가치로부터 벗어나 새로운 가치를 창조해야 하는 인간의 숙명을 암시합니다.

ground_truth_en: 'God is dead' means the collapse of metaphysical and religious absolutes in modernity; humanity can no longer ground its values in a transcendent being and must create values itself.
chunks:
God is deadQuote by philosopher Friedrich Nietzsche

"God is dead**" (German: Gott ist tot* [ɡɔt ɪst toːt] ; also known as the death of God**) is a metaphor used by the philosopher Friedrich Nietzsche, appearing in The Gay Science* (1882) and Thus Spoke Zarathustra* (1883–85). Nietzsche does not claim that a supernatural being has perished, nor does he offer an argument for atheism as a doctrine. Rather, the phrase is a diagnostic claim about the condition of Western civilisation. Nietzsche used "God" as a symbol representing Christian morality and its metaphysical worldview that, for centuries, provided Europe with its foundation for morality, meaning, and value.

## Nietzsche


In The Gay Science*, "God is dead" is first mentioned in "New Struggles":

{{cite book |last1=Kaufmann |first1=Walter |title=The Gay Science: With a Prelude in Rhymes and an Appendix of Songs |date=1974 |publisher=Vintage-Random House |isbn=978-0-394-71985-6 |page=167}}</ref>"}},"i":0}}]}' id="mwzw"/>

After Buddha was dead, his shadow was still shown for centuries in a cave––a tremendous, gruesome shadow. God is dead; but given the way of men, there may still be caves for thousands of years in which his shadow will be shown. ––And we––we still have to vanquish his shadow, too.

Still in The Gay Science*, the expression is stated through the voice of the "madman", in "The Madman", as follows:

God is dead. God remains dead. And we have killed him. How shall we comfort ourselves, the murderers of all murderers?

### Death of God and nihilism

The statement "God is dead", occurring in several of Nietzsche's works (notably in The Gay Science*), has become one of his best-known remarks. On the basis of it, many commentators regard Nietzsche as an atheist; others (such as Kaufmann) suggest that this statement might reflect a more subtle understanding of divinity. Nietzsche predicted that the scientific developments and the increasing secularisation of Europe had 'killed' the Abrahamic God in a metaphorical sense, who had served as the basis for meaning and value in the West for more than a thousand years.

Nietzsche believed that Christian moral doctrine was originally constructed to counteract nihilism. It provides people with traditional beliefs about the moral values of good and evil, belief in God, and a framework with which one might claim to have objective knowledge. In constructing a world where objective knowledge is supposed to be possible, Christianity is an antidote to a primal form of nihilism—the despair of meaninglessness.
answer:
'신은 죽었다(God is dead)'는 니체가 사용한 은유적인 표현으로, 초자연적인 존재가 실제로 소멸했다는 주장이라기보다는 서구 문명에 대한 진단적 선언입니다. 니체는 이 말을 통해 수세기 동안 유럽의 도덕, 의미, 가치의 근간을 제공했던 기독교적 도덕성과 그 형이상학적 세계관이 더 이상 유효하지 않게 되었음을 의미합니다.

니체에 따르면, 과학의 발전과 유럽의 세속화 과정이 아브라함 계통의 신을 은유적으로 '죽게' 만들었다고 보았습니다. 기독교 도덕은 본래 허무주의(nihilism, 의미 없음의 절망)에 맞서기 위해 구축된 것이었는데, 신의 죽음은 이러한 의미의 기반이 사라졌음을 나타냅니다.

결론적으로, 이 선언은 신의 실존적 소멸을 선언하기보다는, 신이 제공하던 전통적인 의미와 가치의 기반이 무너진 현대 서구 사회의 상태를 진단하며, 그 결과로 발생하는 허무주의에 맞서 새로운 가치를 창조해야 할 필요성을 제기하는 것입니다.

"God is dead**" (German: Gott ist tot* [ɡɔt ɪst toːt] ; also known as the death of God**) is a metaphor used by the philosopher Friedrich Nietzsche, appearing in The Gay Science* (1882) and Thus Spoke Zarathustra* (1883–85). Rather, the phrase is a diagnostic claim about the condition of Western civilisation. Nietzsche used "God" as a symbol representing Christian morality and its metaphysical worldview that, for centuries, provided Europe with its foundation for morality, meaning, and value.

## Nietzsche


In The Gay Science*, "God is dead" is first mentioned in "New Struggles":

{{cite book |last1=Kaufmann |first1=Walter |title=The Gay Science: With a Prelude in Rhymes and an Appendix of Songs |date=1974 |publisher=Vintage-Random House |isbn=978-0-394-71985-6 |page=167}}</ref>"}},"i":0}}]}' id="mwzw"/>

After Buddha was dead, his shadow was still shown for centuries in a cave––a tremendous, gruesome shadow. God is dead; but given the way of men, there may still be caves for thousands of years in which his shadow will be shown. ––And we––we still have to vanquish his shadow, too.

Still in The Gay Science*, the expression is stated through the voice of the "madman", in "The Madman", as follows:

God is dead. God remains dead. And we have killed him. How shall we comfort ourselves, the murderers of all murderers?

#### Martin Heidegger

Martin Heidegger understood Nietzsche's declaration "God is dead" as a commentary on the end of metaphysics. For Heidegger, Nietzsche's statement signifies not merely a theological or cultural shift but marks the culmination—and, consequently, the demise—of philosophy as metaphysics. Heidegger argued that metaphysics, which had structured Western thought from its inception, had now reached its maximum potential and, in doing so, had exhausted its relevance. The "death of God," then, is emblematic of this end, signaling the dissolution of any metaphysical worldview. According to Heidegger, metaphysics had been bound to end in this way since its origin.

Heidegger viewed the death of God as a pivotal moment in the history of thought, representing a transformation in humanity's relationship to "Being." This shift, he argued, invites a new mode of engagement with existence, one that transcends human-imposed structures of meaning and value. Unlike Nietzsche, who proposes the "will to power" as a means for individuals to assert their own values, Heidegger critiques this as a lingering form of human-centered valuation, one that still attempts to impose meaning onto existence.
answer:
니체의 "신은 죽었다(God is dead)"는 초자연적인 존재가 실제로 소멸했다는 신학적 선언이 아닙니다. 오히려 이 말은 서구 문명 전체의 근간이었던 기독교적 도덕성과 그에 기반한 형이상학적 세계관이 더 이상 유효하지 않게 되었음을 진단하는 비유적인 표현입니다. 니체에게 있어 '신(God)'은 수세기 동안 유럽에 도덕, 의미, 가치를 제공해 온 상징이었으며, 이 신의 죽음은 서구 사상의 근본적인 변화를 알리는 신호탄이었습니다.

이러한 '신은 죽음'은 단순히 종교적 변화를 넘어, 서구 사상의 핵심이었던 형이상학(metaphysics)의 종말을 의미합니다. 하이데거(Martin Heidegger)의 해석처럼, 이는 서구 사상이 구축해 온 모든 의미와 가치의 구조가 한계에 도달했음을 상징하며, 기존의 형이상학적 세계관이 해체되는 순간을 나타냅니다. 즉, 인간이 스스로 의미와 가치를 부여하려는 시도가 새로운 국면으로 접어들었음을 의미합니다.

결론적으로, 니체의 선언은 기존의 절대적이고 보편적인 가치 체계가 무너진 상태를 선언하며, 이는 인간 스스로가 새로운 가치를 창조해야 하는 역사적 전환점을 의미합니다. 니체는 이러한 변화 속에서 개인이 자신의 가치를 스스로 긍정하고 실현하는 '위버멘쉬(Übermensch)'의 등장을 암시합니다.

ground_truth_en: Zarathustra descends to teach humanity about the Übermensch — that man is something to be overcome, and to share his accumulated wisdom.
chunks:
## Synopsis
### First part

The book begins with a prologue that sets up many of the themes that will be explored throughout the work. Zarathustra is introduced as a hermit who has lived ten years on a mountain with his two companions, an eagle and a serpent. One morning – inspired by the sun, which he describes as happy only when it shines upon others – Zarathustra returns to his human community to share his wisdom. Upon descending the mountain, he encounters an old holy man living in the forest, who spends his days praising God. Zarathustra marvels that the man has not yet heard that "God is dead".

Arriving at the nearest town, Zarathustra addresses a crowd that has gathered to watch a tightrope walker. He tells them that mankind's goal must be to create something superior to itself – a new type of human, the Übermensch*. All men, he says, must be prepared to will their own destruction in order to bring the Übermensch* into being. The crowd greets this speech with scorn and mockery, and the tightrope show begins, high above the audience. When the rope-dancer is halfway across, a clown comes up behind him, urging him to get out of the way. The clown then leaps over the rope-dancer, causing the latter to lose balance and plummet to the ground. The crowd scatters, but Zarathustra stays with the fatally injured tightrope performer and implies that he admires him for having chosen such a dangerous livelihood. Zarathustra later takes the corpse of the rope-dancer on his shoulders into the forest and lays it in a hollow tree. He decides that from this point on, he will no longer attempt to speak to the masses, but only to a few chosen disciples.

There follows a series of discourses in which Zarathustra overturns many of the precepts of Christian morality. He gathers a group of disciples, but ultimately abandons them, saying that he will not return until they have disowned him.

Zarathushtra Spitama**, more commonly known as Zoroaster**, was an Iranian religious reformer who challenged the tenets of the contemporary Ancient Iranian religion, becoming the spiritual founder of Zoroastrianism. In the oldest Zoroastrian scriptures, the Gathas, which he is traditionally believed to have authored, he is described as a preacher and a poet-prophet. Some have claimed, with much scholarly controversy, to find his influence in Heraclitus, Plato, Pythagoras, and, perhaps less controversially, in the Abrahamic religions, including Judaism, Christianity, and Islam, particularly through concepts of cosmic dualism and personal morality.

He spoke an Old Iranian language, named Avestan by scholars after the corpus of Zoroastrian religious texts written in that language. Based on this, it is tentative to place his homeland somewhere in the eastern regions of the Greater Iran (possibly Bactra, now in modern-day Afghanistan). His life is traditionally dated to some time around the 7th and 6th centuries BC; though most scholars, using linguistic and socio-cultural evidence, suggest a dating even further back to the end of the 2nd and the beginning of the 1st millennium BC.

Zoroastrianism eventually became Greater Iran's most prominent religion from around the 6th century BC, enjoying official sanction during the time of the Sassanid Empire, until the 7th century AD, when the religion began to decline following the Arab-Muslim conquest of Iran. Zoroaster is credited with authorship of the Gathas as well as the Yasna Haptanghaiti*, a series of hymns composed in Old Avestan that cover core Zoroastrian beliefs. Little is known about Zoroaster; most of his life is known only from these scant texts. By modern standards of historiography, no evidence can place him into a fixed period and the historicization surrounding him may be a part of a trend from before the 10th century AD that historicizes legends and myths.

His name likely means "he who manages camels", though its etymology has multiple interpretations, and the Greek form "Zoroaster" stems from later transliterations. According to Zoroastrian tradition, Zoroaster was trained as a priest from a young age and, around age 30, experienced a divine revelation which introduced him to Ahura Mazda, the Wise Lord, and the dualism of truth (asha*) versus deception (druj*). He is said to have gained royal patronage under King Vishtaspa, spread his teachings, and founded a community, marrying three times and having six children. Zoroastrian texts portray his philosophy as emphasizing free will, ethical responsibility, and aligning with asha* through good thoughts, words, and deeds.

### Section 5

When Zarathustra had spoken these words, he again looked at the people,
and was silent. “There they stand,” said he to his heart; “there they
laugh: they understand me not; I am not the mouth for these ears.

Must one first batter their ears, that they may learn to hear with their
eyes? Must one clatter like kettledrums and penitential preachers? Or do
they only believe the stammerer?

They have something whereof they are proud. What do they call it, that
which maketh them proud? Culture, they call it; it distinguisheth them
from the goatherds.

They dislike, therefore, to hear of ‘contempt’ of themselves. So I will
appeal to their pride.

I will speak unto them of the most contemptible thing: that, however, is
THE LAST MAN!”

And thus spake Zarathustra unto the people:

It is time for man to fix his goal. It is time for man to plant the germ
of his highest hope.

Still is his soil rich enough for it. But that soil will one day be
poor and exhausted, and no lofty tree will any longer be able to grow
thereon.

Alas! there cometh the time when man will no longer launch the arrow of
his longing beyond man—and the string of his bow will have unlearned to
whizz!

I tell you: one must still have chaos in one, to give birth to a dancing
star. I tell you: ye have still chaos in you.

Alas! There cometh the time when man will no longer give birth to any
star. Alas! There cometh the time of the most despicable man, who can no
longer despise himself.

Lo! I show you THE LAST MAN.

“What is love? What is creation? What is longing? What is a star?”—so
asketh the last man and blinketh.

The earth hath then become small, and on it there hoppeth the last man
who maketh everything small. His species is ineradicable like that of
the ground-flea; the last man liveth longest.

“We have discovered happiness”—say the last men, and blink thereby.

They have left the regions where it is hard to live; for they need
warmth. One still loveth one’s neighbour and rubbeth against him; for
one needeth warmth.

Turning ill and being distrustful, they consider sinful: they walk
warily. He is a fool who still stumbleth over stones or men!

A little poison now and then: that maketh pleasant dreams. And much
poison at last for a pleasant death.

One still worketh, for work is a pastime. But one is careful lest the
pastime should hurt one.

One no longer becometh poor or rich; both are too burdensome. Who still
wanteth to rule? Who still wanteth to obey? Both are too burdensome.

No shepherd, and one herd! Every one wanteth the same; every one is
equal: he who hath other sentiments goeth voluntarily into the madhouse.

“Formerly all the world was insane,”—say the subtlest of them, and
blink thereby.

They are clever and know all that hath happened: so there is
no end to their raillery. People still fall out, but are soon
reconciled—otherwise it spoileth their stomachs.

They have their little pleasures for the day, and their little pleasures
for the night, but they have a regard for health.

“We have discovered happiness,”—say the last men, and blink thereby.—

And here ended the first discourse of Zarathustra, which is also
called “The Prologue”: for at this point the shouting and mirth of the
multitude interrupted him. “Give us this last man, O Zarathustra,”—they
called out—“make us into these last men! Then will we make thee a
present of the Superman!” And all the people exulted and smacked their
lips. Zarathustra, however, turned sad, and said to his heart:

“They understand me not: I am not the mouth for these ears.

Too long, perhaps, have I lived in the mountains; too much have I
hearkened unto the brooks and trees: now do I speak unto them as unto
the goatherds.

Calm is my soul, and clear, like the mountains in the morning. But they
think me cold, and a mocker with terrible jests.

And now do they look at me and laugh: and while they laugh they hate me
too. There is ice in their laughter.”
answer:
차라투스트라가 사람들에게 가르치고자 한 핵심은 인간이 스스로를 초월하는 존재, 즉 **위버멘쉬(Übermensch)**를 창조하는 것이라고 말하는 것입니다. 그는 인류의 목표는 자신보다 우월한 새로운 인간 유형을 만들어내는 것이어야 한다고 주장했습니다. 이를 위해서는 모든 인간이 자신의 파멸을 의지하여 위버멘쉬를 탄생시키는 데 동의해야 한다고 역설했습니다.

또한, 차라투스트라는 기존의 기독교적 도덕률을 뒤엎는 가르침을 제시하며, 인간이 스스로의 한계를 극복하고 새로운 가치를 창조하도록 촉구했습니다. 그는 사람들이 자신들의 자부심의 근원(문화)을 인식하고, 스스로를 경멸하는 것에서부터 시작하여 궁극적으로는 '가장 마지막 인간(THE LAST MAN)'의 상태에 도달해야 한다고 제시했습니다.

결론적으로, 그의 가르침은 현재의 안주와 평범함에 만족하는 대중을 비판하고, 끊임없이 자신을 극복하며 새로운 '별(star)'을 낳을 수 있는 혼돈(chaos)을 내면에 품어야 함을 강조하는 것입니다. 이는 기존의 가치관에 얽매이지 않고 스스로의 운명을 개척하는 주체적인 삶을 촉구하는 것입니다.

## 6. Reception and Influence

There has been considerable interest in German idealism in the last twenty years, as hostility waned in analytic philosophy, traditional assumptions faded in continental philosophy, and bridges were built between the two approaches. Philosophers like Richard Bernstein and Richard Rorty, inspired by Wilfrid Sellars, may be credited with re-introducing Hegel to analytic philosophy as an alternative to classical empiricism. Robert Pippin later defended a non-metaphysical Hegel, which has been a subject of intense debate, but which has also made Hegel relevant to contemporary debates about realism and anti-realism. More recently, Robert Brandom has championed the “normative” conception of rationality that he finds in Kant and Hegel, and which suggests that concepts function as rules regulating judgment rather than mere representations. Some, like Catherine Malabou, have even attempted to apply the insights of the German idealists to contemporary neuroscience. Finally, it would be remiss not to mention the extraordinary historical-philosophical scholarship, in both German and English, that has been produced on German idealism in recent years. The literature listed in the bibliography has not only enriched our understanding of German idealism with new editions, translations, and commentaries, it has also expanded the horizons of philosophical scholarship by identifying new problems and new solutions to problems arising in different traditions and contexts.

차라투스트라(Zarathustra)가 사람들에게 가르치고자 한 핵심은 인간이 스스로를 초월하는 존재, 즉 **위버멘쉬(Übermensch)**를 창조하는 것이라고 말합니다. 그는 인류의 목표가 자신보다 우월한 무언가를 만들어내는 것이어야 한다고 주장했습니다.

이러한 위버멘쉬를 만들기 위해서는 모든 인간이 자신의 파멸을 의지해야 한다고 역설했습니다. 이는 기존의 도덕적 가르침이나 기존의 질서에 얽매이지 않고, 스스로의 의지를 통해 새로운 가치를 창조하려는 의지를 강조하는 것입니다.

결론적으로 차라투스트라의 가르침은 기존의 도덕관을 뒤엎고, 개인의 자유 의지와 윤리적 책임을 통해 스스로의 운명을 개척하며 '선한 생각, 말, 행동'을 통해 진리(asha*)에 부합하도록 살아가도록 촉구하는 것이었습니다.

ground_truth_en: Zarathustra descends to teach humanity about the Übermensch — that man is something to be overcome, and to share his accumulated wisdom.
chunks:
## Synopsis
### First part

The book begins with a prologue that sets up many of the themes that will be explored throughout the work. Zarathustra is introduced as a hermit who has lived ten years on a mountain with his two companions, an eagle and a serpent. One morning – inspired by the sun, which he describes as happy only when it shines upon others – Zarathustra returns to his human community to share his wisdom. Upon descending the mountain, he encounters an old holy man living in the forest, who spends his days praising God. Zarathustra marvels that the man has not yet heard that "God is dead".

Arriving at the nearest town, Zarathustra addresses a crowd that has gathered to watch a tightrope walker. He tells them that mankind's goal must be to create something superior to itself – a new type of human, the Übermensch*. All men, he says, must be prepared to will their own destruction in order to bring the Übermensch* into being. The crowd greets this speech with scorn and mockery, and the tightrope show begins, high above the audience. When the rope-dancer is halfway across, a clown comes up behind him, urging him to get out of the way. The clown then leaps over the rope-dancer, causing the latter to lose balance and plummet to the ground. The crowd scatters, but Zarathustra stays with the fatally injured tightrope performer and implies that he admires him for having chosen such a dangerous livelihood. Zarathustra later takes the corpse of the rope-dancer on his shoulders into the forest and lays it in a hollow tree. He decides that from this point on, he will no longer attempt to speak to the masses, but only to a few chosen disciples.

There follows a series of discourses in which Zarathustra overturns many of the precepts of Christian morality. He gathers a group of disciples, but ultimately abandons them, saying that he will not return until they have disowned him.

### Section 3

When Zarathustra arrived at the nearest town which adjoineth the forest,
he found many people assembled in the market-place; for it had been
announced that a rope-dancer would give a performance. And Zarathustra
spake thus unto the people:

I TEACH YOU THE SUPERMAN. Man is something that is to be surpassed. What
have ye done to surpass man?

All beings hitherto have created something beyond themselves: and ye
want to be the ebb of that great tide, and would rather go back to the
beast than surpass man?

What is the ape to man? A laughing-stock, a thing of shame. And just the
same shall man be to the Superman: a laughing-stock, a thing of shame.

Ye have made your way from the worm to man, and much within you is still
worm. Once were ye apes, and even yet man is more of an ape than any of
the apes.

Even the wisest among you is only a disharmony and hybrid of plant and
phantom. But do I bid you become phantoms or plants?

Lo, I teach you the Superman!

The Superman is the meaning of the earth. Let your will say: The
Superman SHALL BE the meaning of the earth!

I conjure you, my brethren, REMAIN TRUE TO THE EARTH, and believe not
those who speak unto you of superearthly hopes! Poisoners are they,
whether they know it or not.

Despisers of life are they, decaying ones and poisoned ones themselves,
of whom the earth is weary: so away with them!

Once blasphemy against God was the greatest blasphemy; but God died,
and therewith also those blasphemers. To blaspheme the earth is now the
dreadfulest sin, and to rate the heart of the unknowable higher than the
meaning of the earth!

Once the soul looked contemptuously on the body, and then that contempt
was the supreme thing:—the soul wished the body meagre, ghastly, and
famished. Thus it thought to escape from the body and the earth.

Oh, that soul was itself meagre, ghastly, and famished; and cruelty was
the delight of that soul!

But ye, also, my brethren, tell me: What doth your body say about
your soul? Is your soul not poverty and pollution and wretched
self-complacency?

Verily, a polluted stream is man. One must be a sea, to receive a
polluted stream without becoming impure.

Lo, I teach you the Superman: he is that sea; in him can your great
contempt be submerged.

What is the greatest thing ye can experience? It is the hour of great
contempt. The hour in which even your happiness becometh loathsome unto
you, and so also your reason and virtue.

The hour when ye say: “What good is my happiness! It is poverty and
pollution and wretched self-complacency. But my happiness should justify
existence itself!”

The hour when ye say: “What good is my reason! Doth it long for
knowledge as the lion for his food? It is poverty and pollution and
wretched self-complacency!”

The hour when ye say: “What good is my virtue! As yet it hath not made
me passionate. How weary I am of my good and my bad! It is all poverty
and pollution and wretched self-complacency!”

The hour when ye say: “What good is my justice! I do not see that I am
fervour and fuel. The just, however, are fervour and fuel!”

The hour when ye say: “What good is my pity! Is not pity the cross on
which he is nailed who loveth man? But my pity is not a crucifixion.”

Have ye ever spoken thus? Have ye ever cried thus? Ah! would that I had
heard you crying thus!

It is not your sin—it is your self-satisfaction that crieth unto
heaven; your very sparingness in sin crieth unto heaven!

Where is the lightning to lick you with its tongue? Where is the frenzy
with which ye should be inoculated?

Lo, I teach you the Superman: he is that lightning, he is that frenzy!—

When Zarathustra had thus spoken, one of the people called out: “We have
now heard enough of the rope-dancer; it is time now for us to see him!”
 And all the people laughed at Zarathustra. But the rope-dancer, who
thought the words applied to him, began his performance.

## Synopsis
### Second part

Zarathustra retires to his mountain cave, and several years pass by. One night, he dreams that he looks into a mirror and sees the face of a devil instead of his own; he takes this as a sign that his doctrines are being distorted by his enemies, and joyfully descends the mountain to recover his lost disciples.

More discourses follow, which continue to develop the themes of the death of God and the rise of the Übermensch*, and also introduce the concept of the will to power. There are hints, however, that Zarathustra is holding something back. A series of dreams and visions prompt him to reveal this secret teaching, but he cannot bring himself to do so. He withdraws from his disciples once more, in order to perfect himself.
answer:
차라투스트라가 사람들에게 가르치려는 핵심은 '위버멘쉬(Übermensch)'를 창조하는 것입니다. 그는 인류가 스스로를 능가하는 더 우월한 존재, 즉 새로운 유형의 인간을 만들어야 한다고 주장합니다. 이를 위해 인간은 자신의 파괴를 의지하여 위버멘쉬를 탄생시키는 데 동참해야 한다고 말합니다.

그는 기존의 기독교적 도덕률을 뒤엎으며, 인간이 스스로의 나약함과 자기만족에 빠져 있는 현실을 비판합니다. 차라투스트라는 인간이 겪는 '위대한 경멸의 시간(hour of great contempt)'을 지적하며, 진정한 초인(Superman)은 이러한 자기만족과 나약함을 극복하고 '번개(lightning)'와 '광란(frenzy)'과 같은 강렬한 의지를 가진 존재라고 가르칩니다.

결론적으로, 차라투스트라가 가르치려는 것은 단순히 새로운 사상을 제시하는 것이 아니라, 인간이 자신의 한계를 극복하고 '땅의 의미(meaning of the earth)'가 될 수 있는 강력한 의지, 즉 위버멘쉬가 되도록 이끄는 것입니다.

## Appearance in Thus Spoke Zarathustra*

The last man's first appearance is in "Zarathustra's Prologue". According to Nietzsche, the last man is the goal that modern society and Western civilization have apparently set for themselves. After having unsuccessfully attempted to get the populace to accept the Übermensch* as the goal of society, Zarathustra confronts them with a goal so disgusting that he assumes that it will revolt them – a culture which seeks only passive comfort and routine, avoiding everything that could potentially bring risk, pain, or disappointment. Zarathustra fails in this attempt, and instead of repelling and manipulating the populace into pursuing the goal of the Übermensch, the populace take Zarathustra literally and choose the "disgusting" goal of becoming the last men. This decision leaves Zarathustra disheartened and disappointed.
answer:
차라투스트라가 사람들에게 가르치려는 핵심은 '위버멘쉬(Übermensch)'를 창조하는 것입니다. 그는 인류의 목표는 스스로를 능가하는 새로운 유형의 인간, 즉 위버멘쉬를 만들어내는 것이라고 주장합니다. 이를 위해 인간들은 자신을 극복하고 더 높은 경지로 나아가야 하며, 그 과정에서 자신의 파괴를 의지하여 위버멘쉬를 탄생시켜야 한다고 역설합니다.

더 나아가 차라투스트라는 기존의 기독교적 도덕률을 뒤엎으며, 인간이 겪는 자기만족과 안주에 대한 비판을 제기합니다. 그는 현재의 인간들이 느끼는 행복, 이성, 덕, 정의, 연민 등이 모두 '가난, 오염, 비참한 자기만족(poverty and pollution and wretched self-complacency)'이라는 상태에 머물러 있음을 지적합니다.

결국 차라투스트라가 제시하는 위버멘쉬는 이러한 자기만족에 갇힌 상태에서 벗어나, 삶의 모든 고통과 모순을 긍정하고 '번개(lightning)'와 같은 격렬한 열정(frenzy)을 통해 자신의 존재를 정당화하는 존재, 즉 '바다(sea)'와 같은 포용력을 지닌 존재가 되어야 함을 가르치려 합니다.

When Zarathustra had spoken these words, he again looked at the people,
and was silent. “There they stand,” said he to his heart; “there they
laugh: they understand me not; I am not the mouth for these ears.

Must one first batter their ears, that they may learn to hear with their
eyes? Must one clatter like kettledrums and penitential preachers? Or do
they only believe the stammerer?

They have something whereof they are proud. What do they call it, that
which maketh them proud? Culture, they call it; it distinguisheth them
from the goatherds.

They dislike, therefore, to hear of ‘contempt’ of themselves. So I will
appeal to their pride.

I will speak unto them of the most contemptible thing: that, however, is
THE LAST MAN!”

And thus spake Zarathustra unto the people:

It is time for man to fix his goal. It is time for man to plant the germ
of his highest hope.

Still is his soil rich enough for it.

## Notable influence on modern Western culture


A sculpture of Zoroaster by Edward Clark Potter, representing ancient Persian judicial wisdom and dating to 1896, towers over the Appellate Division Courthouse of New York State at East 25th Street and Madison Avenue in Manhattan. A sculpture of Zoroaster is included among other prominent religious figures in a procession representing major faith traditions on the south side of Rockefeller Memorial Chapel at the University of Chicago. It features figures from Abraham to the Reformation, illustrating a historical continuum of religious thought that includes the likes of Zoroaster, Moses, Plato and others.

차라투스트라가 사람들에게 가르치려는 핵심은 인류가 스스로를 뛰어넘는 더 우월한 존재를 창조해야 한다는 것입니다. 그는 인간의 목표는 자신보다 더 뛰어난 새로운 유형의 인간, 즉 **위버멘쉬(Übermensch)**를 만들어내는 것이어야 한다고 주장합니다.

이러한 위버멘쉬를 탄생시키기 위해서는 모든 인간이 자신의 파멸을 의지하여 그 위버멘쉬를 낳을 준비가 되어 있어야 한다고 말합니다. 하지만 그는 대중이 자신의 말을 이해하지 못하고 조롱할 때, 그들의 자부심을 이용하여 가장 경멸스러운 진실, 즉 "마지막 인간(THE LAST MAN)"에 대해 이야기함으로써 그들의 목표를 설정하도록 촉구합니다.

결론적으로, 차라투스트라는 인간이 자신의 가장 높은 희망의 씨앗을 심고 목표를 정해야 할 때가 왔으며, 그 땅이 그것을 위한 충분한 토양이 되어야 한다고 역설하며 인간의 자기 극복과 초월을 촉구하고 있습니다.

ground_truth_en: Zarathustra descends to teach humanity about the Übermensch — that man is something to be overcome, and to share his accumulated wisdom.

### Section 3

When Zarathustra arrived at the nearest town which adjoineth the forest,
he found many people assembled in the market-place; for it had been
announced that a rope-dancer would give a performance. And Zarathustra
spake thus unto the people:

I TEACH YOU THE SUPERMAN. Man is something that is to be surpassed. What
have ye done to surpass man?

All beings hitherto have created something beyond themselves: and ye
want to be the ebb of that great tide, and would rather go back to the
beast than surpass man?

What is the ape to man? A laughing-stock, a thing of shame. And just the
same shall man be to the Superman: a laughing-stock, a thing of shame.

Ye have made your way from the worm to man, and much within you is still
worm. Once were ye apes, and even yet man is more of an ape than any of
the apes.

Even the wisest among you is only a disharmony and hybrid of plant and
phantom. But do I bid you become phantoms or plants?

Lo, I teach you the Superman!

### First part

The book begins with a prologue that sets up many of the themes that will be explored throughout the work. Zarathustra is introduced as a hermit who has lived ten years on a mountain with his two companions, an eagle and a serpent. One morning – inspired by the sun, which he describes as happy only when it shines upon others – Zarathustra returns to his human community to share his wisdom. Upon descending the mountain, he encounters an old holy man living in the forest, who spends his days praising God. Zarathustra marvels that the man has not yet heard that "God is dead".

Arriving at the nearest town, Zarathustra addresses a crowd that has gathered to watch a tightrope walker. He tells them that mankind's goal must be to create something superior to itself – a new type of human, the Übermensch*. All men, he says, must be prepared to will their own destruction in order to bring the Übermensch* into being. The crowd greets this speech with scorn and mockery, and the tightrope show begins, high above the audience.

And when one giveth the blind man eyes,
then doth he see too many bad things on the earth: so that he curseth
him who healed him. He, however, who maketh the lame man run, inflicteth
upon him the greatest injury; for hardly can he run, when his vices
run away with him—so do the people teach concerning cripples. And why
should not Zarathustra also learn from the people, when the people learn
from Zarathustra?

It is, however, the smallest thing unto me since I have been amongst
men, to see one person lacking an eye, another an ear, and a third a
leg, and that others have lost the tongue, or the nose, or the head.

I see and have seen worse things, and divers things so hideous, that I
should neither like to speak of all matters, nor even keep silent about
some of them: namely, men who lack everything, except that they have
too much of one thing—men who are nothing more than a big eye, or a big
mouth, or a big belly, or something else big,—reversed cripples, I call
such men.
answer:
차라투스트라가 사람들에게 가르치려는 핵심은 '위버멘쉬(Übermensch)'를 창조하는 것입니다. 그는 인간이 스스로를 뛰어넘는 존재, 즉 더 우월한 새로운 유형의 인간을 만들어야 한다고 주장합니다. 이는 인류가 현재의 자신을 넘어서는 것을 목표로 삼아야 하며, 이를 위해 스스로의 파괴를 감수하고 위버멘쉬를 탄생시켜야 한다고 말합니다.

또한, 그는 인간이 현재의 상태에 머무르거나 짐승과 같은 본성에 갇혀 있는 것을 비판합니다. 그는 인간이 벌레(worm)에서 벗어나야 하며, 현재의 인간조차도 짐승보다 더 짐승과 같은 면이 남아있다고 지적합니다. 따라서 차라투스트라는 사람들에게 자신을 극복하고 더 높은 경지로 나아가도록 촉구하며, 스스로를 초월하는 존재가 되라고 가르치려 합니다.

chunks:
### Section 25

He who hath grown wise concerning old origins, lo, he will at last seek
after the fountains of the future and new origins.—

O my brethren, not long will it be until NEW PEOPLES shall arise and new
fountains shall rush down into new depths.

For the earthquake—it choketh up many wells, it causeth much
languishing: but it bringeth also to light inner powers and secrets.

The earthquake discloseth new fountains. In the earthquake of old
peoples new fountains burst forth.

And whoever calleth out: “Lo, here is a well for many thirsty ones, one
heart for many longing ones, one will for many instruments”:—around him
collecteth a PEOPLE, that is to say, many attempting ones.

Who can command, who must obey—THAT IS THERE ATTEMPTED! Ah, with what
long seeking and solving and failing and learning and re-attempting!

Human society: it is an attempt—so I teach—a long seeking: it seeketh
however the ruler!—

—An attempt, my brethren! And NO “contract”!

And ye also asked yourselves often: “Who is Zarathustra to us? What
shall he be called by us?” And like me, did ye give yourselves questions
for answers.

Is he a promiser? Or a fulfiller? A conqueror? Or an inheritor? A
harvest? Or a ploughshare? A physician? Or a healed one?

Is he a poet? Or a genuine one? An emancipator? Or a subjugator? A good
one? Or an evil one?

I walk amongst men as the fragments of the future: that future which I
contemplate.

And it is all my poetisation and aspiration to compose and collect into
unity what is fragment and riddle and fearful chance.

And how could I endure to be a man, if man were not also the composer,
and riddle-reader, and redeemer of chance!

To redeem what is past, and to transform every “It was” into “Thus would
I have it!”—that only do I call redemption!

Will—so is the emancipator and joy-bringer called: thus have I taught
you, my friends!

answer:
차라투스트라가 사람들에게 가르치려는 핵심은 '슈퍼맨(Superman)'입니다. 그는 인간이 스스로를 뛰어넘어야 할 존재이며, 지금까지 모든 존재들이 자신을 넘어서는 무언가를 창조해 왔음을 지적합니다.

차라투스트라는 사람들이 그 위대한 흐름(great tide)의 물결이 되어야 하며, 짐승으로 돌아가는 대신 인간을 초월해야 한다고 주장합니다. 그는 인간이 여전히 벌레(worm)의 속성을 가지고 있음을 지적하며, 인간이  apes(유인원)보다 더 원시적인 상태에 있음을 상기시킵니다.

궁극적으로 차라투스트라가 가르치는 것은 인간이 스스로를 극복하고 새로운 차원의 존재로 나아가도록 이끄는 것입니다. 그는 "슈퍼맨"을 통해 인간이 자신의 한계를 넘어서고 새로운 가치를 창조하는 존재가 되기를 촉구합니다.

## Notable influence on modern Western culture


A sculpture of Zoroaster by Edward Clark Potter, representing ancient Persian judicial wisdom and dating to 1896, towers over the Appellate Division Courthouse of New York State at East 25th Street and Madison Avenue in Manhattan. A sculpture of Zoroaster is included among other prominent religious figures in a procession representing major faith traditions on the south side of Rockefeller Memorial Chapel at the University of Chicago. It features figures from Abraham to the Reformation, illustrating a historical continuum of religious thought that includes the likes of Zoroaster, Moses, Plato and others.

그는 사회를 '시도(attempt)'이자 '긴 탐색(long seeking)'으로 보며, 사회가 권력자를 추구하는 과정일 뿐이며 어떤 '계약(contract)'도 아니라고 주장합니다. 또한, 차라투스트라는 과거의 것을 구원하고 모든 '과거(It was)'를 "내가 그렇게 하리라(Thus would I have it!)"로 변모시키는 것을 '구원(redemption)'이라고 정의합니다.

### Section 3

When Zarathustra arrived at the nearest town which adjoineth the forest,
he found many people assembled in the market-place; for it had been

spake thus unto the people:

I TEACH YOU THE SUPERMAN. Man is something that is to be surpassed. What
have ye done to surpass man?

All beings hitherto have created something beyond themselves: and ye
want to be the ebb of that great tide, and would rather go back to the
beast than surpass man?

What is the ape to man? A laughing-stock, a thing of shame. And just the
same shall man be to the Superman: a laughing-stock, a thing of shame.

Ye have made your way from the worm to man, and much within you is still
worm. Once were ye apes, and even yet man is more of an ape than any of
the apes.

Even the wisest among you is only a disharmony and hybrid of plant and
phantom. But do I bid you become phantoms or plants?

Lo, I teach you the Superman!

### Section 5

When Zarathustra had spoken these words, he again looked at the people,
and was silent. “There they stand,” said he to his heart; “there they
laugh: they understand me not; I am not the mouth for these ears.

Must one first batter their ears, that they may learn to hear with their
eyes? Must one clatter like kettledrums and penitential preachers? Or do
they only believe the stammerer?

They have something whereof they are proud. What do they call it, that
which maketh them proud? Culture, they call it; it distinguisheth them
from the goatherds.

They dislike, therefore, to hear of ‘contempt’ of themselves. So I will
appeal to their pride.

I will speak unto them of the most contemptible thing: that, however, is
THE LAST MAN!”

And thus spake Zarathustra unto the people:

It is time for man to fix his goal. It is time for man to plant the germ
of his highest hope.

Still is his soil rich enough for it.

## XLII. Redemption

When Zarathustra went one day over the great bridge, then did the
cripples and beggars surround him, and a hunchback spake thus unto him:

“Behold, Zarathustra! Even the people learn from thee, and acquire faith
in thy teaching: but for them to believe fully in thee, one thing is
still needful—thou must first of all convince us cripples! Here hast
thou now a fine selection, and verily, an opportunity with more than one
forelock! The blind canst thou heal, and make the lame run; and from
him who hath too much behind, couldst thou well, also, take away a
little;—that, I think, would be the right method to make the cripples
believe in Zarathustra!”

Zarathustra, however, answered thus unto him who so spake: When one
taketh his hump from the hunchback, then doth one take from him his
spirit—so do the people teach.
answer:
차라투스트라가 사람들에게 가르치려는 핵심은 '위버멘쉬(Übermensch)'를 제시하는 것입니다. 그는 인간이 현재의 상태에 머무르지 않고 자신을 뛰어넘어야 한다고 주장하며, 인간이 스스로를 초월하는 존재가 되어야 함을 역설합니다. 이는 인간이 기존의 한계를 극복하고 더 높은 경지로 나아가도록 촉구하는 메시지입니다.

그는 인간이 스스로를 뛰어넘는 존재, 즉 '슈퍼맨(Superman)'이 되라고 가르칩니다. 이 과정에서 그는 현재의 인간이 겪고 있는 자기 자신에 대한 인식, 즉 '원시적인 존재(worm)'의 잔재를 극복하고 진정한 초월을 이루어야 함을 강조합니다.

결론적으로 차라투스트라의 가르침은 사람들이 자신들의 현재 상태에 만족하지 말고, 가장 높은 희망의 씨앗을 심어 스스로의 잠재력을 실현하여 위버멘쉬로 거듭나도록 이끄는 것입니다.

of his highest hope.

Still is his soil rich enough for it.

## XLII. Redemption

When Zarathustra went one day over the great bridge, then did the
cripples and beggars surround him, and a hunchback spake thus unto him:

“Behold, Zarathustra! Even the people learn from thee, and acquire faith
in thy teaching: but for them to believe fully in thee, one thing is
still needful—thou must first of all convince us cripples! Here hast
thou now a fine selection, and verily, an opportunity with more than one
forelock! The blind canst thou heal, and make the lame run; and from
him who hath too much behind, couldst thou well, also, take away a
little;—that, I think, would be the right method to make the cripples
believe in Zarathustra!”

Zarathustra, however, answered thus unto him who so spake: When one
taketh his hump from the hunchback, then doth one take from him his
spirit—so do the people teach.
answer:
차라투스트라가 사람들에게 가르치려는 핵심은 '위버멘쉬(Übermensch)'를 제시하는 것입니다. 그는 인간은 극복되어야 할 존재이며, 지금까지 모든 존재들이 자신을 넘어서는 무언가를 창조해 왔음을 지적합니다. 사람들은 이 거대한 흐름(great tide)의 물결이 되기를 원하며, 인간을 넘어서는 것을 두려워하여 짐승으로 돌아가기를 원한다고 비판합니다.

차라투스트라는 인간이 겪고 있는 현 상태, 즉 짐승과 인간 사이의 혼란을 지적하며, 인간 내부에 여전히 '벌레(worm)'와 같은 원초적인 부분이 남아있음을 상기시킵니다. 그는 현 상태의 지혜로운 자들조차도 식물과 환영의 불협화음(disharmony and hybrid of plant and phantom)에 불과하다고 말하며, 그들에게 맹목적인 식물이나 환영이 되라고 요구하지 않습니다.

결론적으로, 차라투스트라의 가르침은 사람들에게 자신들의 목표를 명확히 설정하고, 가장 높은 희망의 씨앗을 심도록 촉구하는 것입니다. 그는 '최후의 인간(THE LAST MAN)'이라는 가장 경멸받을 만한 것을 언급함으로써, 사람들이 스스로의 자부심(culture)을 내려놓고 자신들의 잠재력을 극복하고 새로운 차원의 존재로 나아가도록 이끌고자 합니다.

ground_truth_en: Humanity is a bridge or rope stretched between beast and Übermensch; the current human is something to be surpassed, not an end in itself.
chunks:
## Philosophy
### Übermensch

Another concept important to understanding Nietzsche is the Übermensch* (Superman). Writing about nihilism in Also Sprach Zarathustra*, Nietzsche introduced an Übermensch*. According to Laurence Lampert, "the death of God must be followed by a long twilight of piety and nihilism (II. 19; III. 8). Zarathustra's gift of the overman is given to mankind not aware of the problem to which the overman is the solution." Zarathustra presents the Übermensch* as the creator of new values, and he appears as a solution to the problem of the death of God and nihilism. The Übermensch* does not follow the morality of common people since that favours mediocrity but rises above the notion of good and evil and above the "herd". In this way Zarathustra proclaims his ultimate goal as the journey towards the state of the Übermensch*. He wants a kind of spiritual evolution of self-awareness and overcoming of traditional views on morality and justice that stem from the superstitious beliefs still deeply rooted or related to the notion of God and Christianity.

From Thus Spoke Zarathustra* (Zarathustra's Prologue; pp. 9–11):

*I teach you the Übermensch*. Man is something that shall be overcome. What have you done to overcome him? All beings so far have created something beyond themselves: and you want to be the ebb of that great tide, and would rather go back to the beast than overcome man? What is the ape to man? A laughing-stock or a painful embarrassment. And just the same shall man be to the Übermensch: a laughing-stock or a painful embarrassment. You have made your way from worm to man, and much within you is still worm. Once you were apes, and even yet man is more of an ape than any ape. Even the wisest among you is only a conflict and hybrid of plant and ghost. But do I bid you become ghosts or plants? Behold, I teach you the Übermensch! The Übermensch is the meaning of the earth. Let your will say: The Übermensch *shall be* the meaning of the earth... Man is a rope stretched between the animal and the Übermensch—a rope over an abyss... What is great in man is that he is a bridge and not a goal: what is lovable in man is that he is an *over-going* and a *going under*.

Zarathustra contrasts the Übermensch* with the last man of egalitarian modernity (the most obvious example being democracy), an alternative goal humanity might set for itself. The last man is possible only by mankind's having bred an apathetic creature who has no great passion or commitment, who is unable to dream, who merely earns his living and keeps warm. This concept appears only in Thus Spoke Zarathustra*, and is presented as a condition that would render the creation of the Übermensch* impossible.[*better source needed*]

ÜbermenschConcept in the philosophy of Friedrich Nietzsche The Übermensch*** (/ˈuːbərmɛnʃ/ OO-bər-mensh*, German: [ˈyːbɐmɛnʃ] ; literal translation"}]],"parts":[{"template":{"target":{"wt":"lit","href":"./Template:Lit"},"params":{"1":{"wt":"Superman"},"2":{"wt":"Beyond-Man"}},"i":0}}]}' id="mwEQ">lit. 'Superman' or 'Beyond-Man') is a concept in the philosophy of Friedrich Nietzsche. In his 1883 book, Thus Spoke Zarathustra* (German: Also sprach Zarathustra*), Nietzsche has his character Zarathustra posit the Übermensch* as a goal for humanity to set for itself. The Übermensch* represents a shift from otherworldly Christian values and manifests the grounded human ideal. Zarathustra proclaims the will of the Übermensch* to give meaning to life on planet Earth, and admonishes his audience to ignore those who promise other-worldly fulfillment. In 1909, Thomas Common translated the term to English as "Superman", following the terminology of George Bernard Shaw's 1903 stage play Man and Superman*. Walter Kaufmann blamed this translation for the widespread misidentification of Nietzsche's concept with the comic-book character Superman. Kaufmann and others preferred to translate Übermensch* as "overman". A translation like "superior humans" might better fit the concept of Nietzsche as he unfolds his narrative. Scholars continue to employ both terms, with some simply opting for the German word.

## As a goal

Zarathustra first announces the Übermensch* as a goal humanity can set for itself. All human life would be given meaning by how it advanced a new generation of human beings. The aspiration of a woman would be to give birth to an Übermensch*, for example; her relationships with men would be judged by this standard.

Zarathustra contrasts the Übermensch* with the degenerate "last man" of egalitarian modernity, an alternative goal which humanity might set for itself. "Last man" appears only in Thus Spoke Zarathustra*, and is presented as a smothering of aspiration antithetical to the spirit of the Übermensch*.

According to Rüdiger Safranski, some commentators associate the Übermensch* with a program of eugenics.
answer:
위버멘쉬(Übermensch)와 인간의 관계는 니체가 제시하는 인간 존재의 극복과 새로운 가치 창조라는 목표를 통해 설명됩니다. 니체는 인간을 극복해야 할 대상으로 보았으며, 위버멘쉬는 바로 이 인간을 넘어서는 존재, 즉 '초인(Übermensch)'을 의미합니다.

자라투스트라는 인간이 겪는 존재론적 한계를 지적하며, 인간은 동물과 위버멘쉬 사이의 밧줄과 같다고 비유합니다. 여기서 인간은 목표 그 자체가 아니라, 동물과 위버멘쉬 사이의 '다리(bridge)'이며 '넘어감(over-going)'과 '아래로 감(going under)'을 경험하는 존재로 묘사됩니다. 즉, 인간은 위버멘쉬로 나아가기 위한 중간 단계이자 역동적인 과정 속에 놓여 있습니다.

결론적으로, 위버멘쉬는 기존의 도덕이나 종교적 가치관에 얽매이지 않고 스스로 새로운 가치를 창조하며 삶에 의미를 부여하는 존재입니다. 반면, '마지막 인간(last man)'은 열등한 욕망과 무기력함에 빠져 있어 위버멘쉬의 등장을 불가능하게 만드는 상태로 대비됩니다. 따라서 인간의 역할은 위버멘쉬라는 궁극적인 목표를 향해 스스로를 극복하고 정신적으로 진화시키는 여정 그 자체라고 할 수 있습니다.

## 6. Reception and Influence

There has been considerable interest in German idealism in the last twenty years, as hostility waned in analytic philosophy, traditional assumptions faded in continental philosophy, and bridges were built between the two approaches. Philosophers like Richard Bernstein and Richard Rorty, inspired by Wilfrid Sellars, may be credited with re-introducing Hegel to analytic philosophy as an alternative to classical empiricism. Robert Pippin later defended a non-metaphysical Hegel, which has been a subject of intense debate, but which has also made Hegel relevant to contemporary debates about realism and anti-realism. More recently, Robert Brandom has championed the “normative” conception of rationality that he finds in Kant and Hegel, and which suggests that concepts function as rules regulating judgment rather than mere representations. Some, like Catherine Malabou, have even attempted to apply the insights of the German idealists to contemporary neuroscience. Finally, it would be remiss not to mention the extraordinary historical-philosophical scholarship, in both German and English, that has been produced on German idealism in recent years. The literature listed in the bibliography has not only enriched our understanding of German idealism with new editions, translations, and commentaries, it has also expanded the horizons of philosophical scholarship by identifying new problems and new solutions to problems arising in different traditions and contexts.

Kaufmann and others preferred to translate Übermensch* as "overman". A translation like "superior humans" might better fit the concept of Nietzsche as he unfolds his narrative. Scholars continue to employ both terms, with some simply opting for the German word.
answer:
위버멘쉬(Übermensch)와 인간의 관계는 니체의 철학에서 매우 역동적이고 극복을 지향하는 관계로 설명됩니다. 니체는 인간을 극복해야 할 대상으로 보았으며, 위버멘쉬는 바로 그 극복의 목표이자 해답으로 제시됩니다. 『차라투스트라는 이렇게 말했다』에서 자라투스트라는 인간이 자신을 극복해야 한다고 선언하며, 인간은 동물과 위버멘쉬 사이의 밧줄과 같다고 비유합니다.

이 관계에서 인간은 '동물(animal)'과 '위버멘쉬(Übermensch)' 사이의 밧줄에 놓인 존재로 묘사됩니다. 이는 인간이 현재의 상태에 머물러 있는 것이 아니라, 더 높은 경지로 나아가야 함을 시사합니다. 위버멘쉬는 기존의 도덕이나 신앙에서 비롯된 전통적인 가치관을 넘어 새로운 가치를 창조하는 존재이며, '대지의 의미(the meaning of the earth)'가 되는 것을 지향합니다.

결론적으로, 인간은 위버멘쉬라는 궁극적인 목표를 향해 나아가는 '다리(a bridge)'와 같으며, 위버멘쉬는 인간의 한계를 넘어서는 존재로서, 인간이 스스로의 의지를 통해 정신적 진화를 이루고 전통적인 도덕관을 초월하도록 이끄는 존재라고 할 수 있습니다.

## As a goal

Zarathustra first announces the Übermensch* as a goal humanity can set for itself. All human life would be given meaning by how it advanced a new generation of human beings. The aspiration of a woman would be to give birth to an Übermensch*, for example; her relationships with men would be judged by this standard.

Zarathustra contrasts the Übermensch* with the degenerate "last man" of egalitarian modernity, an alternative goal which humanity might set for itself. "Last man" appears only in Thus Spoke Zarathustra*, and is presented as a smothering of aspiration antithetical to the spirit of the Übermensch*.

According to Rüdiger Safranski, some commentators associate the Übermensch* with a program of eugenics.

ÜbermenschConcept in the philosophy of Friedrich Nietzsche The Übermensch*** (/ˈuːbərmɛnʃ/ OO-bər-mensh*, German: [ˈyːbɐmɛnʃ] ; literal translation"}]],"parts":[{"template":{"target":{"wt":"lit","href":"./Template:Lit"},"params":{"1":{"wt":"Superman"},"2":{"wt":"Beyond-Man"}},"i":0}}]}' id="mwEQ">lit. 'Superman' or 'Beyond-Man') is a concept in the philosophy of Friedrich Nietzsche. In his 1883 book, Thus Spoke Zarathustra* (German: Also sprach Zarathustra*), Nietzsche has his character Zarathustra posit the Übermensch* as a goal for humanity to set for itself. The Übermensch* represents a shift from otherworldly Christian values and manifests the grounded human ideal. Zarathustra proclaims the will of the Übermensch* to give meaning to life on planet Earth, and admonishes his audience to ignore those who promise other-worldly fulfillment. In 1909, Thomas Common translated the term to English as "Superman", following the terminology of George Bernard Shaw's 1903 stage play Man and Superman*. Walter Kaufmann blamed this translation for the widespread misidentification of Nietzsche's concept with the comic-book character Superman. Kaufmann and others preferred to translate Übermensch* as "overman". A translation like "superior humans" might better fit the concept of Nietzsche as he unfolds his narrative. Scholars continue to employ both terms, with some simply opting for the German word.

## 5. The Human Exemplar

Whatever else may be said about the *Übermensch, *Nietzsche clearly had in mind an exemplary figure and an exception among humans, one “whose inexhaustible fertility and power keep up the faith in man.” For some commentators, Nietzsche’s distinction between overman and the last man has political ramifications. The hope for an overman figure to appear would seem to be permissible for one individual, many, or even a social ideal, depending on the culture within which it appears. Modernity, in Nietzsche’s view, is in such a state of decadence that it would be fortunate, indeed, to see the emergence of even one such type, given that modern sociopolitical arrangements are more conducive to creating the egalitarian “last man” who “blinks” at expectations for rank, self-overcoming, and striving for greatness. The last men are “ the most harmful to the species because they preserve their existence as much at the expense of the truth as at the expense of the future” (“Why I am a Destiny” in *Ecce Homo *1). Although Nietzsche never lays out a precise political program from these ideas, it is at least clear that theoretical justifications for complacency or passivity are antithetical to his philosophy. What, then, may be said about Nietzsche as political thinker? Nietzsche’s political sympathies are definitely not democratic in any ordinary way of thinking about that sort of arrangement. Nor are they socialist or Marxist.
answer:
니체의 철학에서 **위버멘쉬(Übermensch)**는 인류가 스스로 설정할 수 있는 궁극적인 목표이자 이상적인 인간상으로 제시됩니다. 위버멘쉬는 초월적인 기독교적 가치관에서 벗어나 현실 세계에서 삶에 의미를 부여하는 인간의 이상을 상징합니다. 즉, 위버멘쉬는 삶의 의미를 창조하고 새로운 인간 세대를 만들어냄으로써 인류의 발전을 이끌어가는 존재로 그려집니다.

위버멘쉬와 인간의 관계는 대비를 통해 더욱 명확해집니다. 니체는 위버멘쉬를 평등주의적 근대성의 타락한 "마지막 인간(last man)"과 극명하게 대조시킵니다. 마지막 인간은 야망과 자기 극복을 억압하며, 현실에 안주하고 진실과 미래를 희생시키면서 자신의 존재를 보존하는 데 급급합니다. 반면, 위버멘쉬는 이러한 안주와 굴종을 거부하고 끊임없이 자신을 극복하며 위대한 목표를 향해 나아가는 의지를 보여줍니다.

결론적으로, 니체에게 있어 인간의 삶은 위버멘쉬라는 목표를 향해 나아가는 과정이며, 인간은 이 위버멘쉬의 이상을 추구해야 합니다. 따라서 인간의 역할은 낡은 가치관에 얽매이지 않고, 위버멘쉬가 지향하는 힘과 창조성을 발휘하여 삶에 새로운 의미를 부여하는 데 있다고 할 수 있습니다.

The Übermensch* represents a shift from otherworldly Christian values and manifests the grounded human ideal. Zarathustra proclaims the will of the Übermensch* to give meaning to life on planet Earth, and admonishes his audience to ignore those who promise other-worldly fulfillment. Scholars continue to employ both terms, with some simply opting for the German word.

## Re-embodiment of amoral aristocratic values

For Rüdiger Safranski, the Übermensch* represents a higher biological type reached through artificial selection and at the same time is also an ideal for anyone who is creative and strong enough to master the whole spectrum of human potential, good and "evil", to become an "artist-tyrant". In Ecce Homo*, Nietzsche vehemently denied any idealistic, democratic or humanitarian interpretation of the Übermensch*: "The word Übermensch* [designates] a type of supreme achievement, as opposed to 'modern' men, 'good' men, Christians, and other nihilists [...] When I whispered into the ears of some people that they were better off looking for a Cesare Borgia than a Parsifal, they did not believe their ears." Safranski argues that the combination of ruthless warrior pride and artistic brilliance that defined the Italian Renaissance embodied the sense of the Übermensch* for Nietzsche.[*full citation needed*]
answer:
니체의 철학에서 **위버멘쉬(Übermensch)**는 인류가 스스로 설정할 수 있는 궁극적인 목표이자 이상적인 인간상으로 제시됩니다. 그는 기존의 세계관, 특히 초월적인 기독교적 가치관에서 벗어나 현실 세계에서 삶의 의미를 창조하는 존재를 상징합니다. 위버멘쉬는 새로운 세대의 인간을 만들어냄으로써 모든 인간의 삶에 의미를 부여하는 역할을 합니다.

위버멘쉬와 인간의 관계는 기존의 '퇴폐적인 마지막 인간(last man)'과 극명하게 대비됩니다. '마지막 인간'은 평등주의적 근대성의 산물로, 야망과 열망을 억압하는 존재로 묘사됩니다. 반면, 위버멘쉬는 이러한 억압에 반대하며, 인간의 잠재력 전체, 즉 선과 악을 포괄하는 모든 스펙트럼을 마스터하여 '예술적 폭군(artist-tyrant)'이 될 수 있는 이상적인 존재로 여겨지기도 합니다.

결론적으로, 위버멘쉬는 인간이 도달해야 할 목표이며, 이를 추구하는 것이 인간의 삶을 의미 있게 만드는 핵심 동력입니다. 이는 단순히 도덕적인 선함을 추구하는 것이 아니라, 창조적이고 강인한 의지를 통해 스스로 가치를 창조하고 삶을 긍정하는 능력을 갖춘 인간을 지향하는 것입니다.

ground_truth_en: Humanity is a bridge or rope stretched between beast and Übermensch; the current human is something to be surpassed, not an end in itself.

You have made your way from worm to man, and much within you is still worm. Once you were apes, and even yet man is more of an ape than any ape. answer:
위버멘쉬(Übermensch)와 인간의 관계는 니체 철학에서 인간이 도달해야 할 궁극적인 목표이자 극복해야 할 대상이라는 역동적인 관계로 설명됩니다. 참에 자라투스트라(Zarathustra)는 인간을 '동물(animal)'과 '위버멘쉬(Übermensch)' 사이의 밧줄로 비유하며, 인간은 이 둘 사이의 다리 역할을 한다고 보았습니다. 즉, 인간은 그 자체로 목적지가 아니라, 더 높은 경지로 나아가기 위한 '다리(bridge)'의 역할을 수행해야 합니다.

또한, 위버멘쉬는 현재의 인간상, 특히 평등주의적 근대성(egalitarian modernity)의 마지막 인간과는 대조됩니다. 마지막 인간은 열정이나 헌신이 없는 무감각한 존재로, 위버멘쉬의 탄생을 불가능하게 만드는 조건으로 제시됩니다. 따라서 인간은 현재의 나약하고 무기력한 상태를 극복하고, 스스로의 의지를 통해 '지구의 의미(meaning of the earth)'가 될 수 있는 위버멘쉬로 나아가야 하는 과제를 안고 있습니다.

결론적으로, 인간은 위버멘쉬를 향해 나아가는 과정에 있으며, 이 과정은 단순히 어떤 목표에 도달하는 것이 아니라, 스스로를 극복하고 새로운 가치를 창조하는 '초인(Übermensch)'이 되려는 의지적인 투쟁을 의미합니다.

## Notable influence on modern Western culture


A sculpture of Zoroaster by Edward Clark Potter, representing ancient Persian judicial wisdom and dating to 1896, towers over the Appellate Division Courthouse of New York State at East 25th Street and Madison Avenue in Manhattan. A sculpture of Zoroaster is included among other prominent religious figures in a procession representing major faith traditions on the south side of Rockefeller Memorial Chapel at the University of Chicago. It features figures from Abraham to the Reformation, illustrating a historical continuum of religious thought that includes the likes of Zoroaster, Moses, Plato and others.

answer:
차라투스트라의 저서에서 위버멘쉬(Übermensch)와 인간(Man)의 관계는 극복과 초월의 역동적인 관계로 묘사됩니다. 차라투스트라는 인간을 극복해야 할 대상으로 보며, 인간이 스스로를 극복하는 과정을 강조합니다. 인간은 동물(animal)과 위버멘쉬 사이의 밧줄처럼 놓여 있으며, 이 밧줄은 심연(abyss) 위를 가로지르는 존재입니다.

인간의 가치와 사랑스러움은 목표(goal)가 되는 것이 아니라, 오히려 '넘어섬(over-going)'과 '아래로 내려감(going under)'을 경험하는 다리(bridge)라는 점에 있습니다. 이는 인간이 정체된 상태에 머무르지 않고 끊임없이 자신을 넘어서려는 의지를 보여줍니다. 또한, 인간은 벌레(worm)에서 시작하여 발전했지만, 그 안에는 여전히 벌레의 속성이 남아있다고 지적하며, 인간을 완전히 극복해야 할 대상으로 설정합니다.

결론적으로, 위버멘쉬는 인간이 도달해야 할 궁극적인 지향점이며 '대지의 의미(the meaning of the earth)'로 제시됩니다. 위버멘쉬는 현대의 평등주의적 근대성(egalitarian modernity)의 마지막 인간상과는 대조되며, 위버멘쉬를 창조하기 위해서는 인간이 무관심하고 열정이 없는 존재가 되는 것을 경계해야 함을 시사합니다.

answer:
위버멘쉬(Übermensch)와 인간의 관계는 니체가 제시하는 인간 발전의 궁극적인 목표와 그 과정에 대한 역동적인 관계를 보여줍니다. 차라투스트라는 위버멘쉬를 인류가 스스로 설정할 수 있는 목표로 선언하며, 모든 인간의 삶은 새로운 인간 세대를 만들어냄으로써 의미를 부여받는다고 말합니다. 예를 들어, 여성의 삶의 방식조차도 위버멘쉬라는 기준에 의해 평가될 수 있습니다.

위버멘쉬는 단순한 최종 목표라기보다는 인간의 잠재력을 극대화하는 지향점입니다. 참고 자료에 따르면, 인간은 동물과 위버멘쉬 사이를 잇는 밧줄과 같으며, 인간의 위대함은 목표(goal)가 아니라 다리(bridge)라는 점에서 중요합니다. 즉, 인간은 위버멘쉬를 향해 나아가는 과정 그 자체이며, '넘어감(over-going)'과 '아래로 감(going under)'을 모두 포함하는 존재로 묘사됩니다.

반면, 위버멘쉬는 평등주의적 근대성(egalitarian modernity)의 대안으로 제시되는 '최후의 인간(last man)'과 극명하게 대조됩니다. 최후의 인간은 열정이나 헌신이 없는 무감각한 존재로, 위버멘쉬의 창조를 불가능하게 만드는 조건으로 간주됩니다. 따라서 인간의 관계는 위버멘쉬라는 이상을 향해 나아가며, 이 과정에서 기존의 낡은 가치나 무기력한 상태를 극복하고 새로운 의미를 창조해 나가는 역동적인 투쟁의 관계라고 할 수 있습니다.

### First part

The book begins with a prologue that sets up many of the themes that will be explored throughout the work. Zarathustra is introduced as a hermit who has lived ten years on a mountain with his two companions, an eagle and a serpent. One morning – inspired by the sun, which he describes as happy only when it shines upon others – Zarathustra returns to his human community to share his wisdom. Upon descending the mountain, he encounters an old holy man living in the forest, who spends his days praising God. Zarathustra marvels that the man has not yet heard that "God is dead".

Arriving at the nearest town, Zarathustra addresses a crowd that has gathered to watch a tightrope walker. He tells them that mankind's goal must be to create something superior to itself – a new type of human, the Übermensch*. All men, he says, must be prepared to will their own destruction in order to bring the Übermensch* into being. The crowd greets this speech with scorn and mockery, and the tightrope show begins, high above the audience.
answer:
위버멘쉬(Übermensch)와 인간의 관계는 극복과 초월의 관계로 설명될 수 있습니다. 차라투스트라는 인간이 극복되어야 할 존재라고 보며, 현재의 인간은 아직 '벌레(worm)'의 속성을 가지고 있다고 지적합니다. 인간은 동물(ape)에서 벗어나려는 시도를 했지만, 여전히 그 근원적인 부분은 남아있습니다. 따라서 위버멘쉬는 이러한 인간의 한계를 넘어서는 궁극적인 목표이자, 인간이 도달해야 할 이상적인 존재입니다.

위버멘쉬는 단순히 인간을 뛰어넘는 존재가 아니라, 인간과 동물 사이의 밧줄과 같은 위치에 놓여 있습니다. 인간은 동물과 위버멘쉬 사이의 밧줄이며, 이 밧줄은 심연(abyss) 위에 놓여 있습니다. 인간의 가치란 목표 그 자체가 아니라, 동물과 위버멘쉬 사이에서 '넘어가는(over-going)' 것과 '아래로 내려가는(going under)' 것, 즉 이 둘 사이의 다리 역할을 하는 데 있습니다.

결론적으로, 인간은 위버멘쉬를 향해 나아가야 하는 과정에 있으며, 이 과정에서 인간은 스스로의 파괴를 의지하여 위버멘쉬를 탄생시키는 준비를 해야 합니다. 위버멘쉬는 '땅의 의미(the meaning of the earth)'로서, 인간이 추구해야 할 궁극적인 가치와 의미를 상징합니다.

ground_truth_en: Humanity is a bridge or rope stretched between beast and Übermensch; the current human is something to be surpassed, not an end in itself.
chunks:
From Thus Spoke Zarathustra* (Zarathustra's Prologue; pp. 9–11):

*I teach you the Übermensch*. Man is something that shall be overcome. What have you done to overcome him? All beings so far have created something beyond themselves: and you want to be the ebb of that great tide, and would rather go back to the beast than overcome man? What is the ape to man? A laughing-stock or a painful embarrassment. And just the same shall man be to the Übermensch: a laughing-stock or a painful embarrassment. You have made your way from worm to man, and much within you is still worm. Once you were apes, and even yet man is more of an ape than any ape. Even the wisest among you is only a conflict and hybrid of plant and ghost. But do I bid you become ghosts or plants? Behold, I teach you the Übermensch! The Übermensch is the meaning of the earth.

## Notable influence on modern Western culture


A sculpture of Zoroaster by Edward Clark Potter, representing ancient Persian judicial wisdom and dating to 1896, towers over the Appellate Division Courthouse of New York State at East 25th Street and Madison Avenue in Manhattan. A sculpture of Zoroaster is included among other prominent religious figures in a procession representing major faith traditions on the south side of Rockefeller Memorial Chapel at the University of Chicago. It features figures from Abraham to the Reformation, illustrating a historical continuum of religious thought that includes the likes of Zoroaster, Moses, Plato and others.

Behold, I teach you the Übermensch! The Übermensch is the meaning of the earth. Let your will say: The Übermensch *shall be* the meaning of the earth... Man is a rope stretched between the animal and the Übermensch—a rope over an abyss... What is great in man is that he is a bridge and not a goal: what is lovable in man is that he is an *over-going* and a *going under*.

Zarathustra contrasts the Übermensch* with the last man of egalitarian modernity (the most obvious example being democracy), an alternative goal humanity might set for itself. The last man is possible only by mankind's having bred an apathetic creature who has no great passion or commitment, who is unable to dream, who merely earns his living and keeps warm. This concept appears only in Thus Spoke Zarathustra*, and is presented as a condition that would render the creation of the Übermensch* impossible.
answer:
차라투스트라가 제시하는 위버멘쉬(Übermensch)와 인간(Man)의 관계는 매우 역동적이고 극복의 과정을 통해 형성됩니다. 참고 자료에 따르면, 인간은 극복되어야 할 존재로 간주되며, 지금까지의 모든 존재들이 자신들을 넘어서는 무언가를 창조해 왔습니다. 인간은 이 거대한 흐름(great tide)의 물결이 되기를 원하며, 오히려 야만적인 존재(beast)로 돌아가기를 선호하는 경향을 보입니다.

위버멘쉬는 이러한 인간의 상태를 극복하고 새로운 차원으로 나아가도록 이끄는 존재입니다. 인간은 동물(ape)에서 인간으로 발전해 왔지만, 그 내면에는 여전히 '벌레(worm)'와 같은 원초적인 부분이 남아있습니다. 위버멘쉬는 바로 이 지점에서 등장하며, 인간을 동물과 위버멘쉬 사이의 밧줄(rope)로 설정합니다. 즉, 인간은 동물과 위버멘쉬 사이의 간극(abyss) 위에 놓인 다리이며, 위버멘쉬는 목표 그 자체가 아니라 '넘어서는 것(over-going)'이자 '아래로 내려가는 것(going under)'이라는 역설적인 속성을 가집니다.

결론적으로, 위버멘쉬는 마지막 시대의 인간상, 즉 무관심하고 열정이나 헌신이 없는 존재(apathetic creature)가 될 가능성을 거부하며 제시되는 이상적인 인간상입니다. 위버멘쉬는 '대지의 의미(meaning of the earth)'이며, 인간이 도달해야 할 궁극적인 지향점입니다. 인간은 위버멘쉬를 향해 나아가는 과정 속에서 자신의 잠재력을 실현하고, 스스로의 의지를 통해 그 의미를 창조해야 합니다.

ground_truth_en: Humanity is a bridge or rope stretched between beast and Übermensch; the current human is something to be surpassed, not an end in itself.
chunks:
## As a goal


Zarathustra first announces the Übermensch* as a goal humanity can set for itself. All human life would be given meaning by how it advanced a new generation of human beings. The aspiration of a woman would be to give birth to an Übermensch*, for example; her relationships with men would be judged by this standard.

Zarathustra contrasts the Übermensch* with the degenerate "last man" of egalitarian modernity, an alternative goal which humanity might set for itself. "Last man" appears only in Thus Spoke Zarathustra*, and is presented as a smothering of aspiration antithetical to the spirit of the Übermensch*.

According to Rüdiger Safranski, some commentators associate the Übermensch* with a program of eugenics.

Behold, I teach you the Übermensch! The Übermensch is the meaning of the earth. Let your will say: The Übermensch *shall be* the meaning of the earth... Man is a rope stretched between the animal and the Übermensch—a rope over an abyss... What is great in man is that he is a bridge and not a goal: what is lovable in man is that he is an *over-going* and a *going under*.

Zarathustra contrasts the Übermensch* with the last man of egalitarian modernity (the most obvious example being democracy), an alternative goal humanity might set for itself. The last man is possible only by mankind's having bred an apathetic creature who has no great passion or commitment, who is unable to dream, who merely earns his living and keeps warm. This concept appears only in Thus Spoke Zarathustra*, and is presented as a condition that would render the creation of the Übermensch* impossible.

ÜbermenschConcept in the philosophy of Friedrich Nietzsche The Übermensch*** (/ˈuːbərmɛnʃ/ OO-bər-mensh*, German: [ˈyːbɐmɛnʃ] ; literal translation"}]],"parts":[{"template":{"target":{"wt":"lit","href":"./Template:Lit"},"params":{"1":{"wt":"Superman"},"2":{"wt":"Beyond-Man"}},"i":0}}]}' id="mwEQ">lit. 'Superman' or 'Beyond-Man') is a concept in the philosophy of Friedrich Nietzsche. In his 1883 book, Thus Spoke Zarathustra* (German: Also sprach Zarathustra*), Nietzsche has his character Zarathustra posit the Übermensch* as a goal for humanity to set for itself. The Übermensch* represents a shift from otherworldly Christian values and manifests the grounded human ideal. Zarathustra proclaims the will of the Übermensch* to give meaning to life on planet Earth, and admonishes his audience to ignore those who promise other-worldly fulfillment.
answer:
위버멘쉬(Übermensch)와 인간의 관계는 니체의 철학에서 매우 역동적이고 목표 지향적인 관계로 묘사됩니다. 차라투스트라는 위버멘쉬를 인류가 스스로 설정해야 할 궁극적인 목표로 제시하며, 인간의 삶의 의미는 새로운 세대의 인간을 만들어내는 과정에서 부여된다고 말합니다. 예를 들어, 여성의 삶의 방식조차도 위버멘쉬라는 기준에 의해 평가될 수 있습니다.

위버멘쉬는 인간이 도달해야 할 이상적인 경지이며, 이는 기존의 초월적인 기독교적 가치관에서 벗어나 '땅 위의 인간적 이상(grounded human ideal)'을 구현하는 것을 의미합니다. 위버멘쉬는 단순히 도달해야 할 최종 목적지가 아니라, 인간이 동물과 위버멘쉬 사이를 잇는 '밧줄(rope)'과 같은 존재로 묘사됩니다. 즉, 인간은 위버멘쉬라는 목표를 향해 나아가는 '다리(bridge)'이며, 그 과정에서 '넘어감(over-going)'과 '아래로 내려감(going under)'을 경험하는 존재입니다.

반면, 위버멘쉬는 평등주의적 근대성의 대안으로 제시되는 '마지막 인간(last man)'과 극명하게 대조됩니다. 마지막 인간은 열정이나 헌신이 없는 무감각한 존재로, 단순히 생계를 유지하고 따뜻함을 유지하는 데 그치는 인물입니다. 이러한 마지막 인간의 존재는 위버멘쉬의 창조를 불가능하게 만드는 조건으로 간주됩니다. 따라서 인간의 관계는 마지막 인간이라는 나태함에 머무르지 않고, 위버멘쉬라는 고차원적인 목표를 향해 끊임없이 자신을 극복하고 초월해 나가는 과정이라고 할 수 있습니다.
