“What art thou doing there?” said he at last, “I
knew long ago that the devil would trip me up. Now he draggeth me to
hell: wilt thou prevent him?”

“On mine honour, my friend,” answered Zarathustra, “there is nothing of
all that whereof thou speakest: there is no devil and no hell. Thy soul
will be dead even sooner than thy body: fear, therefore, nothing any
more!”

The man looked up distrustfully. “If thou speakest the truth,” said he,
“I lose nothing when I lose my life. I am not much more than an animal
which hath been taught to dance by blows and scanty fare.”

“Not at all,” said Zarathustra, “thou hast made danger thy calling;
therein there is nothing contemptible. Now thou perishest by thy
calling: therefore will I bury thee with mine own hands.”

When Zarathustra had said this the dying one did not reply further; but
he moved his hand as if he sought the hand of Zarathustra in gratitude.

‘Alas! where is there still a sea in which one could be drowned?’ so
soundeth our plaint—across shallow swamps.

Verily, even for dying have we become too weary; now do we keep awake
and live on—in sepulchres.”

Thus did Zarathustra hear a soothsayer speak; and the foreboding touched
his heart and transformed him. Sorrowfully did he go about and wearily;
and he became like unto those of whom the soothsayer had spoken.—

Verily, said he unto his disciples, a little while, and there cometh the
long twilight. Alas, how shall I preserve my light through it!

That it may not smother in this sorrowfulness! To remoter worlds shall
it be a light, and also to remotest nights!

Thus did Zarathustra go about grieved in his heart, and for three days
he did not take any meat or drink: he had no rest, and lost his speech.
At last it came to pass that he fell into a deep sleep.
answer:
광대가 죽어가며 차라투스트라에게 한 말에 대한 직접적인 내용은 제시된 참고 자료에 명확하게 나와 있지 않습니다. 하지만 참고 자료 2를 통해 차라투스트라와 대화하는 한 인물이 자신의 삶과 죽음에 대해 회의적인 태도를 보이며 "나는 단지 매와 적은 식사로 춤추는 법을 배운 동물에 불과하다"고 말하는 장면이 나타납니다.

이러한 맥락에서 볼 때, 광대(혹은 그와 유사한 존재)가 죽음을 앞두고 차라투스트라에게 던질 수 있는 말은 아마도 자신의 존재나 삶의 가치에 대한 허무함이나, 혹은 삶의 고통에 대한 절망적인 탄식일 수 있습니다. 참고 자료 3에서 차라투스트라가 겪는 깊은 슬픔과 고뇌를 묘사하는 부분은, 죽음과 어둠 속에서 자신의 '빛'을 어떻게 지켜낼 것인가에 대한 철학적 고뇌를 보여줍니다.

answer:
광대가 죽어가며 차라투스트라에게 한 말은 "당신은 거기서 무엇을 하고 있습니까?"였습니다. 이는 차라투스트라가 자신의 죽음을 앞두고, 그를 찾아온 이(혹은 죽어가는 존재)에게 현재의 상황에 대해 묻는 질문입니다.

이 질문은 죽음이라는 실존적 상황 앞에서 삶의 의미나 현재의 행위에 대한 근본적인 질문을 던지는 것으로 해석될 수 있습니다. 차라투스트라는 이 질문에 대해 자신이 오랫동안 악마(devil)에게 방해받을 것이라고 예상했으나, 이제 그 악마가 자신을 지옥으로 끌고 간다고 말하며 그를 막을 수 있는지 묻습니다.

차라투스트라의 대답은 매우 단호합니다. 그는 "내 명예를 걸고, 친구여, 당신이 말하는 모든 것에는 아무것도 없습니다. 악마도 지옥도 없습니다. 당신의 영혼은 당신의 육체보다 더 빨리 죽을 것입니다. 그러므로 이제 아무것도 두려워하지 마십시오!"라고 말하며, 두려움 없이 자신의 운명을 받아들이고 삶의 위험 속에서 의미를 찾으라고 격려합니다.

ground_truth_en: The dying tightrope walker fears the devil will drag his soul to hell; Zarathustra reassures him there is no devil, no hell, and his soul will die even before his body.
chunks:
At the gate of the town the grave-diggers met him: they shone their
torch on his face, and, recognising Zarathustra, they sorely derided
him. “Zarathustra is carrying away the dead dog: a fine thing that
Zarathustra hath turned a grave-digger! For our hands are too cleanly
for that roast. Will Zarathustra steal the bite from the devil? Well
then, good luck to the repast! If only the devil is not a better thief
than Zarathustra!—he will steal them both, he will eat them both!” And
they laughed among themselves, and put their heads together.

Zarathustra made no answer thereto, but went on his way. When he had
gone on for two hours, past forests and swamps, he had heard too much of
the hungry howling of the wolves, and he himself became a-hungry. So he
halted at a lonely house in which a light was burning.

“Hunger attacketh me,” said Zarathustra, “like a robber.

“What art thou doing there?” said he at last, “I
knew long ago that the devil would trip me up. Now he draggeth me to
hell: wilt thou prevent him?”

“On mine honour, my friend,” answered Zarathustra, “there is nothing of
all that whereof thou speakest: there is no devil and no hell. Thy soul
will be dead even sooner than thy body: fear, therefore, nothing any
more!”

The man looked up distrustfully. “If thou speakest the truth,” said he,
“I lose nothing when I lose my life. I am not much more than an animal
which hath been taught to dance by blows and scanty fare.”

“Not at all,” said Zarathustra, “thou hast made danger thy calling;
therein there is nothing contemptible. Now thou perishest by thy
calling: therefore will I bury thee with mine own hands.”

When Zarathustra had said this the dying one did not reply further; but
he moved his hand as if he sought the hand of Zarathustra in gratitude.

My cave is large and deep and hath many corners; there findeth he

Thou outcast, who hast cast thyself out, thou wilt not live amongst men

answer:
광대가 죽어가며 차라투스트라에게 한 말은 매우 비판적이고 염세적인 내용이 담겨 있습니다. 그는 차라투스트라의 행동이나 존재에 대해 깊은 불신과 경멸을 드러냅니다.

구체적으로, 광대는 차라투스트라를 "이 모든 것을 지켜본 신(God who beheld everything, AND ALSO MAN)"이라고 묘사하며, 그런 존재가 죽어야 한다고 말합니다. 그는 "이러한 증인이 살아있는 것을 견딜 수 없다"고 주장하며, 차라투스트라의 존재 자체가 인간이 견딜 수 없는 어떤 끔찍한 것을 목격했기 때문에 파멸해야 한다고 역설합니다.

결론적으로, 광대는 차라투스트라를 향해 "이 배척받은 자(Thou outcast, who hast cast thyself out), 너는 사람들 사이의 연민 속에서 살지 않을 것이다. 그렇다면 나처럼 되어라!"라고 말하며, 차라투스트라의 삶의 방식에 대한 극단적인 비판과 함께 자신과 같은 배척된 존재가 되어야 한다고 촉구합니다.

ground_truth_en: The dying tightrope walker fears the devil will drag his soul to hell; Zarathustra reassures him there is no devil, no hell, and his soul will die even before his body.
chunks:
“What art thou doing there?” said he at last, “I
knew long ago that the devil would trip me up. Now he draggeth me to
hell: wilt thou prevent him?”

“On mine honour, my friend,” answered Zarathustra, “there is nothing of
all that whereof thou speakest: there is no devil and no hell. Thy soul
will be dead even sooner than thy body: fear, therefore, nothing any
more!”

The man looked up distrustfully. “If thou speakest the truth,” said he,
“I lose nothing when I lose my life. I am not much more than an animal
which hath been taught to dance by blows and scanty fare.”

“Not at all,” said Zarathustra, “thou hast made danger thy calling;
therein there is nothing contemptible. Now thou perishest by thy
calling: therefore will I bury thee with mine own hands.”

When Zarathustra had said this the dying one did not reply further; but
he moved his hand as if he sought the hand of Zarathustra in gratitude.

This most
prying, over-intrusive, over-pitiful one had to die.

He ever beheld ME: on such a witness I would have revenge—or not live
myself.

The God who beheld everything, AND ALSO MAN: that God had to die! Man
cannot ENDURE it that such a witness should live.”

Thus spake the ugliest man. Zarathustra however got up, and prepared to
go on: for he felt frozen to the very bowels.

“Thou nondescript,” said he, “thou warnedst me against thy path. As
thanks for it I praise mine to thee. Behold, up thither is the cave of
Zarathustra.

My cave is large and deep and hath many corners; there findeth he
that is most hidden his hiding-place. And close beside it, there are
a hundred lurking-places and by-places for creeping, fluttering, and
hopping creatures.

Thou outcast, who hast cast thyself out, thou wilt not live amongst men
and men’s pity? Well then, do like me!

Speak, thou nondescript!”

“O Zarathustra,” answered the ugliest man, “thou art a rogue!

Whether HE yet liveth, or again liveth, or is thoroughly dead—which of
us both knoweth that best? I ask thee.

One thing however do I know,—from thyself did I learn it once, O
Zarathustra: he who wanteth to kill most thoroughly, LAUGHETH.

‘Not by wrath but by laughter doth one kill’—thus spakest thou once,
O Zarathustra, thou hidden one, thou destroyer without wrath, thou
dangerous saint,—thou art a rogue!”
answer:
광대가 죽어가며 차라투스트라에게 한 말은 그의 삶과 죽음에 대한 근본적인 질문과 깨달음을 담고 있습니다. 그는 자신이 겪는 상황과 존재의 의미에 대해 차라투스트라에게 묻습니다. 특히 그는 차라투스트라가 말하는 진실에 대한 반응과 자신의 존재 가치에 대해 의문을 제기합니다.

광대는 "네가 진실을 말한다면, 내 목숨을 잃어도 나는 아무것도 잃지 않는다. 나는 단지 매와 적은 식사로 춤추는 법을 배운 동물에 불과하다"고 말하며 자신의 비참한 현실을 드러냅니다. 이는 삶의 고통 속에서 인간이 느끼는 무력감과 존재의 허무함을 보여줍니다.

결론적으로 광대는 차라투스트라의 가르침에 대한 자신의 입장을 정리하며, 차라투스트라가 제시하는 길을 따르지 않는 자신의 위치를 인정하면서도, 차라투스트라의 가르침에 대한 존경을 표하는 태도를 보입니다.

ground_truth_en: Zarathustra carries the dead tightrope walker's corpse on his back out of the city and lays it in a hollow tree to protect it from wolves.
chunks:
### Section 8

When Zarathustra had said this to his heart, he put the corpse upon his
shoulders and set out on his way. Yet had he not gone a hundred steps,
when there stole a man up to him and whispered in his ear—and lo!
he that spake was the buffoon from the tower. “Leave this town, O
Zarathustra,” said he, “there are too many here who hate thee. The
good and just hate thee, and call thee their enemy and despiser; the
believers in the orthodox belief hate thee, and call thee a danger to
the multitude. It was thy good fortune to be laughed at: and verily thou
spakest like a buffoon. It was thy good fortune to associate with the
dead dog; by so humiliating thyself thou hast saved thy life to-day.
Depart, however, from this town,—or to-morrow I shall jump over thee,
a living man over a dead one.” And when he had said this, the buffoon
vanished; Zarathustra, however, went on through the dark streets.

At the gate of the town the grave-diggers met him: they shone their
torch on his face, and, recognising Zarathustra, they sorely derided
him. “Zarathustra is carrying away the dead dog: a fine thing that
Zarathustra hath turned a grave-digger! For our hands are too cleanly
for that roast. Will Zarathustra steal the bite from the devil? Well
then, good luck to the repast! If only the devil is not a better thief
than Zarathustra!—he will steal them both, he will eat them both!” And
they laughed among themselves, and put their heads together.

Zarathustra made no answer thereto, but went on his way. When he had
gone on for two hours, past forests and swamps, he had heard too much of
the hungry howling of the wolves, and he himself became a-hungry. So he
halted at a lonely house in which a light was burning.

“Hunger attacketh me,” said Zarathustra, “like a robber. Among forests
and swamps my hunger attacketh me, and late in the night.

“Strange humours hath my hunger. Often it cometh to me only after a
repast, and all day it hath failed to come: where hath it been?”

And thereupon Zarathustra knocked at the door of the house. An old man
appeared, who carried a light, and asked: “Who cometh unto me and my bad
sleep?”

“A living man and a dead one,” said Zarathustra. “Give me something to
eat and drink, I forgot it during the day. He that feedeth the hungry
refresheth his own soul, saith wisdom.”

The old man withdrew, but came back immediately and offered Zarathustra
bread and wine. “A bad country for the hungry,” said he; “that is why
I live here. Animal and man come unto me, the anchorite. But bid thy
companion eat and drink also, he is wearier than thou.” Zarathustra
answered: “My companion is dead; I shall hardly be able to persuade him
to eat.” “That doth not concern me,” said the old man sullenly; “he
that knocketh at my door must take what I offer him. Eat, and fare ye
well!”—

Thereafter Zarathustra again went on for two hours, trusting to the path
and the light of the stars: for he was an experienced night-walker, and
liked to look into the face of all that slept. When the morning dawned,
however, Zarathustra found himself in a thick forest, and no path was
any longer visible. He then put the dead man in a hollow tree at his
head—for he wanted to protect him from the wolves—and laid himself
down on the ground and moss. And immediately he fell asleep, tired in
body, but with a tranquil soul.

### Section 6

Then, however, something happened which made every mouth mute and every
eye fixed. In the meantime, of course, the rope-dancer had commenced his
performance: he had come out at a little door, and was going along the
rope which was stretched between two towers, so that it hung above the
market-place and the people. When he was just midway across, the little
door opened once more, and a gaudily-dressed fellow like a buffoon
sprang out, and went rapidly after the first one. “Go on, halt-foot,”
 cried his frightful voice, “go on, lazy-bones, interloper,
sallow-face!—lest I tickle thee with my heel! What dost thou here
between the towers? In the tower is the place for thee, thou shouldst be
locked up; to one better than thyself thou blockest the way!”—And with
every word he came nearer and nearer the first one. When, however, he
was but a step behind, there happened the frightful thing which made
every mouth mute and every eye fixed—he uttered a yell like a devil,
and jumped over the other who was in his way. The latter, however, when
he thus saw his rival triumph, lost at the same time his head and his
footing on the rope; he threw his pole away, and shot downwards faster
than it, like an eddy of arms and legs, into the depth. The market-place
and the people were like the sea when the storm cometh on: they all flew
apart and in disorder, especially where the body was about to fall.

Zarathustra, however, remained standing, and just beside him fell the
body, badly injured and disfigured, but not yet dead. After a while
consciousness returned to the shattered man, and he saw Zarathustra
kneeling beside him. “What art thou doing there?” said he at last, “I
knew long ago that the devil would trip me up. Now he draggeth me to
hell: wilt thou prevent him?”

“On mine honour, my friend,” answered Zarathustra, “there is nothing of
all that whereof thou speakest: there is no devil and no hell. Thy soul
will be dead even sooner than thy body: fear, therefore, nothing any
more!”

The man looked up distrustfully. “If thou speakest the truth,” said he,
“I lose nothing when I lose my life. I am not much more than an animal
which hath been taught to dance by blows and scanty fare.”

“Not at all,” said Zarathustra, “thou hast made danger thy calling;
therein there is nothing contemptible. Now thou perishest by thy
calling: therefore will I bury thee with mine own hands.”

When Zarathustra had said this the dying one did not reply further; but
he moved his hand as if he sought the hand of Zarathustra in gratitude.

## Synopsis
### First part

The book begins with a prologue that sets up many of the themes that will be explored throughout the work. Zarathustra is introduced as a hermit who has lived ten years on a mountain with his two companions, an eagle and a serpent. One morning – inspired by the sun, which he describes as happy only when it shines upon others – Zarathustra returns to his human community to share his wisdom. Upon descending the mountain, he encounters an old holy man living in the forest, who spends his days praising God. Zarathustra marvels that the man has not yet heard that "God is dead".

Arriving at the nearest town, Zarathustra addresses a crowd that has gathered to watch a tightrope walker. He tells them that mankind's goal must be to create something superior to itself – a new type of human, the Übermensch*. All men, he says, must be prepared to will their own destruction in order to bring the Übermensch* into being. The crowd greets this speech with scorn and mockery, and the tightrope show begins, high above the audience. When the rope-dancer is halfway across, a clown comes up behind him, urging him to get out of the way. The clown then leaps over the rope-dancer, causing the latter to lose balance and plummet to the ground. The crowd scatters, but Zarathustra stays with the fatally injured tightrope performer and implies that he admires him for having chosen such a dangerous livelihood. Zarathustra later takes the corpse of the rope-dancer on his shoulders into the forest and lays it in a hollow tree. He decides that from this point on, he will no longer attempt to speak to the masses, but only to a few chosen disciples.

There follows a series of discourses in which Zarathustra overturns many of the precepts of Christian morality. He gathers a group of disciples, but ultimately abandons them, saying that he will not return until they have disowned him.
answer:
차라투스트라는 여러 상황에서 시신을 처리하는데, 이는 그의 철학적 태도와 삶의 방식을 반영합니다. 예를 들어, 그는 처음에는 밧줄 곡예사의 시신을 자신의 어깨에 메고 숲으로 가져가 나무 구멍에 눕혀 늑대들로부터 보호했습니다. 이는 그가 위험을 감수하는 삶의 방식을 긍정하고, 죽음을 두려워하지 않는 태도를 보여줍니다.

또한, 그는 다른 상황에서도 시신을 다룹니다. 그는 굶주림을 느꼈을 때 '살아있는 사람과 죽은 사람'을 데리고 와서 음식을 얻으려 했습니다. 이는 그가 생명과 죽음의 경계를 넘나들며 삶의 본질을 탐구하고 있음을 시사합니다.

결론적으로, 차라투스트라에게 시신은 단순한 죽음의 잔해가 아니라, 삶과 죽음, 그리고 기존의 도덕적 가치관을 초월하는 경험의 일부로 다루어지는 대상이었습니다.

## 6. Reception and Influence

There has been considerable interest in German idealism in the last twenty years, as hostility waned in analytic philosophy, traditional assumptions faded in continental philosophy, and bridges were built between the two approaches. Philosophers like Richard Bernstein and Richard Rorty, inspired by Wilfrid Sellars, may be credited with re-introducing Hegel to analytic philosophy as an alternative to classical empiricism. Robert Pippin later defended a non-metaphysical Hegel, which has been a subject of intense debate, but which has also made Hegel relevant to contemporary debates about realism and anti-realism. More recently, Robert Brandom has championed the “normative” conception of rationality that he finds in Kant and Hegel, and which suggests that concepts function as rules regulating judgment rather than mere representations. Some, like Catherine Malabou, have even attempted to apply the insights of the German idealists to contemporary neuroscience. Finally, it would be remiss not to mention the extraordinary historical-philosophical scholarship, in both German and English, that has been produced on German idealism in recent years. The literature listed in the bibliography has not only enriched our understanding of German idealism with new editions, translations, and commentaries, it has also expanded the horizons of philosophical scholarship by identifying new problems and new solutions to problems arising in different traditions and contexts.

answer:
차라투스트라가 시신을 처리한 방식은 상황과 맥락에 따라 다르게 나타납니다. 처음에는 그가 짊어지고 있던 시신을 가지고 떠났으며, 이는 그가 겪은 모욕과 조롱 속에서도 자신의 길을 계속 나아가는 행위로 해석될 수 있습니다. 또한, 숲속에서 그는 늑대들로부터 시신을 보호하기 위해 죽은 사람을 나무 구멍에 두었습니다.

더 극적인 장면에서는, 밧줄 곡예사와의 충돌 후 심하게 다친 시신을 발견했을 때, 차라투스트라는 그 곁에 무릎을 꿇고 있었습니다. 그는 죽어가는 사람에게 "네 영혼은 네 육체보다 더 빨리 죽을 것이니, 그러므로 아무것도 두려워하지 말라"고 말하며, 그를 자신의 손으로 묻어주겠다고 말함으로써 시신을 처리하는 데 있어 일종의 위로와 수용의 태도를 보였습니다.

to eat.” “That doth not concern me,” said the old man sullenly; “he
that knocketh at my door must take what I offer him. Eat, and fare ye
well!”—

Thereafter Zarathustra again went on for two hours, trusting to the path
and the light of the stars: for he was an experienced night-walker, and
liked to look into the face of all that slept. When the morning dawned,
however, Zarathustra found himself in a thick forest, and no path was
any longer visible. He then put the dead man in a hollow tree at his
head—for he wanted to protect him from the wolves—and laid himself
down on the ground and moss. And immediately he fell asleep, tired in
body, but with a tranquil soul.

## Synopsis
### First part

The book begins with a prologue that sets up many of the themes that will be explored throughout the work. Zarathustra is introduced as a hermit who has lived ten years on a mountain with his two companions, an eagle and a serpent. One morning – inspired by the sun, which he describes as happy only when it shines upon others – Zarathustra returns to his human community to share his wisdom. Upon descending the mountain, he encounters an old holy man living in the forest, who spends his days praising God. Zarathustra marvels that the man has not yet heard that "God is dead".

Arriving at the nearest town, Zarathustra addresses a crowd that has gathered to watch a tightrope walker. He tells them that mankind's goal must be to create something superior to itself – a new type of human, the Übermensch*. All men, he says, must be prepared to will their own destruction in order to bring the Übermensch* into being. The crowd greets this speech with scorn and mockery, and the tightrope show begins, high above the audience. When the rope-dancer is halfway across, a clown comes up behind him, urging him to get out of the way. The clown then leaps over the rope-dancer, causing the latter to lose balance and plummet to the ground. The crowd scatters, but Zarathustra stays with the fatally injured tightrope performer and implies that he admires him for having chosen such a dangerous livelihood. Zarathustra later takes the corpse of the rope-dancer on his shoulders into the forest and lays it in a hollow tree. He decides that from this point on, he will no longer attempt to speak to the masses, but only to a few chosen disciples.

There follows a series of discourses in which Zarathustra overturns many of the precepts of Christian morality. He gathers a group of disciples, but ultimately abandons them, saying that he will not return until they have disowned him.

### Section 7

Meanwhile the evening came on, and the market-place veiled itself in
gloom. Then the people dispersed, for even curiosity and terror become
fatigued. Zarathustra, however, still sat beside the dead man on the
ground, absorbed in thought: so he forgot the time. But at last it
became night, and a cold wind blew upon the lonely one. Then arose
Zarathustra and said to his heart:

Verily, a fine catch of fish hath Zarathustra made to-day! It is not a
man he hath caught, but a corpse.

Sombre is human life, and as yet without meaning: a buffoon may be
fateful to it.

I want to teach men the sense of their existence, which is the Superman,
the lightning out of the dark cloud—man.

But still am I far from them, and my sense speaketh not unto their
sense. To men I am still something between a fool and a corpse.

Gloomy is the night, gloomy are the ways of Zarathustra. Come, thou cold
and stiff companion! I carry thee to the place where I shall bury thee
with mine own hands.
answer:
차라투스트라는 여러 상황에서 시신을 처리했는데, 이는 그의 철학적 여정과 인간 존재에 대한 그의 관점을 반영합니다.

첫 번째 사례에서, 마을 사람들에게 조롱당한 후, 그는 죽은 개(dead dog)를 짊어지고 떠났습니다. 또한, 밧줄 곡예사가 다친 후에는 그 시신을 어깨에 메고 숲으로 가져가 나무 구멍에 묻었습니다. 이는 그가 세상의 비난과 죽음, 그리고 기존의 가치관을 초월하여 나아가는 과정을 상징적으로 보여줍니다.

나중에 그는 자신이 잡은 것이 인간이 아닌 '시신(corpse)'이라고 인식하며, 인간의 삶이 아직 의미가 없다고 보았습니다. 결국 그는 자신의 동반자(companion)를 데리고 그를 묻어버리겠다고 말하며, 자신의 여정 속에서 죽음과 무의미함을 직면하고 극복하려는 태도를 보였습니다.

When Zarathustra had said this to his heart, he put the corpse upon his
shoulders and set out on his way. Yet had he not gone a hundred steps,
when there stole a man up to him and whispered in his ear—and lo!
he that spake was the buffoon from the tower. “Leave this town, O
Zarathustra,” said he, “there are too many here who hate thee. The
good and just hate thee, and call thee their enemy and despiser; the
believers in the orthodox belief hate thee, and call thee a danger to
the multitude. It was thy good fortune to be laughed at: and verily thou
spakest like a buffoon. It was thy good fortune to associate with the
dead dog; by so humiliating thyself thou hast saved thy life to-day.
Depart, however, from this town,—or to-morrow I shall jump over thee,
a living man over a dead one.” And when he had said this, the buffoon
vanished; Zarathustra, however, went on through the dark streets.

At the gate of the town the grave-diggers met him: they shone their
torch on his face, and, recognising Zarathustra, they sorely derided
him. “Zarathustra is carrying away the dead dog: a fine thing that
Zarathustra hath turned a grave-digger! For our hands are too cleanly
for that roast. Will Zarathustra steal the bite from the devil? Well
then, good luck to the repast! If only the devil is not a better thief
than Zarathustra!—he will steal them both, he will eat them both!” And
they laughed among themselves, and put their heads together.

Zarathustra made no answer thereto, but went on his way. When he had
gone on for two hours, past forests and swamps, he had heard too much of
the hungry howling of the wolves, and he himself became a-hungry. So he
halted at a lonely house in which a light was burning.

“Hunger attacketh me,” said Zarathustra, “like a robber. Among forests
and swamps my hunger attacketh me, and late in the night.

“Strange humours hath my hunger. Often it cometh to me only after a
repast, and all day it hath failed to come: where hath it been?”

And thereupon Zarathustra knocked at the door of the house. An old man
appeared, who carried a light, and asked: “Who cometh unto me and my bad
sleep?”

“A living man and a dead one,” said Zarathustra. “Give me something to
eat and drink, I forgot it during the day. He that feedeth the hungry
refresheth his own soul, saith wisdom.”

The old man withdrew, but came back immediately and offered Zarathustra
bread and wine. “A bad country for the hungry,” said he; “that is why
I live here. Animal and man come unto me, the anchorite. But bid thy
companion eat and drink also, he is wearier than thou.” Zarathustra
answered: “My companion is dead; I shall hardly be able to persuade him
to eat.” “That doth not concern me,” said the old man sullenly; “he
that knocketh at my door must take what I offer him. Eat, and fare ye
well!”—

any longer visible. He then put the dead man in a hollow tree at his
head—for he wanted to protect him from the wolves—and laid himself

body, but with a tranquil soul.

His third wife, Hvōvi, was childless. Zoroaster died when he was 77 years and 40 days old. There are conflicting traditions on Zoroaster's manner of death. The most common is that he was murdered by a karapan* (priest of the old religion) named Brādrēs, while performing at an altar. The Dēnkart*, and the epic Shahnameh*, ascribe his death to a Turanian soldier named Baraturish, potentially a spin on the same figure, while other traditions combine both accounts or hold that he died of old age.

When, however, he
was but a step behind, there happened the frightful thing which made
every mouth mute and every eye fixed—he uttered a yell like a devil,
and jumped over the other who was in his way. The latter, however, when
he thus saw his rival triumph, lost at the same time his head and his
footing on the rope; he threw his pole away, and shot downwards faster
than it, like an eddy of arms and legs, into the depth. The market-place
and the people were like the sea when the storm cometh on: they all flew
apart and in disorder, especially where the body was about to fall.

Zarathustra, however, remained standing, and just beside him fell the
body, badly injured and disfigured, but not yet dead. After a while
consciousness returned to the shattered man, and he saw Zarathustra
kneeling beside him. “What art thou doing there?” said he at last, “I
knew long ago that the devil would trip me up.
answer:
차라투스트라는 위험한 곡예사(tightrope performer)의 시신을 발견한 후, 그 시신을 자신의 어깨에 메고 숲으로 가져가 나무 구멍에 묻었습니다. 이는 그가 더 이상 대중에게 말하지 않고 소수의 선택받은 제자들에게만 이야기하겠다는 결심을 보여주는 행위로 해석될 수 있습니다.

이러한 행동은 차라투스트라가 대중의 시선과 일반적인 도덕률에서 벗어나 자신만의 길을 가겠다는 의지를 상징합니다. 그는 대중과의 소통을 거부하고, 자신만의 새로운 가치와 사상을 추구하는 존재로서의 역할을 강화하고 있습니다.

결론적으로, 시신을 처리하는 행위는 차라투스트라가 기존의 질서와 대중의 기대에서 벗어나, 자신만의 철학적 여정을 시작했음을 보여주는 중요한 전환점이라고 볼 수 있습니다.

## Notable influence on modern Western culture


A sculpture of Zoroaster by Edward Clark Potter, representing ancient Persian judicial wisdom and dating to 1896, towers over the Appellate Division Courthouse of New York State at East 25th Street and Madison Avenue in Manhattan. A sculpture of Zoroaster is included among other prominent religious figures in a procession representing major faith traditions on the south side of Rockefeller Memorial Chapel at the University of Chicago. It features figures from Abraham to the Reformation, illustrating a historical continuum of religious thought that includes the likes of Zoroaster, Moses, Plato and others.

His third wife, Hvōvi, was childless. Zoroaster died when he was 77 years and 40 days old. There are conflicting traditions on Zoroaster's manner of death. The most common is that he was murdered by a karapan* (priest of the old religion) named Brādrēs, while performing at an altar. The Dēnkart*, and the epic Shahnameh*, ascribe his death to a Turanian soldier named Baraturish, potentially a spin on the same figure, while other traditions combine both accounts or hold that he died of old age.

이러한 행위는 그가 대중(masses)에게 더 이상 연설하지 않고, 오직 소수의 선택받은 제자들(chosen disciples)에게만 가르침을 전하겠다는 결심을 상징합니다. 이는 기존의 도덕적 가르침을 뒤엎는 그의 철학적 전환과, 대중적인 영향력에서 벗어나 새로운 가치를 추구하는 그의 태도를 보여줍니다.

knew long ago that the devil would trip me up. Now he draggeth me to
hell: wilt thou prevent him?”

“On mine honour, my friend,” answered Zarathustra, “there is nothing of
all that whereof thou speakest: there is no devil and no hell. Thy soul
will be dead even sooner than thy body: fear, therefore, nothing any
more!”

The man looked up distrustfully. “If thou speakest the truth,” said he,
“I lose nothing when I lose my life. I am not much more than an animal
which hath been taught to dance by blows and scanty fare.”

“Not at all,” said Zarathustra, “thou hast made danger thy calling;
therein there is nothing contemptible. Now thou perishest by thy
calling: therefore will I bury thee with mine own hands.”

When Zarathustra had said this the dying one did not reply further; but
he moved his hand as if he sought the hand of Zarathustra in gratitude.
answer:
차라투스트라가 시신을 처리한 방식은 그의 정신적 여정과 태도의 변화를 상징적으로 보여줍니다. 처음에는 그가 마음속으로 결심을 했을 때 시신을 어깨에 메고 길을 떠났습니다. 이후, 그는 위험한 상황(줄타기 공연자의 죽음)을 겪은 후, 그 공연자의 시신을 어깨에 메어 숲속의 속이 빈 나무에 묻었습니다.

이러한 행위는 차라투스트라가 대중과의 소통을 포기하고 소수의 선택된 제자들에게만 집중하기로 결정했음을 암시합니다. 그는 더 이상 대중에게 말하려 하지 않고, 자신의 길을 걸으며 새로운 가치를 추구하는 모습을 보여줍니다.

결론적으로, 시신을 처리하는 행위는 그가 기존의 도덕적 가치관을 거부하고, 위험을 감수하는 삶의 방식을 긍정하며, 자신의 새로운 사명을 수행하는 과정에서 겪는 극적인 전환점을 나타냅니다.

answer:
차라투스트라는 위험에 처한 곡예사의 시신을 발견한 후, 그를 포기하지 않고 자신의 어깨에 메어 숲속의 속이 빈 나무에 묻었습니다. 이는 그가 더 이상 대중에게 말하지 않고 소수의 선택받은 제자들에게만 이야기하겠다는 결심을 보여주는 행위였습니다.

이러한 행동은 차라투스트라가 기존의 도덕적 규범들을 뒤엎는 담론을 이어가면서, 자신을 따르지 않는 이들을 배제하고 새로운 가치를 추구하는 그의 철학적 태도를 반영합니다. 그는 위험을 감수한 삶 자체에 가치를 부여하며, 죽음 앞에서도 두려워하지 말라고 격려하는 모습을 보였습니다.

결론적으로, 차라투스트라의 시신 처리 방식은 그가 기존 질서에 얽매이지 않고, 자신의 길을 걸으며 스스로의 운명을 개척하는 '위버멘쉬(Übermensch)'의 모습을 상징적으로 보여줍니다.

## Notable influence on modern Western culture


A sculpture of Zoroaster by Edward Clark Potter, representing ancient Persian judicial wisdom and dating to 1896, towers over the Appellate Division Courthouse of New York State at East 25th Street and Madison Avenue in Manhattan. A sculpture of Zoroaster is included among other prominent religious figures in a procession representing major faith traditions on the south side of Rockefeller Memorial Chapel at the University of Chicago. It features figures from Abraham to the Reformation, illustrating a historical continuum of religious thought that includes the likes of Zoroaster, Moses, Plato and others.

ground_truth_en: Zarathustra carries the dead tightrope walker's corpse on his back out of the city and lays it in a hollow tree to protect it from wolves.
chunks:
### Section 8

When Zarathustra had said this to his heart, he put the corpse upon his
shoulders and set out on his way. Yet had he not gone a hundred steps,
when there stole a man up to him and whispered in his ear—and lo!
he that spake was the buffoon from the tower. “Leave this town, O
Zarathustra,” said he, “there are too many here who hate thee. The
good and just hate thee, and call thee their enemy and despiser; the
believers in the orthodox belief hate thee, and call thee a danger to
the multitude. It was thy good fortune to be laughed at: and verily thou
spakest like a buffoon. It was thy good fortune to associate with the
dead dog; by so humiliating thyself thou hast saved thy life to-day.
Depart, however, from this town,—or to-morrow I shall jump over thee,
a living man over a dead one.” And when he had said this, the buffoon
vanished; Zarathustra, however, went on through the dark streets.

The crowd greets this speech with scorn and mockery, and the tightrope show begins, high above the audience. When the rope-dancer is halfway across, a clown comes up behind him, urging him to get out of the way. The clown then leaps over the rope-dancer, causing the latter to lose balance and plummet to the ground. The crowd scatters, but Zarathustra stays with the fatally injured tightrope performer and implies that he admires him for having chosen such a dangerous livelihood. Zarathustra later takes the corpse of the rope-dancer on his shoulders into the forest and lays it in a hollow tree. He decides that from this point on, he will no longer attempt to speak to the masses, but only to a few chosen disciples.

There follows a series of discourses in which Zarathustra overturns many of the precepts of Christian morality. He gathers a group of disciples, but ultimately abandons them, saying that he will not return until they have disowned him.

“A bad country for the hungry,” said he; “that is why
I live here. Animal and man come unto me, the anchorite. But bid thy
companion eat and drink also, he is wearier than thou.” Zarathustra
answered: “My companion is dead; I shall hardly be able to persuade him
to eat.” “That doth not concern me,” said the old man sullenly; “he
that knocketh at my door must take what I offer him. Eat, and fare ye
well!”—

Thereafter Zarathustra again went on for two hours, trusting to the path
and the light of the stars: for he was an experienced night-walker, and
liked to look into the face of all that slept. When the morning dawned,
however, Zarathustra found himself in a thick forest, and no path was
any longer visible. He then put the dead man in a hollow tree at his
head—for he wanted to protect him from the wolves—and laid himself
down on the ground and moss. And immediately he fell asleep, tired in
body, but with a tranquil soul.
answer:
차라투스트라가 시신을 처리한 방식은 그의 철학적 여정과 태도의 변화를 상징적으로 보여줍니다. 처음에는 그가 짊어졌던 시신을 어깨에 메고 길을 떠났습니다. 이후, 그는 위험한 상황에서 다리줄타기 공연자가 쓰러졌을 때, 그 시신을 어깨에 메어 숲속의 구멍에 묻었습니다. 이는 그가 대중과의 소통을 포기하고 소수의 선택받은 제자들에게만 집중하기로 결정한 후의 행위로 해석될 수 있습니다.

나중에 다른 상황에서, 차라투스트라는 늑대들로부터 시신을 보호하기 위해 그 시신을 나무 구멍에 두었습니다. 이는 그가 세상의 일반적인 도덕이나 대중의 시선으로부터 벗어나, 자신만의 길을 걸으며 고독하지만 평온한 영혼을 추구했음을 나타냅니다.

결론적으로, 차라투스트라에게 시신은 단순히 죽은 자의 유해가 아니라, 그가 세상의 가치와 도덕으로부터 분리되어 자신만의 가치를 실현해 나가는 과정에서 겪는 상징적인 무게이자, 그가 선택한 삶의 방식을 지탱하는 일부로 처리된 것으로 볼 수 있습니다.

### Section 2

Hardly, however, had Zarathustra spoken these words, when he fell down
as one dead, and remained long as one dead. When however he again came
to himself, then was he pale and trembling, and remained lying; and for
long he would neither eat nor drink. This condition continued for seven
days; his animals, however, did not leave him day nor night, except that
the eagle flew forth to fetch food. And what it fetched and foraged,
it laid on Zarathustra’s couch: so that Zarathustra at last lay among
yellow and red berries, grapes, rosy apples, sweet-smelling herbage, and
pine-cones. At his feet, however, two lambs were stretched, which the
eagle had with difficulty carried off from their shepherds.

At last, after seven days, Zarathustra raised himself upon his couch,
took a rosy apple in his hand, smelt it and found its smell pleasant.
Then did his animals think the time had come to speak unto him.

“What art thou doing there?” said he at last, “I
knew long ago that the devil would trip me up. Now he draggeth me to
hell: wilt thou prevent him?”

“On mine honour, my friend,” answered Zarathustra, “there is nothing of
all that whereof thou speakest: there is no devil and no hell. Thy soul
will be dead even sooner than thy body: fear, therefore, nothing any
more!”

The man looked up distrustfully. “If thou speakest the truth,” said he,
“I lose nothing when I lose my life. I am not much more than an animal
which hath been taught to dance by blows and scanty fare.”

“Not at all,” said Zarathustra, “thou hast made danger thy calling;
therein there is nothing contemptible. Now thou perishest by thy
calling: therefore will I bury thee with mine own hands.”

When Zarathustra had said this the dying one did not reply further; but
he moved his hand as if he sought the hand of Zarathustra in gratitude.
answer:
차라투스트라는 줄타기 공연자가 쓰러져 죽은 후, 그 시신을 자신의 어깨에 메고 숲으로 가져가 나무 구멍에 묻었습니다. 이는 그가 더 이상 대중에게 말하지 않고 소수의 선택받은 제자들에게만 이야기하겠다는 결심을 보여주는 행위로 해석될 수 있습니다.

이러한 행동은 그가 기존의 도덕적 가르침을 뒤엎는 담론을 펼치면서, 대중과의 관계를 단절하고 자신만의 새로운 길을 모색하고 있음을 시사합니다. 그는 기존의 질서나 도덕에 얽매이지 않고, 자신만의 가치를 추구하는 새로운 존재(위버멘쉬, Übermensch)를 향한 여정을 상징적으로 보여줍니다.

결론적으로, 차라투스트라의 시신 처리 방식은 기존의 가치 체계로부터의 단절과, 자신만의 새로운 사상과 가치를 실현하기 위한 고독하고도 극단적인 선택을 나타냅니다.

ground_truth_en: The Last Man is the contemptible figure who seeks only comfort and small pleasures, having lost all aspiration and the capacity for self-overcoming; he is the antithesis of the Übermensch.
chunks:
Last manAntithesis to the Übermensch in Nietzschean philosophy

* (Learn how and when to remove this message)*

The last man** (German: Letzter Mensch*) is a term used by the philosopher Friedrich Nietzsche in Thus Spoke Zarathustra* to describe the antithesis of his theorized superior being, the Übermensch*, whose imminent appearance is heralded by Zarathustra. The last man is the archetypal passive nihilist. He is tired of life, takes no risks, and seeks only comfort and security. Therefore, the last man is unable to build and act upon a self-actualized ethos.

## Appearance in Thus Spoke Zarathustra*

The last man's first appearance is in "Zarathustra's Prologue". According to Nietzsche, the last man is the goal that modern society and Western civilization have apparently set for themselves. After having unsuccessfully attempted to get the populace to accept the Übermensch* as the goal of society, Zarathustra confronts them with a goal so disgusting that he assumes that it will revolt them – a culture which seeks only passive comfort and routine, avoiding everything that could potentially bring risk, pain, or disappointment. Zarathustra fails in this attempt, and instead of repelling and manipulating the populace into pursuing the goal of the Übermensch, the populace take Zarathustra literally and choose the "disgusting" goal of becoming the last men. This decision leaves Zarathustra disheartened and disappointed.

## 5. The Human Exemplar

Whatever else may be said about the *Übermensch, *Nietzsche clearly had in mind an exemplary figure and an exception among humans, one “whose inexhaustible fertility and power keep up the faith in man.” For some commentators, Nietzsche’s distinction between overman and the last man has political ramifications. The hope for an overman figure to appear would seem to be permissible for one individual, many, or even a social ideal, depending on the culture within which it appears. Modernity, in Nietzsche’s view, is in such a state of decadence that it would be fortunate, indeed, to see the emergence of even one such type, given that modern sociopolitical arrangements are more conducive to creating the egalitarian “last man” who “blinks” at expectations for rank, self-overcoming, and striving for greatness. The last men are “ the most harmful to the species because they preserve their existence as much at the expense of the truth as at the expense of the future” (“Why I am a Destiny” in *Ecce Homo *1). Although Nietzsche never lays out a precise political program from these ideas, it is at least clear that theoretical justifications for complacency or passivity are antithetical to his philosophy. What, then, may be said about Nietzsche as political thinker? Nietzsche’s political sympathies are definitely not democratic in any ordinary way of thinking about that sort of arrangement. Nor are they socialist or Marxist.
answer:
'마지막 인간(Last man, 독일어: Letzter Mensch)'은 니체가 그의 이론에서 제시한 우월한 존재인 **위버멘쉬(Übermensch)**의 정반대 개념으로 사용된 용어입니다. 마지막 인간은 수동적 허무주의자(passive nihilist)의 전형으로 묘사됩니다. 그는 삶에 지쳐 있으며 위험을 감수하지 않고 오직 안락함과 안정만을 추구합니다. 따라서 마지막 인간은 자기실현된 윤리(self-actualized ethos)를 구축하고 행동하는 능력이 없습니다.

니체는 마지막 인간을 현대 사회와 서구 문명이 스스로 설정한 목표로 보았습니다. 이들은 위험, 고통, 실망을 초래할 수 있는 모든 것을 피하고 수동적인 안락함과 일상을 추구하는 문화를 지향합니다. 결국, 니체가 위버멘쉬를 사회의 목표로 제시하려 했을 때, 대중은 오히려 이러한 '역겨운' 목표, 즉 마지막 인간이 되는 것을 선택하게 되면서 니체는 실망하게 됩니다.

더 나아가, 마지막 인간은 종(species)에게 가장 해로운 존재로 간주됩니다. 그들은 진실이나 미래를 희생하면서 자신의 존재를 유지하려 하기 때문에, 자기 극복(self-overcoming)과 위대함을 추구하는 것과는 정반대되는 태도를 보입니다. 니체의 철학적 관점에서 볼 때, 이러한 안주나 수동성에 대한 이론적 정당화는 그의 철학과 상충됩니다.

Last manAntithesis to the Übermensch in Nietzschean philosophy

* (Learn how and when to remove this message)*

The last man** (German: Letzter Mensch*) is a term used by the philosopher Friedrich Nietzsche in Thus Spoke Zarathustra* to describe the antithesis of his theorized superior being, the Übermensch*, whose imminent appearance is heralded by Zarathustra. The last man is the archetypal passive nihilist. He is tired of life, takes no risks, and seeks only comfort and security. Therefore, the last man is unable to build and act upon a self-actualized ethos.

## 6. Reception and Influence

There has been considerable interest in German idealism in the last twenty years, as hostility waned in analytic philosophy, traditional assumptions faded in continental philosophy, and bridges were built between the two approaches. Philosophers like Richard Bernstein and Richard Rorty, inspired by Wilfrid Sellars, may be credited with re-introducing Hegel to analytic philosophy as an alternative to classical empiricism. Robert Pippin later defended a non-metaphysical Hegel, which has been a subject of intense debate, but which has also made Hegel relevant to contemporary debates about realism and anti-realism. More recently, Robert Brandom has championed the “normative” conception of rationality that he finds in Kant and Hegel, and which suggests that concepts function as rules regulating judgment rather than mere representations. Some, like Catherine Malabou, have even attempted to apply the insights of the German idealists to contemporary neuroscience. Finally, it would be remiss not to mention the extraordinary historical-philosophical scholarship, in both German and English, that has been produced on German idealism in recent years. The literature listed in the bibliography has not only enriched our understanding of German idealism with new editions, translations, and commentaries, it has also expanded the horizons of philosophical scholarship by identifying new problems and new solutions to problems arising in different traditions and contexts.

## Appearance in Thus Spoke Zarathustra*

The last man's first appearance is in "Zarathustra's Prologue". According to Nietzsche, the last man is the goal that modern society and Western civilization have apparently set for themselves. Zarathustra fails in this attempt, and instead of repelling and manipulating the populace into pursuing the goal of the Übermensch, the populace take Zarathustra literally and choose the "disgusting" goal of becoming the last men. '마지막 인간(Last man)'은 니체가 그의 이론에서 제시한 우월한 존재인 **위버멘쉬(Übermensch)**의 정반대 개념으로 사용되는 용어입니다. 마지막 인간은 수동적 허무주의자(passive nihilist)의 전형을 나타냅니다.

그는 삶에 지쳐 있으며 위험을 감수하지 않고 오직 안락함과 안정만을 추구합니다. 따라서 마지막 인간은 자기실현된 윤리(self-actualized ethos)를 구축하고 행동하는 능력이 없습니다.

The last man** (German: Letzter Mensch*) is a term used by the philosopher Friedrich Nietzsche in Thus Spoke Zarathustra* to describe the antithesis of his theorized superior being, the Übermensch*, whose imminent appearance is heralded by Zarathustra. The last man is the archetypal passive nihilist. He is tired of life, takes no risks, and seeks only comfort and security. Therefore, the last man is unable to build and act upon a self-actualized ethos.

## Appearance in Thus Spoke Zarathustra*

The last man's first appearance is in "Zarathustra's Prologue". According to Nietzsche, the last man is the goal that modern society and Western civilization have apparently set for themselves. After having unsuccessfully attempted to get the populace to accept the Übermensch* as the goal of society, Zarathustra confronts them with a goal so disgusting that he assumes that it will revolt them – a culture which seeks only passive comfort and routine, avoiding everything that could potentially bring risk, pain, or disappointment. Zarathustra fails in this attempt, and instead of repelling and manipulating the populace into pursuing the goal of the Übermensch, the populace take Zarathustra literally and choose the "disgusting" goal of becoming the last men. This decision leaves Zarathustra disheartened and disappointed.

## As a goal

Zarathustra first announces the Übermensch* as a goal humanity can set for itself. All human life would be given meaning by how it advanced a new generation of human beings. The aspiration of a woman would be to give birth to an Übermensch*, for example; her relationships with men would be judged by this standard.

Zarathustra contrasts the Übermensch* with the degenerate "last man" of egalitarian modernity, an alternative goal which humanity might set for itself. "Last man" appears only in Thus Spoke Zarathustra*, and is presented as a smothering of aspiration antithetical to the spirit of the Übermensch*.

According to Rüdiger Safranski, some commentators associate the Übermensch* with a program of eugenics.
answer:
'마지막 인간(Last man)'은 니체의 철학에서 자신이 제시하는 우월한 존재인 **위버멘쉬(Übermensch)**의 정반대 개념으로 사용됩니다. 마지막 인간은 수동적 허무주의자(passive nihilist)의 전형으로 묘사됩니다.

그는 삶에 지쳐 있으며, 어떤 위험도 감수하지 않고 오직 안락함과 안정만을 추구합니다. 따라서 마지막 인간은 스스로 실현된 정신(self-actualized ethos)을 구축하고 행동하는 능력이 없기 때문에 위버멘쉬와 대립되는 존재로 간주됩니다.

또한, 마지막 인간은 현대 사회와 서구 문명이 스스로 설정한 목표라고 볼 수 있습니다. 이들은 위험, 고통, 실망을 피하고 수동적인 안락함과 일상을 추구하는 문화를 지향하며, 이는 위버멘쉬의 정신과는 정반대되는 '혐오스러운(disgusting)' 목표로 제시됩니다.

마지막 인간은 삶에 지쳐 있으며, 위험을 감수하지 않고 오직 안락함과 안정만을 추구합니다. 따라서 그는 자기실현된 윤리(self-actualized ethos)를 구축하고 행동하는 능력이 결여되어 있습니다. 니체 철학에서 마지막 인간은 현대 사회와 서구 문명이 스스로 설정한 목표로 여겨지며, 이는 위험, 고통, 실망을 피하고 수동적인 편안함과 일상을 추구하는 문화와 연결됩니다.

The last man** (German: Letzter Mensch*) is a term used by the philosopher Friedrich Nietzsche in Thus Spoke Zarathustra* to describe the antithesis of his theorized superior being, the Übermensch*, whose imminent appearance is heralded by Zarathustra. The last man is the archetypal passive nihilist. He is tired of life, takes no risks, and seeks only comfort and security. Therefore, the last man is unable to build and act upon a self-actualized ethos.

## Quotations


Quotations on the concept of the "last man" by Nietzsche:

I call myself the last philosopher because I am the last man. Nobody talks to me as myself, and my voice comes to me like that of a dying person.

— eKGWB/NF-1872,19 – Fragments summer 1872 – early 1873

]</nowiki>] – Fragments November 1882 – February 1883"}},"i":0}}]}' id="mwKg"/>

The opposite of the overman [Übermensch] is the last man: I created him at the same time with that. Everything superhuman appears to man as illness and madness. You have to be a sea to absorb a dirty stream without getting dirty.

Whatever else may be said about the *Übermensch, *Nietzsche clearly had in mind an exemplary figure and an exception among humans, one “whose inexhaustible fertility and power keep up the faith in man.” For some commentators, Nietzsche’s distinction between overman and the last man has political ramifications. The hope for an overman figure to appear would seem to be permissible for one individual, many, or even a social ideal, depending on the culture within which it appears. Modernity, in Nietzsche’s view, is in such a state of decadence that it would be fortunate, indeed, to see the emergence of even one such type, given that modern sociopolitical arrangements are more conducive to creating the egalitarian “last man” who “blinks” at expectations for rank, self-overcoming, and striving for greatness. The last men are “ the most harmful to the species because they preserve their existence as much at the expense of the truth as at the expense of the future” (“Why I am a Destiny” in *Ecce Homo *1).
answer:
'마지막 인간(Last man)'은 니체 철학에서 이상적인 인간상인 위버멘쉬(Übermensch)의 정반대 개념으로 제시됩니다. 마지막 인간은 수동적 허무주의자(passive nihilist)의 전형으로 묘사됩니다.

그는 삶에 지쳐 있으며 위험을 감수하지 않고 오직 안락함과 안정만을 추구합니다. 따라서 마지막 인간은 자기실현된 윤리(self-actualized ethos)를 구축하고 행동할 능력이 없습니다. 참고 자료에 따르면, 위버멘쉬(Übermensch)는 인간에게 질병이나 광기로 비춰지기 때문에, 마지막 인간은 이러한 초인적인 존재의 가능성을 거부하고 현상 유지에 안주하는 존재라고 볼 수 있습니다.

결론적으로, 마지막 인간은 진실이나 미래를 희생시키면서 자신의 존재를 보존하려는 경향이 있어 종(species)에게 가장 해로운 존재로 간주됩니다. 이는 위버멘쉬가 추구하는 자기 극복(self-overcoming)과 대조되는 모습입니다.

## Notable influence on modern Western culture


A sculpture of Zoroaster by Edward Clark Potter, representing ancient Persian judicial wisdom and dating to 1896, towers over the Appellate Division Courthouse of New York State at East 25th Street and Madison Avenue in Manhattan. A sculpture of Zoroaster is included among other prominent religious figures in a procession representing major faith traditions on the south side of Rockefeller Memorial Chapel at the University of Chicago. It features figures from Abraham to the Reformation, illustrating a historical continuum of religious thought that includes the likes of Zoroaster, Moses, Plato and others.

Modernity, in Nietzsche’s view, is in such a state of decadence that it would be fortunate, indeed, to see the emergence of even one such type, given that modern sociopolitical arrangements are more conducive to creating the egalitarian “last man” who “blinks” at expectations for rank, self-overcoming, and striving for greatness. The last men are “ the most harmful to the species because they preserve their existence as much at the expense of the truth as at the expense of the future” (“Why I am a Destiny” in *Ecce Homo *1).

## Quotations


Quotations on the concept of the "last man" by Nietzsche:

I call myself the last philosopher because I am the last man. Nobody talks to me as myself, and my voice comes to me like that of a dying person.

— eKGWB/NF-1872,19 – Fragments summer 1872 – early 1873

]</nowiki>] – Fragments November 1882 – February 1883"}},"i":0}}]}' id="mwKg"/>

The opposite of the overman [Übermensch] is the last man: I created him at the same time with that. Everything superhuman appears to man as illness and madness. You have to be a sea to absorb a dirty stream without getting dirty.
answer:
'마지막 인간(Last man)'은 니체가 그의 이론에서 제시한 우월한 존재인 **위버멘쉬(Übermensch)**의 정반대 개념으로 사용된 용어입니다. 마지막 인간은 삶에 지쳐 있으며 위험을 감수하지 않고 오직 안락함과 안정만을 추구하는 **수동적 허무주의자(passive nihilist)**의 전형을 나타냅니다.

그는 자기실현된 정신(self-actualized ethos)을 구축하고 행동하는 능력이 없기 때문에, 위버멘쉬와는 대조됩니다. 마지막 인간은 삶의 고통이나 도전을 회피하며 현상 유지에만 몰두합니다.

결론적으로, 마지막 인간은 위버멘쉬와 대립하며, 현대 사회의 평등주의적 배열 속에서 기대치나 위대함을 추구하는 것에 눈을 감는 존재로 묘사됩니다. 그는 "진실을 희생하면서까지 자신의 존재를 보존하는" 방식으로 종(species)에 가장 해로운 존재로 여겨지기도 합니다.

The last man** (German: Letzter Mensch*) is a term used by the philosopher Friedrich Nietzsche in Thus Spoke Zarathustra* to describe the antithesis of his theorized superior being, the Übermensch*, whose imminent appearance is heralded by Zarathustra. The last man is the archetypal passive nihilist. He is tired of life, takes no risks, and seeks only comfort and security. Therefore, the last man is unable to build and act upon a self-actualized ethos.

## Notable influence on modern Western culture


A sculpture of Zoroaster by Edward Clark Potter, representing ancient Persian judicial wisdom and dating to 1896, towers over the Appellate Division Courthouse of New York State at East 25th Street and Madison Avenue in Manhattan. A sculpture of Zoroaster is included among other prominent religious figures in a procession representing major faith traditions on the south side of Rockefeller Memorial Chapel at the University of Chicago. It features figures from Abraham to the Reformation, illustrating a historical continuum of religious thought that includes the likes of Zoroaster, Moses, Plato and others.

Whatever else may be said about the *Übermensch, *Nietzsche clearly had in mind an exemplary figure and an exception among humans, one “whose inexhaustible fertility and power keep up the faith in man.” For some commentators, Nietzsche’s distinction between overman and the last man has political ramifications. The hope for an overman figure to appear would seem to be permissible for one individual, many, or even a social ideal, depending on the culture within which it appears. Modernity, in Nietzsche’s view, is in such a state of decadence that it would be fortunate, indeed, to see the emergence of even one such type, given that modern sociopolitical arrangements are more conducive to creating the egalitarian “last man” who “blinks” at expectations for rank, self-overcoming, and striving for greatness. The last men are “ the most harmful to the species because they preserve their existence as much at the expense of the truth as at the expense of the future” (“Why I am a Destiny” in *Ecce Homo *1).
answer:
'마지막 인간(Last man)'은 니체가 그의 이론에서 제시한 우월한 존재인 **위버멘쉬(Übermensch)**의 정반대 개념으로 사용된 용어입니다. 마지막 인간은 근본적으로 수동적인 허무주의자(passive nihilist)의 전형을 나타냅니다.

마지막 인간의 특징은 삶에 지쳐 있으며, 위험을 감수하지 않고 오직 안락함과 안정만을 추구한다는 점입니다. 이러한 성향 때문에 마지막 인간은 자기실현된 윤리(self-actualized ethos)를 구축하고 행동할 능력이 없습니다. 즉, 그는 스스로를 극복하고 더 높은 가치를 창조하려는 의지를 갖지 못합니다.

니체에게 있어 마지막 인간은 종족에게 가장 해로운 존재로 간주됩니다. 왜냐하면 그들은 진실과 미래를 희생하면서 자신의 존재를 유지하려 하기 때문이며, 이는 위버멘쉬가 추구하는 자기 극복과 성장을 방해하기 때문입니다. 현대 사회의 평등주의적 배열은 이러한 '마지막 인간'을 만들어내기에 더 적합하다고 니체는 보았습니다.

Last manAntithesis to the Übermensch in Nietzschean philosophy

* (Learn how and when to remove this message)*

The last man** (German: Letzter Mensch*) is a term used by the philosopher Friedrich Nietzsche in Thus Spoke Zarathustra* to describe the antithesis of his theorized superior being, the Übermensch*, whose imminent appearance is heralded by Zarathustra. The last man is the archetypal passive nihilist. He is tired of life, takes no risks, and seeks only comfort and security. Therefore, the last man is unable to build and act upon a self-actualized ethos.

## Appearance in Thus Spoke Zarathustra*


The last man's first appearance is in "Zarathustra's Prologue". According to Nietzsche, the last man is the goal that modern society and Western civilization have apparently set for themselves. After having unsuccessfully attempted to get the populace to accept the Übermensch* as the goal of society, Zarathustra confronts them with a goal so disgusting that he assumes that it will revolt them – a culture which seeks only passive comfort and routine, avoiding everything that could potentially bring risk, pain, or disappointment. Zarathustra fails in this attempt, and instead of repelling and manipulating the populace into pursuing the goal of the Übermensch, the populace take Zarathustra literally and choose the "disgusting" goal of becoming the last men. This decision leaves Zarathustra disheartened and disappointed.

## Quotations


Quotations on the concept of the "last man" by Nietzsche:

I call myself the last philosopher because I am the last man. Nobody talks to me as myself, and my voice comes to me like that of a dying person.

— eKGWB/NF-1872,19 – Fragments summer 1872 – early 1873

]</nowiki>] – Fragments November 1882 – February 1883"}},"i":0}}]}' id="mwKg"/>

The opposite of the overman [Übermensch] is the last man: I created him at the same time with that. Everything superhuman appears to man as illness and madness. You have to be a sea to absorb a dirty stream without getting dirty.
answer:
'마지막 인간(Last man)'은 니체가 그의 이론에서 제시한 우월한 존재인 **위버멘쉬(Übermensch)**의 정반대 개념으로 사용됩니다. 마지막 인간은 수동적 허무주의자(passive nihilist)의 전형으로 묘사됩니다.

그는 삶에 지쳐 있으며 위험을 감수하지 않고 오직 안락함과 안정만을 추구합니다. 따라서 마지막 인간은 스스로 실현된 윤리(self-actualized ethos)를 구축하고 행동할 능력이 없습니다. 니체는 마지막 인간을 현대 사회와 서구 문명이 스스로 목표로 삼은 존재로 보았으며, 이는 수동적인 편안함과 일상을 추구하며 위험, 고통, 실망을 피하려는 문화와 일치합니다.

결론적으로, 마지막 인간은 위버멘쉬와 대립되는 존재이며, 초인(Übermensch)이 도달해야 할 목표가 아닌, 오히려 그들이 도달하지 않기를 바라는, 안주하고 소극적인 인간상을 상징합니다.

ground_truth_en: The Übermensch creates new values through self-overcoming, while the Last Man abandons aspiration and contents himself with small pleasures and security; they represent opposite human possibilities after the death of God.
chunks:
## Philosophy
### Übermensch

Another concept important to understanding Nietzsche is the Übermensch* (Superman). Writing about nihilism in Also Sprach Zarathustra*, Nietzsche introduced an Übermensch*. According to Laurence Lampert, "the death of God must be followed by a long twilight of piety and nihilism (II. 19; III. 8). Zarathustra's gift of the overman is given to mankind not aware of the problem to which the overman is the solution." Zarathustra presents the Übermensch* as the creator of new values, and he appears as a solution to the problem of the death of God and nihilism. The Übermensch* does not follow the morality of common people since that favours mediocrity but rises above the notion of good and evil and above the "herd". In this way Zarathustra proclaims his ultimate goal as the journey towards the state of the Übermensch*. He wants a kind of spiritual evolution of self-awareness and overcoming of traditional views on morality and justice that stem from the superstitious beliefs still deeply rooted or related to the notion of God and Christianity.

From Thus Spoke Zarathustra* (Zarathustra's Prologue; pp. 9–11):

*I teach you the Übermensch*. Man is something that shall be overcome. What have you done to overcome him? All beings so far have created something beyond themselves: and you want to be the ebb of that great tide, and would rather go back to the beast than overcome man? What is the ape to man? A laughing-stock or a painful embarrassment. And just the same shall man be to the Übermensch: a laughing-stock or a painful embarrassment. You have made your way from worm to man, and much within you is still worm. Once you were apes, and even yet man is more of an ape than any ape. Even the wisest among you is only a conflict and hybrid of plant and ghost. But do I bid you become ghosts or plants? Behold, I teach you the Übermensch! The Übermensch is the meaning of the earth. Let your will say: The Übermensch *shall be* the meaning of the earth... Man is a rope stretched between the animal and the Übermensch—a rope over an abyss... What is great in man is that he is a bridge and not a goal: what is lovable in man is that he is an *over-going* and a *going under*.

Zarathustra contrasts the Übermensch* with the last man of egalitarian modernity (the most obvious example being democracy), an alternative goal humanity might set for itself. The last man is possible only by mankind's having bred an apathetic creature who has no great passion or commitment, who is unable to dream, who merely earns his living and keeps warm. This concept appears only in Thus Spoke Zarathustra*, and is presented as a condition that would render the creation of the Übermensch* impossible.[*better source needed*]

Last manAntithesis to the Übermensch in Nietzschean philosophy

* (Learn how and when to remove this message)*

The last man** (German: Letzter Mensch*) is a term used by the philosopher Friedrich Nietzsche in Thus Spoke Zarathustra* to describe the antithesis of his theorized superior being, the Übermensch*, whose imminent appearance is heralded by Zarathustra. The last man is the archetypal passive nihilist. He is tired of life, takes no risks, and seeks only comfort and security. Therefore, the last man is unable to build and act upon a self-actualized ethos.

## As a goal

Zarathustra first announces the Übermensch* as a goal humanity can set for itself. All human life would be given meaning by how it advanced a new generation of human beings. The aspiration of a woman would be to give birth to an Übermensch*, for example; her relationships with men would be judged by this standard.

Zarathustra contrasts the Übermensch* with the degenerate "last man" of egalitarian modernity, an alternative goal which humanity might set for itself. "Last man" appears only in Thus Spoke Zarathustra*, and is presented as a smothering of aspiration antithetical to the spirit of the Übermensch*.

According to Rüdiger Safranski, some commentators associate the Übermensch* with a program of eugenics.
answer:
위버멘쉬(Übermensch)와 마지막 인간(Last man)은 니체 철학에서 극명하게 대립되는 두 개념입니다. 위버멘쉬는 새로운 가치를 창조하는 존재이며, 신의 죽음(death of God)과 허무주의(nihilism)라는 문제에 대한 해결책으로 제시됩니다. 그는 기존의 도덕이나 선악의 개념을 넘어서고 '무리(herd)'의 가치관을 거부하며, 자기 인식의 영적 진화를 통해 자신을 극복해 나가는 존재입니다.

반면, 마지막 인간은 위버멘쉬의 정반대 개념으로, 평등주의적 근대성의 전형을 보여줍니다. 마지막 인간은 삶에 지쳐 있으며 위험을 감수하지 않고 오직 안락함과 안정만을 추구합니다. 그는 스스로 실현된 윤리(self-actualized ethos)를 구축하거나 행동할 능력이 없기 때문에, 위버멘쉬가 추구하는 창조적이고 역동적인 삶의 방식과는 정반대의 상태에 놓여 있습니다.

요약하자면, 위버멘쉬가 인간을 극복하고 새로운 의미를 창조하려는 '초인(Superman)'의 이상이라면, 마지막 인간은 열정이나 헌신이 결여되어 무기력하게 안주하는 '수동적 허무주의자(passive nihilist)'의 전형이라고 할 수 있습니다.

## 6. Reception and Influence

There has been considerable interest in German idealism in the last twenty years, as hostility waned in analytic philosophy, traditional assumptions faded in continental philosophy, and bridges were built between the two approaches. Philosophers like Richard Bernstein and Richard Rorty, inspired by Wilfrid Sellars, may be credited with re-introducing Hegel to analytic philosophy as an alternative to classical empiricism. Robert Pippin later defended a non-metaphysical Hegel, which has been a subject of intense debate, but which has also made Hegel relevant to contemporary debates about realism and anti-realism. More recently, Robert Brandom has championed the “normative” conception of rationality that he finds in Kant and Hegel, and which suggests that concepts function as rules regulating judgment rather than mere representations. Some, like Catherine Malabou, have even attempted to apply the insights of the German idealists to contemporary neuroscience. Finally, it would be remiss not to mention the extraordinary historical-philosophical scholarship, in both German and English, that has been produced on German idealism in recent years. The literature listed in the bibliography has not only enriched our understanding of German idealism with new editions, translations, and commentaries, it has also expanded the horizons of philosophical scholarship by identifying new problems and new solutions to problems arising in different traditions and contexts.

answer:
위버멘쉬(Übermensch)와 마지막 인간(Last man)은 니체 철학에서 대립되는 두 가지 개념으로 이해할 수 있습니다. 위버멘쉬는 새로운 가치를 창조하는 존재이며, 신의 죽음(death of God)과 허무주의(nihilism)라는 문제에 대한 해결책으로 제시됩니다. 그는 기존의 도덕이나 선악의 개념을 넘어서고 '무리(herd)'의 가치관을 거부하며, 자기 인식의 영적 진화를 통해 전통적인 도덕관을 극복하고자 합니다.

반면에 마지막 인간(Last man)은 위버멘쉬의 정반대 개념으로, 수동적인 허무주의자의 전형입니다. 그는 삶에 지쳐 있으며 위험을 감수하지 않고 오직 안락함과 안정만을 추구합니다. 마지막 인간은 스스로 실현된 윤리(self-actualized ethos)를 구축하고 행동할 능력이 없기 때문에, 위버멘쉬의 등장이 불가능하게 만드는 조건으로 제시됩니다.

요약하자면, 위버멘쉬는 극복하고 창조하는 능동적인 초인(超人)이라면, 마지막 인간은 무기력하고 안주하는 수동적인 존재라는 점에서 근본적인 차이가 있습니다. 니체에게 있어 인간은 동물과 위버멘쉬 사이의 밧줄과 같으며, 위버멘쉬는 인간을 넘어서는 존재로서의 가능성을 상징합니다.

ground_truth_en: The Übermensch creates new values through self-overcoming, while the Last Man abandons aspiration and contents himself with small pleasures and security; they represent opposite human possibilities after the death of God.

Last manAntithesis to the Übermensch in Nietzschean philosophy

* (Learn how and when to remove this message)*

The last man** (German: Letzter Mensch*) is a term used by the philosopher Friedrich Nietzsche in Thus Spoke Zarathustra* to describe the antithesis of his theorized superior being, the Übermensch*, whose imminent appearance is heralded by Zarathustra. The last man is the archetypal passive nihilist. He is tired of life, takes no risks, and seeks only comfort and security. Therefore, the last man is unable to build and act upon a self-actualized ethos.

## Quotations

Quotations on the concept of the "last man" by Nietzsche:

I call myself the last philosopher because I am the last man. Nobody talks to me as myself, and my voice comes to me like that of a dying person.

— eKGWB/NF-1872,19 – Fragments summer 1872 – early 1873

]</nowiki>] – Fragments November 1882 – February 1883"}},"i":0}}]}' id="mwKg"/>

The opposite of the overman [Übermensch] is the last man: I created him at the same time with that. Everything superhuman appears to man as illness and madness. You have to be a sea to absorb a dirty stream without getting dirty.

— eKGWB/NF-1882,4 – Fragments November 1882 – February 1883

] – Friedrich Nietzsche, On the Genealogy of Morals"}},"i":0}}]}' id="mwLA"/>

For this is how things are: the diminution and leveling of European man constitutes our greatest danger, for the sight of him makes us weary.—We can see nothing today that wants to grow greater, we suspect that things will continue to go down, down, to become thinner, more good-natured, more prudent, more comfortable, more mediocre, more indifferent, more Chinese, more Christian—there is no doubt that man is getting 'better' all the time.

—  – Friedrich Nietzsche, On the Genealogy of Morals

] – Friedrich Nietzsche, Beyond Good and Evil : 262"}},"i":0}}]}' id="mwLg"/>

everything around them decays and produces decay, that nothing will endure until the day after tomorrow, except one species of man, the incurably MEDIOCRE. The mediocre alone have a prospect of continuing and propagating themselves--they will be the men of the future, the sole survivors.

—  – Friedrich Nietzsche, Beyond Good and Evil : 262

## 5. The Human Exemplar

Whatever else may be said about the *Übermensch, *Nietzsche clearly had in mind an exemplary figure and an exception among humans, one “whose inexhaustible fertility and power keep up the faith in man.” For some commentators, Nietzsche’s distinction between overman and the last man has political ramifications. The hope for an overman figure to appear would seem to be permissible for one individual, many, or even a social ideal, depending on the culture within which it appears. Modernity, in Nietzsche’s view, is in such a state of decadence that it would be fortunate, indeed, to see the emergence of even one such type, given that modern sociopolitical arrangements are more conducive to creating the egalitarian “last man” who “blinks” at expectations for rank, self-overcoming, and striving for greatness. The last men are “ the most harmful to the species because they preserve their existence as much at the expense of the truth as at the expense of the future” (“Why I am a Destiny” in *Ecce Homo *1). Although Nietzsche never lays out a precise political program from these ideas, it is at least clear that theoretical justifications for complacency or passivity are antithetical to his philosophy. What, then, may be said about Nietzsche as political thinker? Nietzsche’s political sympathies are definitely not democratic in any ordinary way of thinking about that sort of arrangement. Nor are they socialist or Marxist.
answer:
위버멘쉬(Übermensch)와 마지막 인간(Last man)은 니체 철학에서 정반대의 개념으로 대립합니다. 위버멘쉬는 끊임없이 자신을 극복하고 더 높은 가치를 창조해 나가는 초인적인 존재를 의미합니다. 반면에 마지막 인간은 위버멘쉬의 정반대 개념으로, 삶에 지쳐 위험을 감수하지 않고 오직 안락함과 안정만을 추구하는 태도를 가진 존재입니다.

마지막 인간은 수동적 허무주의자(passive nihilist)의 전형으로 묘사됩니다. 그는 삶에 싫증을 느끼고 아무런 위험도 감수하지 않으며, 자기실현된 윤리(self-actualized ethos)를 구축하고 행동하는 능력이 없습니다. 니체는 유럽인이 점차 미미해지고, 더 온순하고, 신중하며, 안락하고, 평범하며, 무관심해지는 경향을 보인다고 보았으며, 이러한 '평범한 인간(the incurably MEDIOCRE)'만이 미래에 생존하고 번성할 것이라고 보았습니다.

결론적으로, 위버멘쉬가 끊임없는 성장과 힘을 통해 인간의 가능성을 증명한다면, 마지막 인간은 이러한 성장을 거부하고 현상 유지와 안락함에 머무름으로써 종의 진보를 저해하는 가장 해로운 존재로 간주됩니다.

answer:
위버멘쉬(Übermensch)와 마지막 인간(Last man)은 니체 철학에서 정반대의 개념으로 대비됩니다. 위버멘쉬는 니체가 상상한 초인적인 존재로, 끊임없는 성장과 힘을 통해 인간의 가능성을 유지시키는 상징적인 인물입니다. 반면에 마지막 인간은 위버멘쉬의 정반대 개념으로, 삶에 지쳐 위험을 감수하지 않고 오직 안락함과 안정만을 추구하는 존재입니다.

마지막 인간은 수동적 허무주의자(passive nihilist)의 전형으로 묘사됩니다. 그는 삶에 지쳐 있으며, 스스로 실현된 윤리(self-actualized ethos)를 구축하고 행동하는 것을 할 수 없습니다. 니체는 유럽인이 겪고 있는 가치와 인간성의 약화, 즉 점진적인 퇴보(diminution and leveling)를 경고하며, 이러한 경향이 결국 '평범하고(mediocre)', '온화하며(good-natured)', '안일한(comfortable)' 존재들로 귀결될 것이라고 보았습니다.

결론적으로, 위버멘쉬가 자기 극복과 초월을 지향하는 반면, 마지막 인간은 이러한 성장을 거부하고 현상 유지와 안락함에 머무르며, 그 결과 종의 미래를 보존하는 대신 진실과 미래를 희생시키는 가장 해로운 존재로 간주됩니다.

ground_truth_en: The Übermensch creates new values through self-overcoming, while the Last Man abandons aspiration and contents himself with small pleasures and security; they represent opposite human possibilities after the death of God.
chunks:
Behold, I teach you the Übermensch! The Übermensch is the meaning of the earth. Let your will say: The Übermensch *shall be* the meaning of the earth... Man is a rope stretched between the animal and the Übermensch—a rope over an abyss... What is great in man is that he is a bridge and not a goal: what is lovable in man is that he is an *over-going* and a *going under*.

Zarathustra contrasts the Übermensch* with the last man of egalitarian modernity (the most obvious example being democracy), an alternative goal humanity might set for itself. The last man is possible only by mankind's having bred an apathetic creature who has no great passion or commitment, who is unable to dream, who merely earns his living and keeps warm. This concept appears only in Thus Spoke Zarathustra*, and is presented as a condition that would render the creation of the Übermensch* impossible.

Last manAntithesis to the Übermensch in Nietzschean philosophy

* (Learn how and when to remove this message)*

The last man** (German: Letzter Mensch*) is a term used by the philosopher Friedrich Nietzsche in Thus Spoke Zarathustra* to describe the antithesis of his theorized superior being, the Übermensch*, whose imminent appearance is heralded by Zarathustra. The last man is the archetypal passive nihilist. He is tired of life, takes no risks, and seeks only comfort and security. Therefore, the last man is unable to build and act upon a self-actualized ethos.

## As a goal


Zarathustra first announces the Übermensch* as a goal humanity can set for itself. All human life would be given meaning by how it advanced a new generation of human beings. The aspiration of a woman would be to give birth to an Übermensch*, for example; her relationships with men would be judged by this standard.

Zarathustra contrasts the Übermensch* with the degenerate "last man" of egalitarian modernity, an alternative goal which humanity might set for itself. "Last man" appears only in Thus Spoke Zarathustra*, and is presented as a smothering of aspiration antithetical to the spirit of the Übermensch*.

According to Rüdiger Safranski, some commentators associate the Übermensch* with a program of eugenics.
answer:
위버멘쉬(Übermensch)와 마지막 인간(Last man)은 니체 철학에서 극명하게 대립하는 두 개념입니다. 위버멘쉬는 인간이 도달해야 할 궁극적인 목표이자 가치이며, 스스로의 의지를 통해 '땅의 의미(the meaning of the earth)'가 될 수 있는 존재를 의미합니다. 이는 끊임없이 자신을 극복하고 새로운 가치를 창조해 나가는 능동적인 존재를 상징합니다.

반면, 마지막 인간(Last man)은 위버멘쉬의 정반대 개념으로 제시됩니다. 마지막 인간은 평등주의적 근대(egalitarian modernity)의 산물로, 열정이나 헌신이 없는 무감각한 존재입니다. 이들은 위험을 감수하지 않고 오직 안락함과 안정만을 추구하며, 꿈을 꾸지 못하고 단순히 생계를 유지하는 데 만족합니다. 따라서 마지막 인간은 스스로 실현된 윤리(self-actualized ethos)를 구축하거나 행동할 능력이 없는, 수동적인 허무주의자(passive nihilist)의 전형입니다.

요약하자면, 위버멘쉬가 창조와 극복을 통해 가치를 창출하는 '초인(Übermensch)'이라면, 마지막 인간은 모든 열망을 억압하고 안주함으로써 인간의 잠재력을 소멸시키는 '반대 개념(antithesis)'이라고 할 수 있습니다.

ground_truth_en: The Übermensch creates new values through self-overcoming, while the Last Man abandons aspiration and contents himself with small pleasures and security; they represent opposite human possibilities after the death of God.
chunks:
Behold, I teach you the Übermensch! The Übermensch is the meaning of the earth. Let your will say: The Übermensch *shall be* the meaning of the earth... Man is a rope stretched between the animal and the Übermensch—a rope over an abyss... What is great in man is that he is a bridge and not a goal: what is lovable in man is that he is an *over-going* and a *going under*.

Zarathustra contrasts the Übermensch* with the last man of egalitarian modernity (the most obvious example being democracy), an alternative goal humanity might set for itself. The last man is possible only by mankind's having bred an apathetic creature who has no great passion or commitment, who is unable to dream, who merely earns his living and keeps warm. This concept appears only in Thus Spoke Zarathustra*, and is presented as a condition that would render the creation of the Übermensch* impossible.

## Notable influence on modern Western culture


A sculpture of Zoroaster by Edward Clark Potter, representing ancient Persian judicial wisdom and dating to 1896, towers over the Appellate Division Courthouse of New York State at East 25th Street and Madison Avenue in Manhattan. A sculpture of Zoroaster is included among other prominent religious figures in a procession representing major faith traditions on the south side of Rockefeller Memorial Chapel at the University of Chicago. It features figures from Abraham to the Reformation, illustrating a historical continuum of religious thought that includes the likes of Zoroaster, Moses, Plato and others.

마지막 인간은 수동적 허무주의자(passive nihilist)의 전형으로 묘사됩니다. 그는 삶에 지쳐 있으며, 스스로 실현된 윤리(self-actualized ethos)를 구축하고 행동하는 것을 거부합니다. 이러한 특성 때문에 마지막 인간은 위버멘쉬와 대조되며, 초인에게는 질병이나 광기로 비춰질 수 있습니다.

결론적으로, 위버멘쉬가 생명력과 힘을 통해 인간의 믿음을 유지하는 모범적인 인물이라면, 마지막 인간은 현실에 안주하며 진실과 미래를 희생시키면서 자신의 존재를 보존하려는 경향이 있어 종(種)에게 가장 해로운 존재로 간주됩니다.

Last manAntithesis to the Übermensch in Nietzschean philosophy

* (Learn how and when to remove this message)*

The last man** (German: Letzter Mensch*) is a term used by the philosopher Friedrich Nietzsche in Thus Spoke Zarathustra* to describe the antithesis of his theorized superior being, the Übermensch*, whose imminent appearance is heralded by Zarathustra. The last man is the archetypal passive nihilist. He is tired of life, takes no risks, and seeks only comfort and security. Therefore, the last man is unable to build and act upon a self-actualized ethos.

## Quotations


Quotations on the concept of the "last man" by Nietzsche:

I call myself the last philosopher because I am the last man. Nobody talks to me as myself, and my voice comes to me like that of a dying person.

— eKGWB/NF-1872,19 – Fragments summer 1872 – early 1873

]</nowiki>] – Fragments November 1882 – February 1883"}},"i":0}}]}' id="mwKg"/>

The opposite of the overman [Übermensch] is the last man: I created him at the same time with that. Everything superhuman appears to man as illness and madness. You have to be a sea to absorb a dirty stream without getting dirty.

Whatever else may be said about the *Übermensch, *Nietzsche clearly had in mind an exemplary figure and an exception among humans, one “whose inexhaustible fertility and power keep up the faith in man.” For some commentators, Nietzsche’s distinction between overman and the last man has political ramifications. The hope for an overman figure to appear would seem to be permissible for one individual, many, or even a social ideal, depending on the culture within which it appears. Modernity, in Nietzsche’s view, is in such a state of decadence that it would be fortunate, indeed, to see the emergence of even one such type, given that modern sociopolitical arrangements are more conducive to creating the egalitarian “last man” who “blinks” at expectations for rank, self-overcoming, and striving for greatness. The last men are “ the most harmful to the species because they preserve their existence as much at the expense of the truth as at the expense of the future” (“Why I am a Destiny” in *Ecce Homo *1).
answer:
위버멘쉬(Übermensch)와 마지막 인간(Last man)은 니체 철학에서 극명하게 대립하는 두 개념입니다. 위버멘쉬는 니체가 제시한 인간의 이상적인 초인(超人)의 모습으로, 끊임없는 자기 극복과 힘을 통해 자신의 가치를 창조하고 삶을 긍정하는 존재를 의미합니다. 그는 인간이 도달해야 할 궁극적인 목표이자, 삶의 긍정적인 힘을 유지시키는 상징적인 인물입니다.

반면, 마지막 인간(Letzter Mensch)은 위버멘쉬의 정반대 개념으로, 수동적 허무주의자(passive nihilist)의 전형입니다. 그는 삶에 지쳐 있으며 위험을 감수하지 않고 오직 안락함과 안정만을 추구합니다. 따라서 마지막 인간은 자기실현된 윤리(self-actualized ethos)를 구축하고 행동하는 능력이 없으며, 위버멘쉬가 추구하는 초인적인 가치와는 정반대의 태도를 보입니다.

결론적으로, 위버멘쉬가 창조와 극복을 통해 가치를 창출하는 능동적인 존재라면, 마지막 인간은 기존의 가치에 안주하며 현실에 둔감해지는 존재입니다. 니체에게 마지막 인간은 종족에게 가장 해로운 존재로 간주되는데, 이는 그들이 진실과 미래를 희생하면서도 자신의 존재를 보존하기 때문입니다.

ground_truth_en: The Übermensch creates new values through self-overcoming, while the Last Man abandons aspiration and contents himself with small pleasures and security; they represent opposite human possibilities after the death of God.
chunks:
Behold, I teach you the Übermensch! The Übermensch is the meaning of the earth. Let your will say: The Übermensch *shall be* the meaning of the earth... Man is a rope stretched between the animal and the Übermensch—a rope over an abyss... What is great in man is that he is a bridge and not a goal: what is lovable in man is that he is an *over-going* and a *going under*.

Zarathustra contrasts the Übermensch* with the last man of egalitarian modernity (the most obvious example being democracy), an alternative goal humanity might set for itself. The last man is possible only by mankind's having bred an apathetic creature who has no great passion or commitment, who is unable to dream, who merely earns his living and keeps warm. This concept appears only in Thus Spoke Zarathustra*, and is presented as a condition that would render the creation of the Übermensch* impossible.

Whatever else may be said about the *Übermensch, *Nietzsche clearly had in mind an exemplary figure and an exception among humans, one “whose inexhaustible fertility and power keep up the faith in man.” For some commentators, Nietzsche’s distinction between overman and the last man has political ramifications. The hope for an overman figure to appear would seem to be permissible for one individual, many, or even a social ideal, depending on the culture within which it appears. Modernity, in Nietzsche’s view, is in such a state of decadence that it would be fortunate, indeed, to see the emergence of even one such type, given that modern sociopolitical arrangements are more conducive to creating the egalitarian “last man” who “blinks” at expectations for rank, self-overcoming, and striving for greatness. The last men are “ the most harmful to the species because they preserve their existence as much at the expense of the truth as at the expense of the future” (“Why I am a Destiny” in *Ecce Homo *1).

## As a goal


Zarathustra first announces the Übermensch* as a goal humanity can set for itself. All human life would be given meaning by how it advanced a new generation of human beings. The aspiration of a woman would be to give birth to an Übermensch*, for example; her relationships with men would be judged by this standard.

Zarathustra contrasts the Übermensch* with the degenerate "last man" of egalitarian modernity, an alternative goal which humanity might set for itself. "Last man" appears only in Thus Spoke Zarathustra*, and is presented as a smothering of aspiration antithetical to the spirit of the Übermensch*.

According to Rüdiger Safranski, some commentators associate the Übermensch* with a program of eugenics.
answer:
위버멘쉬(Übermensch)와 마지막 인간(last man)은 니체가 제시하는 인간상과 목표의 극명한 대조를 이룹니다. 위버멘쉬는 인간이 도달해야 할 궁극적인 목표이자, '대지의 의미(the meaning of the earth)'로 묘사됩니다. 그는 끊임없는 생식력과 힘을 통해 인간에 대한 믿음을 유지시키는 존재로, 스스로를 극복하고 끊임없이 자신을 초월(self-overcoming)하는 존재입니다.

반면, 마지막 인간은 평등주의적 근대(egalitarian modernity)의 산물로 제시됩니다. 이들은 열정이나 헌신이 없는 무감각한 존재로, 꿈을 꾸지 못하고 단순히 생계를 유지하며 따뜻함을 추구하는 데 그칩니다. 마지막 인간은 야망과 위대함에 눈을 감고, 진실과 미래를 희생하면서 자신의 존재를 보존하려는 경향이 있어 종(species)에게 가장 해로운 존재로 여겨집니다.

결론적으로, 위버멘쉬는 인간이 설정할 수 있는 긍정적이고 창조적인 목표인 반면, 마지막 인간은 이러한 야망을 질식시키는 억압적인 상태이며, 니체는 인류가 위버멘쉬를 창조할 수 있는 조건은 마지막 인간의 존재를 극복하는 데 달려 있다고 보았습니다.
