This concept appears only in Thus Spoke Zarathustra*, and is presented as a condition that would render the creation of the Übermensch* impossible.

## Notable influence on modern Western culture


A sculpture of Zoroaster by Edward Clark Potter, representing ancient Persian judicial wisdom and dating to 1896, towers over the Appellate Division Courthouse of New York State at East 25th Street and Madison Avenue in Manhattan. A sculpture of Zoroaster is included among other prominent religious figures in a procession representing major faith traditions on the south side of Rockefeller Memorial Chapel at the University of Chicago. It features figures from Abraham to the Reformation, illustrating a historical continuum of religious thought that includes the likes of Zoroaster, Moses, Plato and others.

Whatever else may be said about the *Übermensch, *Nietzsche clearly had in mind an exemplary figure and an exception among humans, one “whose inexhaustible fertility and power keep up the faith in man.” For some commentators, Nietzsche’s distinction between overman and the last man has political ramifications. The hope for an overman figure to appear would seem to be permissible for one individual, many, or even a social ideal, depending on the culture within which it appears. Modernity, in Nietzsche’s view, is in such a state of decadence that it would be fortunate, indeed, to see the emergence of even one such type, given that modern sociopolitical arrangements are more conducive to creating the egalitarian “last man” who “blinks” at expectations for rank, self-overcoming, and striving for greatness. The last men are “ the most harmful to the species because they preserve their existence as much at the expense of the truth as at the expense of the future” (“Why I am a Destiny” in *Ecce Homo *1).
answer:
위버멘쉬(Übermensch)와 마지막 인간(last man)은 니체가 제시하는 인간상에 있어 극명하게 대조되는 두 개념입니다. 위버멘쉬는 '대지의 의미(the meaning of the earth)'로 묘사되며, 인간이 도달해야 할 궁극적인 목표이자 극복해야 할 이상적인 존재입니다. 그는 끊임없는 생명력과 힘(inexhaustible fertility and power)을 통해 인간에 대한 믿음을 유지시키는 존재로 여겨집니다.

반면, 마지막 인간은 현대의 평등주의적 근대(egalitarian modernity), 특히 민주주의의 가장 명백한 예시로 간주됩니다. 마지막 인간은 열정이나 헌신이 없는 무감각한 존재로, 꿈을 꾸지 못하고 단순히 생계를 유지하며 따뜻함을 유지하는 데 그치는 인물입니다. 그는 위버멘쉬의 탄생을 불가능하게 만드는 조건으로 제시되며, 기대되는 지위, 자기 극복(self-overcoming), 그리고 위대함을 추구하는 것에 눈을 감는 존재로 묘사됩니다.

결론적으로, 위버멘쉬는 자기 극복을 통해 스스로 가치를 창조하고 역동적으로 나아가는 존재인 반면, 마지막 인간은 안주하고 현상 유지에 만족하며 진실과 미래를 희생시키는 존재입니다. 니체에게 있어 현대 사회는 이러한 마지막 인간을 만들어내기에 적합하며, 따라서 위버멘쉬와 같은 초인(overman)의 출현은 현대 문명에 매우 긍정적인 희망으로 간주됩니다.

ground_truth_en: The crowd rejects Zarathustra's teaching of the Übermensch and instead demands he give them the Last Man — they prefer comfortable mediocrity over the difficult task of self-overcoming.
chunks:
Zarathushtra Spitama**, more commonly known as Zoroaster**, was an Iranian religious reformer who challenged the tenets of the contemporary Ancient Iranian religion, becoming the spiritual founder of Zoroastrianism. In the oldest Zoroastrian scriptures, the Gathas, which he is traditionally believed to have authored, he is described as a preacher and a poet-prophet. Some have claimed, with much scholarly controversy, to find his influence in Heraclitus, Plato, Pythagoras, and, perhaps less controversially, in the Abrahamic religions, including Judaism, Christianity, and Islam, particularly through concepts of cosmic dualism and personal morality.

He spoke an Old Iranian language, named Avestan by scholars after the corpus of Zoroastrian religious texts written in that language. Based on this, it is tentative to place his homeland somewhere in the eastern regions of the Greater Iran (possibly Bactra, now in modern-day Afghanistan). His life is traditionally dated to some time around the 7th and 6th centuries BC; though most scholars, using linguistic and socio-cultural evidence, suggest a dating even further back to the end of the 2nd and the beginning of the 1st millennium BC.

Zoroastrianism eventually became Greater Iran's most prominent religion from around the 6th century BC, enjoying official sanction during the time of the Sassanid Empire, until the 7th century AD, when the religion began to decline following the Arab-Muslim conquest of Iran. Zoroaster is credited with authorship of the Gathas as well as the Yasna Haptanghaiti*, a series of hymns composed in Old Avestan that cover core Zoroastrian beliefs. Little is known about Zoroaster; most of his life is known only from these scant texts. By modern standards of historiography, no evidence can place him into a fixed period and the historicization surrounding him may be a part of a trend from before the 10th century AD that historicizes legends and myths.

His name likely means "he who manages camels", though its etymology has multiple interpretations, and the Greek form "Zoroaster" stems from later transliterations. According to Zoroastrian tradition, Zoroaster was trained as a priest from a young age and, around age 30, experienced a divine revelation which introduced him to Ahura Mazda, the Wise Lord, and the dualism of truth (asha*) versus deception (druj*). He is said to have gained royal patronage under King Vishtaspa, spread his teachings, and founded a community, marrying three times and having six children. Zoroastrian texts portray his philosophy as emphasizing free will, ethical responsibility, and aligning with asha* through good thoughts, words, and deeds.

### Section 2

Then, however, did it come to pass that Zarathustra, astonished at such
merely roguish answers, jumped back to the door of his cave, and turning
towards all his guests, cried out with a strong voice:

“O ye wags, all of you, ye buffoons! Why do ye dissemble and disguise
yourselves before me!

How the hearts of all of you convulsed with delight and wickedness,
because ye had at last become again like little children—namely,
pious,—

—Because ye at last did again as children do—namely, prayed, folded
your hands and said ‘good God’!

But now leave, I pray you, THIS nursery, mine own cave, where to-day
all childishness is carried on. Cool down, here outside, your hot
child-wantonness and heart-tumult!

To be sure: except ye become as little children ye shall not enter into
THAT kingdom of heaven.” (And Zarathustra pointed aloft with his hands.)

“But we do not at all want to enter into the kingdom of heaven: we have
become men,—SO WE WANT THE KINGDOM OF EARTH.”

## Life

Zoroaster is recorded as the son of Pourushaspa of the Spitama family, and Dugdōw, while his great-grandfather was Haēčataspa. All the names appear appropriate to the nomadic tradition. His father's name means 'possessing gray horses' (with the word aspa* meaning 'horse'), while his mother's means 'milkmaid'. According to the tradition, he had four brothers, two older and two younger, whose names are given in much later Pahlavi work.

Zoroaster's training for priesthood probably started very early around seven years of age. He became a priest probably around the age of 15, and according to Gathas, gaining knowledge from other teachers and personal experience from traveling when he left his parents at age 20. By the age of 30, Zoroaster experienced a revelation during a spring festival; on the river bank he saw a shining being, who revealed himself as Vohu Manah* (Good Purpose) and taught him about Ahura Mazda* (Wise Lord) and five other radiant figures. Zoroaster soon became aware of the existence of two primal spirits, the second being Angra Mainyu* (Destructive Spirit), with opposing concepts of Asha* (order) and Druj* (deception). Thus he decided to spend his life teaching people to seek Asha*. He received further revelations and saw a vision of the seven Amesha Spenta*, and his teachings were collected in the Gathas* and the Avesta*.

Eventually, at the age of about 42, Zoroaster received the patronage of queen Hutaosa and a ruler named Vishtaspa, an early adherent of Zoroastrianism (possibly from Bactria according to the Shahnameh).

According to the tradition, he lived for many years after Vishtaspa's conversion, managed to establish a faithful community, and married three times. His first two wives bore him three sons, Isat Vâstra, Urvatat Nara, and Hvare Chithra, and three daughters, Freni, Thriti, and Pouruchista. His third wife, Hvōvi, was childless. Zoroaster died when he was 77 years and 40 days old. There are conflicting traditions on Zoroaster's manner of death. The most common is that he was murdered by a karapan* (priest of the old religion) named Brādrēs, while performing at an altar. The Dēnkart*, and the epic Shahnameh*, ascribe his death to a Turanian soldier named Baraturish, potentially a spin on the same figure, while other traditions combine both accounts or hold that he died of old age.
answer:
차라투스트라가 군중에게 거부당한 구체적인 이유에 대해서는 제시된 자료에서 직접적으로 명시하고 있지는 않습니다. 하지만 자료들을 종합해 볼 때, 그의 가르침과 태도가 당시의 사회적 관습이나 일부 사람들의 기대와 충돌했기 때문에 이러한 반응이 나타났을 수 있습니다.

차라투스트라는 아후라 마즈다(Ahura Mazda, 지혜로운 주님)와 진리(asha\*) 대 기만(druj\*)이라는 우주적 이원론(cosmic dualism)을 가르쳤으며, 개인의 자유 의지와 윤리적 책임을 강조했습니다. 이러한 철학은 기존의 종교적 관습이나 사회적 질서에 도전하는 것으로 해석될 여지가 있었을 수 있습니다.

특히 참고 자료 2에서 차라투스트라가 "우리는 천국에 들어가고 싶지 않다. 우리는 땅의 왕국을 원한다"고 말하며 '어린아이 같은 순수함'을 거부하는 장면은, 그가 단순한 종교적 의례나 순응을 넘어선 성숙한 인간의 상태, 즉 '인간(men)'으로서의 삶과 세속적인 현실을 추구하는 태도를 보였음을 보여줍니다. 이러한 급진적이거나 새로운 가치관은 기존 질서에 익숙한 사람들에게는 거부감이나 혼란을 야기했을 가능성이 있습니다.

## 6. Reception and Influence

There has been considerable interest in German idealism in the last twenty years, as hostility waned in analytic philosophy, traditional assumptions faded in continental philosophy, and bridges were built between the two approaches. Philosophers like Richard Bernstein and Richard Rorty, inspired by Wilfrid Sellars, may be credited with re-introducing Hegel to analytic philosophy as an alternative to classical empiricism. Robert Pippin later defended a non-metaphysical Hegel, which has been a subject of intense debate, but which has also made Hegel relevant to contemporary debates about realism and anti-realism. More recently, Robert Brandom has championed the “normative” conception of rationality that he finds in Kant and Hegel, and which suggests that concepts function as rules regulating judgment rather than mere representations. Some, like Catherine Malabou, have even attempted to apply the insights of the German idealists to contemporary neuroscience. Finally, it would be remiss not to mention the extraordinary historical-philosophical scholarship, in both German and English, that has been produced on German idealism in recent years. The literature listed in the bibliography has not only enriched our understanding of German idealism with new editions, translations, and commentaries, it has also expanded the horizons of philosophical scholarship by identifying new problems and new solutions to problems arising in different traditions and contexts.

### Section 2

Then, however, did it come to pass that Zarathustra, astonished at such
merely roguish answers, jumped back to the door of his cave, and turning

“O ye wags, all of you, ye buffoons! Why do ye dissemble and disguise

answer:
차라투스트라가 군중에게 거부당한 이유는 그들이 '어린아이'처럼 행동했기 때문입니다. 그는 군중들이 자신 앞에서 위장하고 숨기려 하는 모습에 놀라움을 표하며, 그들의 마음이 순수함(pious)으로 인해 기쁨과 악함으로 요동치는 것을 보았습니다.

차라투스트라는 그들이 다시 어린아이처럼 기도하고 손을 모으는 모습에서 순수함을 발견했지만, 동시에 그들이 아직 '어린아이'의 상태에 머물러 있음을 지적했습니다. 그는 군중들에게 그들의 '어린아이 같은 짓궂음(child-wantonness)'과 마음의 동요를 잠재우고 밖으로 나가라고 요청했습니다.

결국 차라투스트라가 군중을 거부한 핵심적인 이유는, 그들이 아직 순수한 '어린아이'의 상태에 머물러 있음을 인정하고, 그 상태를 벗어나 '인간(men)'이 되어야만 '땅의 왕국(kingdom of earth)'을 추구할 수 있다고 보았기 때문입니다.

ground_truth_en: The crowd rejects Zarathustra's teaching of the Übermensch and instead demands he give them the Last Man — they prefer comfortable mediocrity over the difficult task of self-overcoming.
chunks:
## Appearance in Thus Spoke Zarathustra*

The last man's first appearance is in "Zarathustra's Prologue". According to Nietzsche, the last man is the goal that modern society and Western civilization have apparently set for themselves. After having unsuccessfully attempted to get the populace to accept the Übermensch* as the goal of society, Zarathustra confronts them with a goal so disgusting that he assumes that it will revolt them – a culture which seeks only passive comfort and routine, avoiding everything that could potentially bring risk, pain, or disappointment. Zarathustra fails in this attempt, and instead of repelling and manipulating the populace into pursuing the goal of the Übermensch, the populace take Zarathustra literally and choose the "disgusting" goal of becoming the last men. This decision leaves Zarathustra disheartened and disappointed.

### Section 8

When Zarathustra had said this to his heart, he put the corpse upon his
shoulders and set out on his way. Yet had he not gone a hundred steps,
when there stole a man up to him and whispered in his ear—and lo!
he that spake was the buffoon from the tower. “Leave this town, O
Zarathustra,” said he, “there are too many here who hate thee. The
good and just hate thee, and call thee their enemy and despiser; the
believers in the orthodox belief hate thee, and call thee a danger to
the multitude. It was thy good fortune to be laughed at: and verily thou
spakest like a buffoon. It was thy good fortune to associate with the
dead dog; by so humiliating thyself thou hast saved thy life to-day.
Depart, however, from this town,—or to-morrow I shall jump over thee,
a living man over a dead one.” And when he had said this, the buffoon
vanished; Zarathustra, however, went on through the dark streets.

At the gate of the town the grave-diggers met him: they shone their
torch on his face, and, recognising Zarathustra, they sorely derided
him. “Zarathustra is carrying away the dead dog: a fine thing that
Zarathustra hath turned a grave-digger! For our hands are too cleanly
for that roast. Will Zarathustra steal the bite from the devil? Well
then, good luck to the repast! If only the devil is not a better thief
than Zarathustra!—he will steal them both, he will eat them both!” And
they laughed among themselves, and put their heads together.

Zarathustra made no answer thereto, but went on his way. When he had
gone on for two hours, past forests and swamps, he had heard too much of
the hungry howling of the wolves, and he himself became a-hungry. So he
halted at a lonely house in which a light was burning.

“Hunger attacketh me,” said Zarathustra, “like a robber. Among forests
and swamps my hunger attacketh me, and late in the night.

“Strange humours hath my hunger. Often it cometh to me only after a
repast, and all day it hath failed to come: where hath it been?”

And thereupon Zarathustra knocked at the door of the house. An old man
appeared, who carried a light, and asked: “Who cometh unto me and my bad
sleep?”

“A living man and a dead one,” said Zarathustra. “Give me something to
eat and drink, I forgot it during the day. He that feedeth the hungry
refresheth his own soul, saith wisdom.”

The old man withdrew, but came back immediately and offered Zarathustra
bread and wine. “A bad country for the hungry,” said he; “that is why
I live here. Animal and man come unto me, the anchorite. But bid thy
companion eat and drink also, he is wearier than thou.” Zarathustra
answered: “My companion is dead; I shall hardly be able to persuade him
to eat.” “That doth not concern me,” said the old man sullenly; “he
that knocketh at my door must take what I offer him. Eat, and fare ye
well!”—

Thereafter Zarathustra again went on for two hours, trusting to the path
and the light of the stars: for he was an experienced night-walker, and
liked to look into the face of all that slept. When the morning dawned,
however, Zarathustra found himself in a thick forest, and no path was
any longer visible. He then put the dead man in a hollow tree at his
head—for he wanted to protect him from the wolves—and laid himself
down on the ground and moss. And immediately he fell asleep, tired in
body, but with a tranquil soul.

### Section 2

When, however, this took place with the ugliest man, Zarathustra stood
there like one drunken: his glance dulled, his tongue faltered and his
feet staggered. And who could divine what thoughts then passed through
Zarathustra’s soul? Apparently, however, his spirit retreated and fled
in advance and was in remote distances, and as it were “wandering on
high mountain-ridges,” as it standeth written, “‘twixt two seas,

—Wandering ‘twixt the past and the future as a heavy cloud.” Gradually,
however, while the higher men held him in their arms, he came back to
himself a little, and resisted with his hands the crowd of the honouring
and caring ones; but he did not speak. All at once, however, he turned
his head quickly, for he seemed to hear something: then laid he his
finger on his mouth and said: “COME!”

And immediately it became still and mysterious round about; from
the depth however there came up slowly the sound of a clock-bell.
Zarathustra listened thereto, like the higher men; then, however, laid
he his finger on his mouth the second time, and said again: “COME! COME!
IT IS GETTING ON TO MIDNIGHT!”—and his voice had changed. But still
he had not moved from the spot. Then it became yet stiller and more
mysterious, and everything hearkened, even the ass, and Zarathustra’s
noble animals, the eagle and the serpent,—likewise the cave of
Zarathustra and the big cool moon, and the night itself. Zarathustra,
however, laid his hand upon his mouth for the third time, and said:

COME! COME! COME! LET US NOW WANDER! IT IS THE HOUR: LET US WANDER INTO
THE NIGHT!
answer:
차라투스트라가 군중에게 거부당한 주된 이유는 그가 제시한 목표가 대중의 기대를 충족시키지 못했기 때문입니다. 니체에 따르면, 현대 사회와 서구 문명이 스스로 목표로 삼은 것은 위버멘쉬(Übermensch)였으나, 차라투스트라는 이 목표를 추구하는 대중에게 '역겹다(disgusting)'고 여겨질 만한 목표를 제시했습니다. 이 목표는 위험, 고통, 실망을 피하고 수동적인 안락함과 일상을 추구하는 문화였기 때문에, 대중은 그를 밀어내거나 조종하려던 시도가 실패로 돌아갔습니다.

결국 대중은 차라투스트라의 시도를 거부하는 대신, 그를 문자 그대로 받아들여 '마지막 인간(the last man)'이 되는 것을 선택했습니다. 이러한 대중의 반응은 차라투스트라에게 실망감과 좌절감을 안겨주었습니다. 즉, 그가 제시한 초월적인 목표 대신, 대중은 가장 나약하고 안락한 상태인 '마지막 인간'이라는 목표를 선택함으로써 차라투스트라의 의도와는 정반대의 길을 택한 것입니다.

요약하자면, 차라투스트라가 거부당한 것은 그가 제시한 이상적인 목표(위버멘쉬)를 대중이 받아들이지 못하고, 오히려 그가 의도했던 것과는 정반대인 '마지막 인간'이라는 목표를 선택했기 때문입니다. 이는 차라투스트라의 비판과 도전이 대중에게 효과적으로 전달되지 못했음을 보여주는 지점입니다.

## Synopsis
### First part

The book begins with a prologue that sets up many of the themes that will be explored throughout the work. Zarathustra is introduced as a hermit who has lived ten years on a mountain with his two companions, an eagle and a serpent. One morning – inspired by the sun, which he describes as happy only when it shines upon others – Zarathustra returns to his human community to share his wisdom. Upon descending the mountain, he encounters an old holy man living in the forest, who spends his days praising God. Zarathustra marvels that the man has not yet heard that "God is dead".

Arriving at the nearest town, Zarathustra addresses a crowd that has gathered to watch a tightrope walker. He tells them that mankind's goal must be to create something superior to itself – a new type of human, the Übermensch*. All men, he says, must be prepared to will their own destruction in order to bring the Übermensch* into being. The crowd greets this speech with scorn and mockery, and the tightrope show begins, high above the audience. When the rope-dancer is halfway across, a clown comes up behind him, urging him to get out of the way. The clown then leaps over the rope-dancer, causing the latter to lose balance and plummet to the ground. The crowd scatters, but Zarathustra stays with the fatally injured tightrope performer and implies that he admires him for having chosen such a dangerous livelihood. Zarathustra later takes the corpse of the rope-dancer on his shoulders into the forest and lays it in a hollow tree. He decides that from this point on, he will no longer attempt to speak to the masses, but only to a few chosen disciples.

There follows a series of discourses in which Zarathustra overturns many of the precepts of Christian morality. He gathers a group of disciples, but ultimately abandons them, saying that he will not return until they have disowned him.
answer:
차라투스트라가 군중에게 거부당한 주된 이유는 그가 제시한 목표, 즉 **위버멘쉬(Übermensch)**에 대한 대중의 반응 때문입니다. 차라투스트라는 인류의 목표는 스스로를 초월하는 새로운 인간, 즉 위버멘쉬를 창조하는 것이어야 한다고 주장했습니다. 하지만 군중은 이러한 주장을 조롱하고 비웃었으며, 그들이 추구하는 것은 위험이나 고통 없이 수동적인 안락함과 일상을 지키는 문화였기 때문에, 차라투스트라가 제시한 '혐오스러운(disgusting)' 목표는 대중을 오히려 거부하게 만들었습니다.

참고 자료에 따르면, 차라투스트라는 대중을 설득하여 위버멘쉬를 추구하도록 유도하려 했으나 실패했습니다. 오히려 대중은 차라투스트라의 말을 문자 그대로 받아들여 '마지막 인간(the last man)'이 되는 것을 선택했습니다. 이는 차라투스트라가 기대했던 반발이나 조종과는 정반대의 결과였으며, 이로 인해 그는 실망하고 낙담하게 되었습니다.

결론적으로, 차라투스트라의 비전은 대중이 회피하려는 안락함과 대조되는 위험을 내포하고 있었기에, 대중은 그 도전을 받아들이지 않고 거부함으로써 차라투스트라의 메시지에 응답하지 않았습니다.

“Give us this last man, O Zarathustra,”—they
called out—“make us into these last men! Then will we make thee a
present of the Superman!” And all the people exulted and smacked their
lips. Zarathustra, however, turned sad, and said to his heart:

“They understand me not: I am not the mouth for these ears.

Too long, perhaps, have I lived in the mountains; too much have I
hearkened unto the brooks and trees: now do I speak unto them as unto
the goatherds.

Calm is my soul, and clear, like the mountains in the morning. But they
think me cold, and a mocker with terrible jests.

And now do they look at me and laugh: and while they laugh they hate me
too. There is ice in their laughter.”

Zoroaster soon became aware of the existence of two primal spirits, the second being Angra Mainyu* (Destructive Spirit), with opposing concepts of Asha* (order) and Druj* (deception). Thus he decided to spend his life teaching people to seek Asha*. He received further revelations and saw a vision of the seven Amesha Spenta*, and his teachings were collected in the Gathas* and the Avesta*.

Eventually, at the age of about 42, Zoroaster received the patronage of queen Hutaosa and a ruler named Vishtaspa, an early adherent of Zoroastrianism (possibly from Bactria according to the Shahnameh).

According to the tradition, he lived for many years after Vishtaspa's conversion, managed to establish a faithful community, and married three times. His first two wives bore him three sons, Isat Vâstra, Urvatat Nara, and Hvare Chithra, and three daughters, Freni, Thriti, and Pouruchista. His third wife, Hvōvi, was childless.

Zarathushtra Spitama**, more commonly known as Zoroaster**, was an Iranian religious reformer who challenged the tenets of the contemporary Ancient Iranian religion, becoming the spiritual founder of Zoroastrianism. In the oldest Zoroastrian scriptures, the Gathas, which he is traditionally believed to have authored, he is described as a preacher and a poet-prophet.
answer:
차라투스트라(Zarathushtra)가 군중에게 거부당한 이유는 그가 제시하는 가르침이나 삶의 방식이 당시 대중의 기대와는 달랐기 때문으로 해석할 수 있습니다. 참고 자료 1에 따르면, 사람들은 그에게 "우리에게 이 마지막 인간(last man)을 만들어 달라"고 요구하며 그를 '초인(Superman)'의 선물로 만들기를 기대했습니다. 그러나 차라투스트라는 이러한 요구에 대해 슬퍼하며, 자신이 대중의 입장이 아니라 목동처럼 그들에게 말하고 있음을 깨닫습니다.

그는 자신이 산속에서 너무 오래 지내거나 시냇물과 나무에 너무 귀 기울여 왔기 때문에, 이제는 목동처럼 사람들에게 이야기하고 있음을 인식합니다. 사람들은 그를 차갑고 끔찍한 농담을 하는 자로 여기며 비웃지만, 사실 그들의 웃음 속에는 '얼음(ice)'과 같은 적대감이 담겨 있음을 통찰합니다. 이는 차라투스트라의 깊은 내면적 통찰과 진실을 대중이 이해하지 못하고 거부하는 상황을 보여줍니다.

결론적으로, 차라투스트라가 거부당한 것은 그의 메시지가 대중의 단순한 요구에 부응하는 것이 아니라, 더 깊은 진실과 변화를 요구했기 때문이며, 그 진실을 받아들이는 대신 거부하고 비웃는 군중의 태도에서 비롯된 것입니다.

## Notable influence on modern Western culture


A sculpture of Zoroaster by Edward Clark Potter, representing ancient Persian judicial wisdom and dating to 1896, towers over the Appellate Division Courthouse of New York State at East 25th Street and Madison Avenue in Manhattan. A sculpture of Zoroaster is included among other prominent religious figures in a procession representing major faith traditions on the south side of Rockefeller Memorial Chapel at the University of Chicago. It features figures from Abraham to the Reformation, illustrating a historical continuum of religious thought that includes the likes of Zoroaster, Moses, Plato and others.

Zoroaster soon became aware of the existence of two primal spirits, the second being Angra Mainyu* (Destructive Spirit), with opposing concepts of Asha* (order) and Druj* (deception). Thus he decided to spend his life teaching people to seek Asha*. He received further revelations and saw a vision of the seven Amesha Spenta*, and his teachings were collected in the Gathas* and the Avesta*.

Eventually, at the age of about 42, Zoroaster received the patronage of queen Hutaosa and a ruler named Vishtaspa, an early adherent of Zoroastrianism (possibly from Bactria according to the Shahnameh).

According to the tradition, he lived for many years after Vishtaspa's conversion, managed to establish a faithful community, and married three times. His first two wives bore him three sons, Isat Vâstra, Urvatat Nara, and Hvare Chithra, and three daughters, Freni, Thriti, and Pouruchista. His third wife, Hvōvi, was childless.
answer:
차라투스트라가 군중에게 거부당한 이유는 그가 제시하는 가르침이나 존재 방식이 대중의 이해와는 달랐기 때문입니다. 참고 자료 1에 따르면, 사람들은 그를 '마지막 인간(last man)'으로 만들고자 했지만, 차라투스트라는 그들의 이해를 받지 못한다고 느꼈습니다. 그는 자신이 산속에서 너무 오래 살았고, 시냇물과 나무에 너무 많이 귀 기울였기 때문에, 이제는 목동들에게 말하듯이 그들에게 이야기하고 있음을 깨달았습니다.

더 나아가, 군중들은 그를 차갑고 끔찍한 농담을 하는 자로 여기며 비웃었고, 그들의 웃음 속에는 '얼음(ice)'이 담겨 있다고 느꼈습니다. 이는 차라투스트라의 내면의 고요함과 명료함(Calm is my soul, and clear, like the mountains in the morning)이 대중에게는 차갑거나 이해하기 어려운 것으로 받아들여졌음을 시사합니다.

결론적으로, 차라투스트라가 거부당한 핵심적인 이유는 그의 깊은 통찰과 고독한 깨달음이 대중의 피상적인 이해와 충돌했기 때문이며, 그들의 반응은 경멸과 적대감으로 나타났습니다.

ground_truth_en: The crowd rejects Zarathustra's teaching of the Übermensch and instead demands he give them the Last Man — they prefer comfortable mediocrity over the difficult task of self-overcoming.
chunks:
## Appearance in Thus Spoke Zarathustra*


The last man's first appearance is in "Zarathustra's Prologue". According to Nietzsche, the last man is the goal that modern society and Western civilization have apparently set for themselves. After having unsuccessfully attempted to get the populace to accept the Übermensch* as the goal of society, Zarathustra confronts them with a goal so disgusting that he assumes that it will revolt them – a culture which seeks only passive comfort and routine, avoiding everything that could potentially bring risk, pain, or disappointment. Zarathustra fails in this attempt, and instead of repelling and manipulating the populace into pursuing the goal of the Übermensch, the populace take Zarathustra literally and choose the "disgusting" goal of becoming the last men. This decision leaves Zarathustra disheartened and disappointed.

At the gate of the town the grave-diggers met him: they shone their
torch on his face, and, recognising Zarathustra, they sorely derided
him. “Zarathustra is carrying away the dead dog: a fine thing that
Zarathustra hath turned a grave-digger! For our hands are too cleanly
for that roast. Will Zarathustra steal the bite from the devil? Well
then, good luck to the repast! If only the devil is not a better thief
than Zarathustra!—he will steal them both, he will eat them both!” And
they laughed among themselves, and put their heads together.

Zarathustra made no answer thereto, but went on his way. When he had
gone on for two hours, past forests and swamps, he had heard too much of
the hungry howling of the wolves, and he himself became a-hungry. So he
halted at a lonely house in which a light was burning.

“Hunger attacketh me,” said Zarathustra, “like a robber.

But they received him
not.”

“But they received me not,” said the voluntary beggar, “thou knowest it,
forsooth. So I went at last to the animals and to those kine.”

“Then learnedst thou,” interrupted Zarathustra, “how much harder it is
to give properly than to take properly, and that bestowing well is an
ART—the last, subtlest master-art of kindness.”

“Especially nowadays,” answered the voluntary beggar: “at present, that
is to say, when everything low hath become rebellious and exclusive and
haughty in its manner—in the manner of the populace.

For the hour hath come, thou knowest it forsooth, for the great, evil,
long, slow mob-and-slave-insurrection: it extendeth and extendeth!

Now doth it provoke the lower classes, all benevolence and petty giving;
and the over-rich may be on their guard!

Whoever at present drip, like bulgy bottles out of all-too-small
necks:—of such bottles at present one willingly breaketh the necks.
answer:
차라투스트라가 군중에게 거부당한 이유는 그가 제시한 목표가 대중에게 거부감을 주었기 때문입니다. 니체에 따르면, 마지막 인간(the last man)은 현대 사회와 서구 문명이 스스로 설정한 목표입니다. 차라투스트라는 이 사회가 수동적인 안락함과 일상만을 추구하며 위험, 고통, 실망을 초래할 수 있는 모든 것을 회피하는, 매우 역겨운 목표를 대중에게 직면시켰습니다.

결과적으로 차라투스트라는 대중을 설득하여 위버멘쉬(Übermensch)를 추구하도록 유도하는 데 실패했습니다. 오히려 대중은 차라투스트라의 말을 문자 그대로 받아들여 '마지막 인간'이 되는 것을 선택했습니다. 이러한 대중의 반응은 차라투스트라에게 실망감과 좌절감을 안겨주었습니다.

요약하자면, 차라투스트라의 비판적이고 도전적인 메시지가 대중의 안일함과 순응적인 성향과 충돌하면서, 대중은 그를 거부하고 자신들의 현재 상태를 유지하는 길을 택했기 때문에 차라투스트라는 군중에게 거부당한 것입니다.

## LXIX. The Shadow

Scarcely however was the voluntary beggar gone in haste, and Zarathustra
again alone, when he heard behind him a new voice which called out:
“Stay! Zarathustra! Do wait! It is myself, forsooth, O Zarathustra,
myself, thy shadow!” But Zarathustra did not wait; for a sudden
irritation came over him on account of the crowd and the crowding in his
mountains. “Whither hath my lonesomeness gone?” spake he.

“It is verily becoming too much for me; these mountains swarm; my
kingdom is no longer of THIS world; I require new mountains.

My shadow calleth me? What matter about my shadow! Let it run after me!
I—run away from it.”

Thus spake Zarathustra to his heart and ran away. But the one behind
followed after him, so that immediately there were three runners,
one after the other—namely, foremost the voluntary beggar, then
Zarathustra, and thirdly, and hindmost, his shadow.
answer:
차라투스트라가 군중에게 거부당한 이유는 그가 제시하는 가치와 사상이 대중에게 이해받지 못하고 거부당했기 때문입니다. 참고 자료 2에 따르면, 사람들은 그에게 "우리에게 마지막 인간(last man)을 달라"고 요구하며 그를 '초인(Superman)'의 선물로 만들기를 원했습니다. 하지만 차라투스트라는 그들이 자신을 이해하지 못한다고 느꼈습니다.

그는 자신이 너무 오랫동안 산속에서 살았으며, 냇물과 나무에 귀 기울여 왔기 때문에, 이제는 목동처럼 그들에게 말하고 있다고 느꼈습니다. 사람들은 그를 차갑고 끔찍한 농담을 하는 자로 여기며 웃지만, 동시에 그를 미워합니다. 이는 그의 깊은 통찰과 새로운 사상이 대중의 단순하고 얄팍한 이해 수준을 넘어서기 때문에 발생하는 괴리감입니다.

결론적으로, 차라투스트라의 메시지는 대중의 현재 상태, 즉 '낮은 것들(everything low)'이 반항적이고 배타적이며 거만해지는 상황에 대한 깊은 성찰을 담고 있지만, 이러한 심오한 진실은 대중의 즉각적이고 피상적인 반응을 이끌어내지 못하고 오히려 거부당하게 된 것입니다.

chunks:
And if ye appertain to me, still it is
not as my right arm.

For he who himself standeth, like you, on sickly and tender legs,
wisheth above all to be TREATED INDULGENTLY, whether he be conscious of
it or hide it from himself.

My arms and my legs, however, I do not treat indulgently, I DO NOT TREAT
MY WARRIORS INDULGENTLY: how then could ye be fit for MY warfare?

With you I should spoil all my victories. And many of you would tumble
over if ye but heard the loud beating of my drums.

Moreover, ye are not sufficiently beautiful and well-born for me. I
require pure, smooth mirrors for my doctrines; on your surface even mine
own likeness is distorted.

On your shoulders presseth many a burden, many a recollection; many a
mischievous dwarf squatteth in your corners. There is concealed populace
also in you.

And though ye be high and of a higher type, much in you is crooked and
misshapen. There is no smith in the world that could hammer you right
and straight for me.

### Section 3

I pass through this people and let fall many words: but they know
neither how to take nor how to retain them.

They wonder why I came not to revile venery and vice; and verily, I came
not to warn against pickpockets either!

They wonder why I am not ready to abet and whet their wisdom: as if they
had not yet enough of wiseacres, whose voices grate on mine ear like
slate-pencils!

And when I call out: “Curse all the cowardly devils in you, that
would fain whimper and fold the hands and adore”—then do they shout:
“Zarathustra is godless.”

And especially do their teachers of submission shout this;—but
precisely in their ears do I love to cry: “Yea! I AM Zarathustra, the
godless!”

Those teachers of submission! Wherever there is aught puny, or sickly,
or scabby, there do they creep like lice; and only my disgust preventeth
me from cracking them.

Well!

His first two wives bore him three sons, Isat Vâstra, Urvatat Nara, and Hvare Chithra, and three daughters, Freni, Thriti, and Pouruchista. His third wife, Hvōvi, was childless.
answer:
차라투스트라가 군중에게 거부당한 이유는 그들의 부족함과 그가 추구하는 이상적인 가치와의 괴리에서 비롯됩니다. 그는 자신을 따르는 이들이 "병약하고 연약한 다리" 위에 서서 스스로를 관대하게 대우받기를 원한다고 보았지만, 자신은 자신의 전사들을 관대하게 대하지 않으므로 그들이 자신의 '전쟁'에 적합하지 않다고 판단했습니다. 또한, 군중들이 자신에게 필요한 순수하고 매끄러운 거울이 아니며, 그들의 모습에는 자신의 모습조차 왜곡되어 있다고 보았습니다.

더 나아가, 차라투스트라는 군중들 속에 숨겨진 많은 짐과 기억들, 그리고 "구부러지고 못생긴" 요소들이 존재한다고 보았습니다. 그는 자신에게 필요한 이상적인 형태를 갖추지 못했기 때문에, 군중들이 자신의 이상에 부합하지 않는다고 여겼습니다. 이는 그가 추구하는 강하고 순수한 이상(Asha*와 같은 질서)에 도달하지 못한 대중의 상태에 대한 그의 근본적인 거부감을 반영합니다.

ground_truth_en: The crowd rejects Zarathustra's teaching of the Übermensch and instead demands he give them the Last Man — they prefer comfortable mediocrity over the difficult task of self-overcoming.
chunks:
And if ye appertain to me, still it is
not as my right arm.

For he who himself standeth, like you, on sickly and tender legs,
wisheth above all to be TREATED INDULGENTLY, whether he be conscious of
it or hide it from himself.

My arms and my legs, however, I do not treat indulgently, I DO NOT TREAT
MY WARRIORS INDULGENTLY: how then could ye be fit for MY warfare?

With you I should spoil all my victories. And many of you would tumble
over if ye but heard the loud beating of my drums.

Moreover, ye are not sufficiently beautiful and well-born for me. I
require pure, smooth mirrors for my doctrines; on your surface even mine
own likeness is distorted.

On your shoulders presseth many a burden, many a recollection; many a
mischievous dwarf squatteth in your corners. There is concealed populace
also in you.

And though ye be high and of a higher type, much in you is crooked and
misshapen. There is no smith in the world that could hammer you right
and straight for me.

## Notable influence on modern Western culture


A sculpture of Zoroaster by Edward Clark Potter, representing ancient Persian judicial wisdom and dating to 1896, towers over the Appellate Division Courthouse of New York State at East 25th Street and Madison Avenue in Manhattan. A sculpture of Zoroaster is included among other prominent religious figures in a procession representing major faith traditions on the south side of Rockefeller Memorial Chapel at the University of Chicago. It features figures from Abraham to the Reformation, illustrating a historical continuum of religious thought that includes the likes of Zoroaster, Moses, Plato and others.

### Section 3

I pass through this people and let fall many words: but they know
neither how to take nor how to retain them.

They wonder why I came not to revile venery and vice; and verily, I came
not to warn against pickpockets either!

They wonder why I am not ready to abet and whet their wisdom: as if they
had not yet enough of wiseacres, whose voices grate on mine ear like
slate-pencils!

And when I call out: “Curse all the cowardly devils in you, that
would fain whimper and fold the hands and adore”—then do they shout:
“Zarathustra is godless.”

And especially do their teachers of submission shout this;—but
precisely in their ears do I love to cry: “Yea! I AM Zarathustra, the
godless!”

Those teachers of submission! Wherever there is aught puny, or sickly,
or scabby, there do they creep like lice; and only my disgust preventeth
me from cracking them.

Well!
answer:
차라투스트라가 군중에게 거부당한 이유는 그들이 자신의 기준에 미치지 못하기 때문입니다. 그는 자신을 '전사(warriors)'로 여기며, 자신과 같은 '병약하고 연약한 다리(sickly and tender legs)'를 가진 이들에게는 관대하게 대하지 않는다고 말합니다. 또한, 군중들이 자신의 '전쟁(warfare)'에 적합하지 않으며, 그의 사상에 필요한 '순수하고 매끄러운 거울(pure, smooth mirrors)'이 부족하다고 평가합니다.

더 나아가, 그는 군중들 안에 많은 짐과 기억, 그리고 '장난기 많은 난쟁이(mischievous dwarf)'들이 숨어 있으며, 비록 그들이 높은 유형일지라도 '구부러지고 찌그러진(crooked and misshapen)' 부분이 많다고 지적합니다. 이러한 불완전함 때문에 그는 그들을 받아들이지 않으며, 심지어 그들의 굴복을 가르치는 이들(teachers of submission)을 경멸합니다.

ground_truth_en: The crowd rejects Zarathustra's teaching of the Übermensch and instead demands he give them the Last Man — they prefer comfortable mediocrity over the difficult task of self-overcoming.

But they received him

“But they received me not,” said the voluntary beggar, “thou knowest it,
forsooth. So I went at last to the animals and to those kine.”

“Then learnedst thou,” interrupted Zarathustra, “how much harder it is
to give properly than to take properly, and that bestowing well is an
ART—the last, subtlest master-art of kindness.”

“Especially nowadays,” answered the voluntary beggar: “at present, that
is to say, when everything low hath become rebellious and exclusive and
haughty in its manner—in the manner of the populace.

For the hour hath come, thou knowest it forsooth, for the great, evil,
long, slow mob-and-slave-insurrection: it extendeth and extendeth!

Now doth it provoke the lower classes, all benevolence and petty giving;
and the over-rich may be on their guard!

Whoever at present drip, like bulgy bottles out of all-too-small
necks:—of such bottles at present one willingly breaketh the necks.

The crowd greets this speech with scorn and mockery, and the tightrope show begins, high above the audience. When the rope-dancer is halfway across, a clown comes up behind him, urging him to get out of the way. The clown then leaps over the rope-dancer, causing the latter to lose balance and plummet to the ground. The crowd scatters, but Zarathustra stays with the fatally injured tightrope performer and implies that he admires him for having chosen such a dangerous livelihood. Zarathustra later takes the corpse of the rope-dancer on his shoulders into the forest and lays it in a hollow tree. He decides that from this point on, he will no longer attempt to speak to the masses, but only to a few chosen disciples.

There follows a series of discourses in which Zarathustra overturns many of the precepts of Christian morality. He gathers a group of disciples, but ultimately abandons them, saying that he will not return until they have disowned him.

As in the sea hast thou lived in solitude, and it hath borne thee up.
Alas, wilt thou now go ashore? Alas, wilt thou again drag thy body
thyself?”

Zarathustra answered: “I love mankind.”

“Why,” said the saint, “did I go into the forest and the desert? Was it
not because I loved men far too well?

Now I love God: men, I do not love. Man is a thing too imperfect for me.
Love to man would be fatal to me.”

Zarathustra answered: “What spake I of love! I am bringing gifts unto
men.”

“Give them nothing,” said the saint. “Take rather part of their load,
and carry it along with them—that will be most agreeable unto them: if
only it be agreeable unto thee!

If, however, thou wilt give unto them, give them no more than an alms,
and let them also beg for it!”

“No,” replied Zarathustra, “I give no alms.
answer:
차라투스트라가 군중에게 거부당한 이유는 그가 대중과의 소통 방식을 근본적으로 바꾸었기 때문입니다. 그는 군중에게 베푸는 행위, 즉 '베풂(giving)'의 방식에 대해 논하며, 특히 현대 사회에서 대중의 태도가 거부적이고 이기적으로 변했음을 지적합니다. 그는 사람들이 자신들의 요구에만 집중하며 타인에게 베푸는 행위를 경멸하는 현상을 보았고, 이러한 상황에서 대중에게 직접적으로 말을 거는 것을 멈추기로 결정합니다.

이러한 변화는 그가 군중의 시선과 반응에 지쳤음을 시사합니다. 참고 자료에 따르면, 차라투스트라는 군중의 조롱과 비웃음 속에서 '줄타기 쇼(tightrope show)'와 같은 위험한 행위를 통해 대중에게 직접적으로 말하려는 시도를 멈추고, 대신 소수의 선택받은 제자들에게만 가르침을 전하기 시작합니다. 이는 대중의 평가나 수용에 얽매이지 않고, 자신만의 길을 걷고자 하는 그의 철학적 입장의 반영입니다.

결론적으로, 차라투스트라가 군중에게 거부당한 것은 그가 대중의 요구에 맞추어 행동하는 대신, 자신의 사상을 실현하기 위해 대중과의 관계를 단절하고 소수의 진정한 제자들과만 교류하는 방향으로 철학적 경로를 전환했기 때문이라고 볼 수 있습니다.

### Section 2

Then, however, did it come to pass that Zarathustra, astonished at such
merely roguish answers, jumped back to the door of his cave, and turning
towards all his guests, cried out with a strong voice:

“O ye wags, all of you, ye buffoons! Why do ye dissemble and disguise
yourselves before me!

How the hearts of all of you convulsed with delight and wickedness,
because ye had at last become again like little children—namely,
pious,—

—Because ye at last did again as children do—namely, prayed, folded
your hands and said ‘good God’!

But now leave, I pray you, THIS nursery, mine own cave, where to-day
all childishness is carried on. Cool down, here outside, your hot
child-wantonness and heart-tumult!

To be sure: except ye become as little children ye shall not enter into
THAT kingdom of heaven.” (And Zarathustra pointed aloft with his hands.)
answer:
제시된 자료만으로는 차라투스트라가 군중에게 구체적으로 거부당한 이유가 명확하게 설명되어 있지 않습니다. 하지만 자료들을 통해 그의 태도와 인간에 대한 관점을 통해 그 배경을 추론해 볼 수 있습니다.

첫째, 차라투스트라는 인간에 대한 사랑을 표현했지만, 이는 신에 대한 사랑과는 구별되는 것이었습니다. 그는 "나는 인간을 사랑한다"고 말했지만, "인간은 나에게 너무 불완전한 존재"라고 선을 그으며 인간에 대한 사랑이 자신에게는 치명적일 수 있음을 시사했습니다. 또한 그는 인간에게 '선물'을 가져다주겠다고 말하며, 자선(alms)을 베풀지 않겠다고 단호하게 거절했는데, 이는 인간의 요구에 휩쓸리거나 물질적인 방식으로 관계를 맺는 것을 거부했음을 보여줍니다.

둘째, 그는 당시의 사회적 분위기, 즉 "모든 낮은 것들이 반항적이고 배타적이며 거만해진" 상황을 인식하고 있었습니다. 그는 대중의 맹목적인 선의나 사소한 베풂이 오히려 위험해지고 있으며, 이러한 상황 속에서 자신만의 길을 모색하고 있음을 암시합니다. 따라서 군중의 맹목적인 반응이나 기대에 부응하지 않거나, 혹은 그들의 방식에 동조하지 않음으로써 거부당했을 가능성이 있습니다.

query: '사람은 짐승과 위버멘쉬 사이에 걸쳐진 줄'이라는 뜻은?
ground_truth_en: Humanity is a rope stretched between beast and Übermensch — a transition, not a destination; what is valuable in man is that he is a bridge, the act of crossing over and going under itself.
chunks:
### Section 4

Zarathustra, however, looked at the people and wondered. Then he spake
thus:

Man is a rope stretched between the animal and the Superman—a rope over
an abyss.

A dangerous crossing, a dangerous wayfaring, a dangerous looking-back, a
dangerous trembling and halting.

What is great in man is that he is a bridge and not a goal: what is
lovable in man is that he is an OVER-GOING and a DOWN-GOING.

I love those that know not how to live except as down-goers, for they
are the over-goers.

I love the great despisers, because they are the great adorers, and
arrows of longing for the other shore.

I love those who do not first seek a reason beyond the stars for going
down and being sacrifices, but sacrifice themselves to the earth, that
the earth of the Superman may hereafter arrive.

I love him who liveth in order to know, and seeketh to know in
order that the Superman may hereafter live. Thus seeketh he his own
down-going.

I love him who laboureth and inventeth, that he may build the house for
the Superman, and prepare for him earth, animal, and plant: for thus
seeketh he his own down-going.

I love him who loveth his virtue: for virtue is the will to down-going,
and an arrow of longing.

I love him who reserveth no share of spirit for himself, but wanteth to
be wholly the spirit of his virtue: thus walketh he as spirit over the
bridge.

I love him who maketh his virtue his inclination and destiny: thus, for
the sake of his virtue, he is willing to live on, or live no more.

I love him who desireth not too many virtues. One virtue is more of a
virtue than two, because it is more of a knot for one’s destiny to cling
to.

I love him whose soul is lavish, who wanteth no thanks and doth not give
back: for he always bestoweth, and desireth not to keep for himself.

I love him who is ashamed when the dice fall in his favour, and who then
asketh: “Am I a dishonest player?”—for he is willing to succumb.

I love him who scattereth golden words in advance of his deeds, and
always doeth more than he promiseth: for he seeketh his own down-going.

I love him who justifieth the future ones, and redeemeth the past ones:
for he is willing to succumb through the present ones.

I love him who chasteneth his God, because he loveth his God: for he
must succumb through the wrath of his God.

I love him whose soul is deep even in the wounding, and may succumb
through a small matter: thus goeth he willingly over the bridge.

I love him whose soul is so overfull that he forgetteth himself, and all
things are in him: thus all things become his down-going.

I love him who is of a free spirit and a free heart: thus is his
head only the bowels of his heart; his heart, however, causeth his
down-going.

I love all who are like heavy drops falling one by one out of the dark
cloud that lowereth over man: they herald the coming of the lightning,
and succumb as heralds.

Lo, I am a herald of the lightning, and a heavy drop out of the cloud:
the lightning, however, is the SUPERMAN.—

## Philosophy
### Übermensch

Another concept important to understanding Nietzsche is the Übermensch* (Superman). Writing about nihilism in Also Sprach Zarathustra*, Nietzsche introduced an Übermensch*. According to Laurence Lampert, "the death of God must be followed by a long twilight of piety and nihilism (II. 19; III. 8). Zarathustra's gift of the overman is given to mankind not aware of the problem to which the overman is the solution." Zarathustra presents the Übermensch* as the creator of new values, and he appears as a solution to the problem of the death of God and nihilism. The Übermensch* does not follow the morality of common people since that favours mediocrity but rises above the notion of good and evil and above the "herd". In this way Zarathustra proclaims his ultimate goal as the journey towards the state of the Übermensch*. He wants a kind of spiritual evolution of self-awareness and overcoming of traditional views on morality and justice that stem from the superstitious beliefs still deeply rooted or related to the notion of God and Christianity.

From Thus Spoke Zarathustra* (Zarathustra's Prologue; pp. 9–11):

*I teach you the Übermensch*. Man is something that shall be overcome. What have you done to overcome him? All beings so far have created something beyond themselves: and you want to be the ebb of that great tide, and would rather go back to the beast than overcome man? What is the ape to man? A laughing-stock or a painful embarrassment. And just the same shall man be to the Übermensch: a laughing-stock or a painful embarrassment. You have made your way from worm to man, and much within you is still worm. Once you were apes, and even yet man is more of an ape than any ape. Even the wisest among you is only a conflict and hybrid of plant and ghost. But do I bid you become ghosts or plants? Behold, I teach you the Übermensch! The Übermensch is the meaning of the earth. Let your will say: The Übermensch *shall be* the meaning of the earth... Man is a rope stretched between the animal and the Übermensch—a rope over an abyss... What is great in man is that he is a bridge and not a goal: what is lovable in man is that he is an *over-going* and a *going under*.

Zarathustra contrasts the Übermensch* with the last man of egalitarian modernity (the most obvious example being democracy), an alternative goal humanity might set for itself. The last man is possible only by mankind's having bred an apathetic creature who has no great passion or commitment, who is unable to dream, who merely earns his living and keeps warm. This concept appears only in Thus Spoke Zarathustra*, and is presented as a condition that would render the creation of the Übermensch* impossible.[*better source needed*]

## Synopsis
### First part

The book begins with a prologue that sets up many of the themes that will be explored throughout the work. Zarathustra is introduced as a hermit who has lived ten years on a mountain with his two companions, an eagle and a serpent. One morning – inspired by the sun, which he describes as happy only when it shines upon others – Zarathustra returns to his human community to share his wisdom. Upon descending the mountain, he encounters an old holy man living in the forest, who spends his days praising God. Zarathustra marvels that the man has not yet heard that "God is dead".

Arriving at the nearest town, Zarathustra addresses a crowd that has gathered to watch a tightrope walker. He tells them that mankind's goal must be to create something superior to itself – a new type of human, the Übermensch*. All men, he says, must be prepared to will their own destruction in order to bring the Übermensch* into being. The crowd greets this speech with scorn and mockery, and the tightrope show begins, high above the audience. When the rope-dancer is halfway across, a clown comes up behind him, urging him to get out of the way. The clown then leaps over the rope-dancer, causing the latter to lose balance and plummet to the ground. The crowd scatters, but Zarathustra stays with the fatally injured tightrope performer and implies that he admires him for having chosen such a dangerous livelihood. Zarathustra later takes the corpse of the rope-dancer on his shoulders into the forest and lays it in a hollow tree. He decides that from this point on, he will no longer attempt to speak to the masses, but only to a few chosen disciples.

There follows a series of discourses in which Zarathustra overturns many of the precepts of Christian morality. He gathers a group of disciples, but ultimately abandons them, saying that he will not return until they have disowned him.
answer:
'사람은 짐승과 위버멘쉬 사이에 걸쳐진 줄'이라는 구절은 인간의 본질과 그 존재의 역동성을 매우 상징적으로 표현합니다. 이는 인간이 단순히 짐승(animal)과 이상적인 초인(Übermensch, 위버멘쉬)이라는 두 극단 사이의 긴장된 연결고리, 즉 '줄(rope)' 위에 놓여 있음을 의미합니다.

이러한 '줄'은 위험하고 불안정한 상태를 나타내는데, 참고 자료에 따르면 이 줄은 '심연(abyss)' 위를 건너는 것이며, 이는 인간의 삶이 본질적으로 위험하고 어려운 도전을 수반함을 시사합니다. 인간은 짐승적인 본능과 초인적인 가능성 사이에서 끊임없이 균형을 잡고 나아가야 하는 과제를 안고 있습니다.

결론적으로, 이 비유는 인간이 정체되어 있지 않고, 짐승의 상태에서 벗어나 위버멘쉬라는 궁극적인 목표를 향해 나아가야 하는 끊임없는 '넘어감(over-going)'과 '내려감(down-going)'의 과정 속에 있음을 보여줍니다.

query: '사람은 짐승과 위버멘쉬 사이에 걸쳐진 줄'이라는 뜻은?
ground_truth_en: Humanity is a rope stretched between beast and Übermensch — a transition, not a destination; what is valuable in man is that he is a bridge, the act of crossing over and going under itself.
chunks:
### Section 4

Zarathustra, however, looked at the people and wondered. Then he spake
thus:

Man is a rope stretched between the animal and the Superman—a rope over
an abyss.

A dangerous crossing, a dangerous wayfaring, a dangerous looking-back, a
dangerous trembling and halting.

What is great in man is that he is a bridge and not a goal: what is
lovable in man is that he is an OVER-GOING and a DOWN-GOING.

I love those that know not how to live except as down-goers, for they
are the over-goers.

I love the great despisers, because they are the great adorers, and
arrows of longing for the other shore.

I love those who do not first seek a reason beyond the stars for going
down and being sacrifices, but sacrifice themselves to the earth, that
the earth of the Superman may hereafter arrive.

I love him who liveth in order to know, and seeketh to know in
order that the Superman may hereafter live. Thus seeketh he his own
down-going.

I love him who laboureth and inventeth, that he may build the house for
the Superman, and prepare for him earth, animal, and plant: for thus
seeketh he his own down-going.

I love him who loveth his virtue: for virtue is the will to down-going,
and an arrow of longing.

I love him who reserveth no share of spirit for himself, but wanteth to
be wholly the spirit of his virtue: thus walketh he as spirit over the
bridge.

I love him who maketh his virtue his inclination and destiny: thus, for
the sake of his virtue, he is willing to live on, or live no more.

I love him who desireth not too many virtues. One virtue is more of a
virtue than two, because it is more of a knot for one’s destiny to cling
to.

I love him whose soul is lavish, who wanteth no thanks and doth not give
back: for he always bestoweth, and desireth not to keep for himself.

I love him who is ashamed when the dice fall in his favour, and who then
asketh: “Am I a dishonest player?”—for he is willing to succumb.

I love him who scattereth golden words in advance of his deeds, and
always doeth more than he promiseth: for he seeketh his own down-going.

I love him who justifieth the future ones, and redeemeth the past ones:
for he is willing to succumb through the present ones.

I love him who chasteneth his God, because he loveth his God: for he
must succumb through the wrath of his God.

I love him whose soul is deep even in the wounding, and may succumb
through a small matter: thus goeth he willingly over the bridge.

I love him whose soul is so overfull that he forgetteth himself, and all
things are in him: thus all things become his down-going.

I love him who is of a free spirit and a free heart: thus is his
head only the bowels of his heart; his heart, however, causeth his
down-going.

I love all who are like heavy drops falling one by one out of the dark
cloud that lowereth over man: they herald the coming of the lightning,
and succumb as heralds.

Lo, I am a herald of the lightning, and a heavy drop out of the cloud:
the lightning, however, is the SUPERMAN.—

## 6. Reception and Influence

There has been considerable interest in German idealism in the last twenty years, as hostility waned in analytic philosophy, traditional assumptions faded in continental philosophy, and bridges were built between the two approaches. Philosophers like Richard Bernstein and Richard Rorty, inspired by Wilfrid Sellars, may be credited with re-introducing Hegel to analytic philosophy as an alternative to classical empiricism. Robert Pippin later defended a non-metaphysical Hegel, which has been a subject of intense debate, but which has also made Hegel relevant to contemporary debates about realism and anti-realism. More recently, Robert Brandom has championed the “normative” conception of rationality that he finds in Kant and Hegel, and which suggests that concepts function as rules regulating judgment rather than mere representations. Some, like Catherine Malabou, have even attempted to apply the insights of the German idealists to contemporary neuroscience. Finally, it would be remiss not to mention the extraordinary historical-philosophical scholarship, in both German and English, that has been produced on German idealism in recent years. The literature listed in the bibliography has not only enriched our understanding of German idealism with new editions, translations, and commentaries, it has also expanded the horizons of philosophical scholarship by identifying new problems and new solutions to problems arising in different traditions and contexts.

## Philosophy
### Übermensch

Another concept important to understanding Nietzsche is the Übermensch* (Superman). Writing about nihilism in Also Sprach Zarathustra*, Nietzsche introduced an Übermensch*. According to Laurence Lampert, "the death of God must be followed by a long twilight of piety and nihilism (II. 19; III. 8). Zarathustra's gift of the overman is given to mankind not aware of the problem to which the overman is the solution." Zarathustra presents the Übermensch* as the creator of new values, and he appears as a solution to the problem of the death of God and nihilism. The Übermensch* does not follow the morality of common people since that favours mediocrity but rises above the notion of good and evil and above the "herd". In this way Zarathustra proclaims his ultimate goal as the journey towards the state of the Übermensch*. He wants a kind of spiritual evolution of self-awareness and overcoming of traditional views on morality and justice that stem from the superstitious beliefs still deeply rooted or related to the notion of God and Christianity.

From Thus Spoke Zarathustra* (Zarathustra's Prologue; pp. 9–11):

*I teach you the Übermensch*. Man is something that shall be overcome. What have you done to overcome him? All beings so far have created something beyond themselves: and you want to be the ebb of that great tide, and would rather go back to the beast than overcome man? What is the ape to man? A laughing-stock or a painful embarrassment. And just the same shall man be to the Übermensch: a laughing-stock or a painful embarrassment. You have made your way from worm to man, and much within you is still worm. Once you were apes, and even yet man is more of an ape than any ape. Even the wisest among you is only a conflict and hybrid of plant and ghost. But do I bid you become ghosts or plants? Behold, I teach you the Übermensch! The Übermensch is the meaning of the earth. Let your will say: The Übermensch *shall be* the meaning of the earth... Man is a rope stretched between the animal and the Übermensch—a rope over an abyss... What is great in man is that he is a bridge and not a goal: what is lovable in man is that he is an *over-going* and a *going under*.

Zarathustra contrasts the Übermensch* with the last man of egalitarian modernity (the most obvious example being democracy), an alternative goal humanity might set for itself. The last man is possible only by mankind's having bred an apathetic creature who has no great passion or commitment, who is unable to dream, who merely earns his living and keeps warm. This concept appears only in Thus Spoke Zarathustra*, and is presented as a condition that would render the creation of the Übermensch* impossible.[*better source needed*]

'사람은 짐승과 위버멘쉬 사이에 걸쳐진 줄'이라는 구절은 인간의 존재론적 위치와 잠재력을 매우 상징적으로 표현합니다. 이는 인간이 단순히 짐승(animal)과 궁극적인 이상적 존재인 위버멘쉬(Übermensch, 초인) 사이를 잇는 매개체, 즉 '다리(bridge)'와 같다는 의미를 내포합니다.

참고 자료에 따르면, 인간은 짐승과 위버멘쉬 사이에 걸쳐진 줄(Man is a rope stretched between the animal and the Superman—a rope over an abyss)로 묘사됩니다. 이 줄은 인간이 현재의 낮은 상태(짐승)와 극복해야 할 궁극적인 목표(위버멘쉬) 사이의 간극, 즉 '심연(abyss)' 위에 놓여 있음을 보여줍니다. 따라서 인간의 역할은 이 두 극단 사이에서 끊임없이 긴장하고 나아가야 하는 과도기적 존재임을 시사합니다.

결론적으로, 이 비유는 인간이 정체되어 있지 않고, 짐승적인 본능을 넘어서 위버멘쉬라는 새로운 가치와 의미를 창조해내는 역동적인 과정 속에 놓여 있음을 강조합니다. 인간은 목표 그 자체가 아니라, 그 목표를 향해 나아가는 '과도(over-going)'와 '하강(down-going)'을 경험하는 존재라는 점이 핵심입니다.

## Synopsis
### First part

The book begins with a prologue that sets up many of the themes that will be explored throughout the work. Zarathustra is introduced as a hermit who has lived ten years on a mountain with his two companions, an eagle and a serpent. One morning – inspired by the sun, which he describes as happy only when it shines upon others – Zarathustra returns to his human community to share his wisdom. Upon descending the mountain, he encounters an old holy man living in the forest, who spends his days praising God. Zarathustra marvels that the man has not yet heard that "God is dead".

Arriving at the nearest town, Zarathustra addresses a crowd that has gathered to watch a tightrope walker. He tells them that mankind's goal must be to create something superior to itself – a new type of human, the Übermensch*. All men, he says, must be prepared to will their own destruction in order to bring the Übermensch* into being. The crowd greets this speech with scorn and mockery, and the tightrope show begins, high above the audience. When the rope-dancer is halfway across, a clown comes up behind him, urging him to get out of the way. The clown then leaps over the rope-dancer, causing the latter to lose balance and plummet to the ground. The crowd scatters, but Zarathustra stays with the fatally injured tightrope performer and implies that he admires him for having chosen such a dangerous livelihood. Zarathustra later takes the corpse of the rope-dancer on his shoulders into the forest and lays it in a hollow tree. He decides that from this point on, he will no longer attempt to speak to the masses, but only to a few chosen disciples.

There follows a series of discourses in which Zarathustra overturns many of the precepts of Christian morality. He gathers a group of disciples, but ultimately abandons them, saying that he will not return until they have disowned him.

ÜbermenschConcept in the philosophy of Friedrich Nietzsche The Übermensch*** (/ˈuːbərmɛnʃ/ OO-bər-mensh*, German: [ˈyːbɐmɛnʃ] ; literal translation"}]],"parts":[{"template":{"target":{"wt":"lit","href":"./Template:Lit"},"params":{"1":{"wt":"Superman"},"2":{"wt":"Beyond-Man"}},"i":0}}]}' id="mwEQ">lit. 'Superman' or 'Beyond-Man') is a concept in the philosophy of Friedrich Nietzsche. In his 1883 book, Thus Spoke Zarathustra* (German: Also sprach Zarathustra*), Nietzsche has his character Zarathustra posit the Übermensch* as a goal for humanity to set for itself. The Übermensch* represents a shift from otherworldly Christian values and manifests the grounded human ideal. Zarathustra proclaims the will of the Übermensch* to give meaning to life on planet Earth, and admonishes his audience to ignore those who promise other-worldly fulfillment. In 1909, Thomas Common translated the term to English as "Superman", following the terminology of George Bernard Shaw's 1903 stage play Man and Superman*. Walter Kaufmann blamed this translation for the widespread misidentification of Nietzsche's concept with the comic-book character Superman. Kaufmann and others preferred to translate Übermensch* as "overman". A translation like "superior humans" might better fit the concept of Nietzsche as he unfolds his narrative. Scholars continue to employ both terms, with some simply opting for the German word.

'사람은 짐승과 위버멘쉬 사이에 걸쳐진 줄'이라는 구절은 니체의 『차라투스트라는 이렇게 말했다』에서 인간의 존재와 궁극적인 목표를 비유적으로 설명하는 매우 중요한 개념입니다. 이 구절은 인간(Man)이 짐승(animal)과 위버멘쉬(Übermensch)라는 두 극단적인 존재 사이의 긴장된 연결고리, 즉 '줄(rope)' 위에 놓여 있음을 의미합니다.

이는 인간이 현재의 상태에서 벗어나 더 높은 차원의 존재로 나아가야 할 과제를 상징합니다. 짐승은 인간 내면에 남아있는 본능적이고 낮은 상태를, 위버멘쉬는 인간이 극복하고 도달해야 할 초월적이고 새로운 가치를 창조하는 이상적인 인간상입니다. 따라서 인간은 이 두 지점 사이에서 끊임없이 갈등하고 균형을 잡아야 하는 상태에 놓여 있으며, 진정한 인간됨은 이 두 경계를 넘어서려는 의지에서 비롯된다는 것을 보여줍니다.

결론적으로, 이 비유는 인간이 단순히 본능에 굴복하는 존재(짐승)에 머무르지 않고, 스스로의 의지를 통해 초월적인 가치를 창조하며 '위버멘쉬'가 되기 위해 끊임없이 노력하고 진화해야 하는 역동적인 과정을 나타냅니다. 인간은 목표 그 자체가 아니라, 짐승과 위버멘쉬 사이의 '다리(bridge)'와 같은 역할을 수행하며 그 사이를 연결하는 존재인 것입니다.

이 비유에서 '줄(rope)'은 인간을 묶어두는 매개체이자 동시에 극복해야 할 경계를 의미합니다. 인간은 짐승적인 본능과 생물학적 한계에 묶여 있는 동시에, 더 높은 가치와 새로운 의미를 추구하는 위버멘쉬의 가능성 사이에서 끊임없이 균형을 잡고 나아가야 하는 위치에 있습니다. 따라서 인간은 정적인 목표가 아니라, 이 두 지점 사이를 오가며 끊임없이 움직이고 극복해야 하는 '다리(bridge)'와 같은 존재로 묘사됩니다.

결론적으로, 이 구절은 인간이 스스로를 짐승의 상태에서 벗어나 위버멘쉬로 나아가기 위해 위험하고 용감한 '건너기(crossing)'의 여정을 감수해야 함을 강조합니다. 인간의 위대함은 최종 목적지가 아니라, 짐승과 초인 사이를 연결하는 '다리'로서의 역할, 즉 '넘어감(over-going)'과 '내려감(going under)'을 모두 경험하는 역동적인 과정에 있음을 보여줍니다.

query: '사람은 짐승과 위버멘쉬 사이에 걸쳐진 줄'이라는 뜻은?
ground_truth_en: Humanity is a rope stretched between beast and Übermensch — a transition, not a destination; what is valuable in man is that he is a bridge, the act of crossing over and going under itself.
chunks:
Behold, I teach you the Übermensch! The Übermensch is the meaning of the earth. Let your will say: The Übermensch *shall be* the meaning of the earth... Man is a rope stretched between the animal and the Übermensch—a rope over an abyss... What is great in man is that he is a bridge and not a goal: what is lovable in man is that he is an *over-going* and a *going under*.

Zarathustra contrasts the Übermensch* with the last man of egalitarian modernity (the most obvious example being democracy), an alternative goal humanity might set for itself. The last man is possible only by mankind's having bred an apathetic creature who has no great passion or commitment, who is unable to dream, who merely earns his living and keeps warm. This concept appears only in Thus Spoke Zarathustra*, and is presented as a condition that would render the creation of the Übermensch* impossible.

### Section 4

Zarathustra, however, looked at the people and wondered. Then he spake
thus:

Man is a rope stretched between the animal and the Superman—a rope over
an abyss.

A dangerous crossing, a dangerous wayfaring, a dangerous looking-back, a
dangerous trembling and halting.

What is great in man is that he is a bridge and not a goal: what is
lovable in man is that he is an OVER-GOING and a DOWN-GOING.

I love those that know not how to live except as down-goers, for they
are the over-goers.

I love the great despisers, because they are the great adorers, and
arrows of longing for the other shore.

I love those who do not first seek a reason beyond the stars for going
down and being sacrifices, but sacrifice themselves to the earth, that
the earth of the Superman may hereafter arrive.

I love him who liveth in order to know, and seeketh to know in
order that the Superman may hereafter live. Thus seeketh he his own
down-going.

From Thus Spoke Zarathustra* (Zarathustra's Prologue; pp. 9–11):

*I teach you the Übermensch*. Man is something that shall be overcome. What have you done to overcome him? All beings so far have created something beyond themselves: and you want to be the ebb of that great tide, and would rather go back to the beast than overcome man? What is the ape to man? A laughing-stock or a painful embarrassment. And just the same shall man be to the Übermensch: a laughing-stock or a painful embarrassment. You have made your way from worm to man, and much within you is still worm. Once you were apes, and even yet man is more of an ape than any ape. Even the wisest among you is only a conflict and hybrid of plant and ghost. But do I bid you become ghosts or plants? Behold, I teach you the Übermensch! The Übermensch is the meaning of the earth.
answer:
'사람은 짐승과 위버멘쉬 사이에 걸쳐진 줄'이라는 구절은 인간의 존재론적 위치와 그 역할에 대한 니체의 심오한 통찰을 담고 있습니다. 이는 인간이 단순히 짐승(animal)과 궁극적인 이상인 위버멘쉬(Übermensch) 사이의 매개체, 즉 '다리(bridge)'와 같은 존재임을 의미합니다.

이 비유는 인간이 짐승의 본능적인 상태에서 벗어나 위버멘쉬라는 초인적인 목표를 향해 나아가는 과정에 놓여 있음을 보여줍니다. 인간은 이 두 극단 사이에서 긴장하고 균형을 잡아야 하는 위험하고도 역동적인 상태에 있습니다.

따라서 인간의 위대함은 최종 목적지인 위버멘쉬 그 자체가 되는 것이 아니라, 그 목표를 향해 나아가는 '다리'로서의 역할에 있습니다. 인간은 이 간극을 건너는 과정, 즉 '오르내림(over-going and going under)'을 통해 자신의 가능성을 드러내게 됩니다.

## Notable influence on modern Western culture


A sculpture of Zoroaster by Edward Clark Potter, representing ancient Persian judicial wisdom and dating to 1896, towers over the Appellate Division Courthouse of New York State at East 25th Street and Madison Avenue in Manhattan. A sculpture of Zoroaster is included among other prominent religious figures in a procession representing major faith traditions on the south side of Rockefeller Memorial Chapel at the University of Chicago. It features figures from Abraham to the Reformation, illustrating a historical continuum of religious thought that includes the likes of Zoroaster, Moses, Plato and others.

### First part

The book begins with a prologue that sets up many of the themes that will be explored throughout the work. Zarathustra is introduced as a hermit who has lived ten years on a mountain with his two companions, an eagle and a serpent. One morning – inspired by the sun, which he describes as happy only when it shines upon others – Zarathustra returns to his human community to share his wisdom. Upon descending the mountain, he encounters an old holy man living in the forest, who spends his days praising God. Zarathustra marvels that the man has not yet heard that "God is dead".

Arriving at the nearest town, Zarathustra addresses a crowd that has gathered to watch a tightrope walker. He tells them that mankind's goal must be to create something superior to itself – a new type of human, the Übermensch*. All men, he says, must be prepared to will their own destruction in order to bring the Übermensch* into being. The crowd greets this speech with scorn and mockery, and the tightrope show begins, high above the audience.

결론적으로, 이 구절은 인간이 현재의 상태에 안주하지 않고, 짐승적인 한계를 넘어 위버멘쉬라는 새로운 가치를 향해 끊임없이 나아가야 하는 역동적인 존재임을 강조합니다. 인간의 위대함은 목표(goal)가 되는 것이 아니라, 짐승과 위버멘쉬를 잇는 '다리(bridge)'로서의 역할에 있다는 점을 보여줍니다.

결론적으로, 인간의 위대함은 목표(goal)가 되는 것이 아니라, 짐승의 상태에서 벗어나 위버멘쉬로 나아가기 위한 '과도(over-going)'이자 '하강(going under)'을 경험하는 '다리'로서의 역할에 있다는 것을 보여줍니다.

query: '사람은 짐승과 위버멘쉬 사이에 걸쳐진 줄'이라는 뜻은?
ground_truth_en: Humanity is a rope stretched between beast and Übermensch — a transition, not a destination; what is valuable in man is that he is a bridge, the act of crossing over and going under itself.
chunks:
Behold, I teach you the Übermensch! The Übermensch is the meaning of the earth. Let your will say: The Übermensch *shall be* the meaning of the earth... Man is a rope stretched between the animal and the Übermensch—a rope over an abyss... What is great in man is that he is a bridge and not a goal: what is lovable in man is that he is an *over-going* and a *going under*.

Zarathustra contrasts the Übermensch* with the last man of egalitarian modernity (the most obvious example being democracy), an alternative goal humanity might set for itself. The last man is possible only by mankind's having bred an apathetic creature who has no great passion or commitment, who is unable to dream, who merely earns his living and keeps warm. This concept appears only in Thus Spoke Zarathustra*, and is presented as a condition that would render the creation of the Übermensch* impossible.

## Notable influence on modern Western culture


A sculpture of Zoroaster by Edward Clark Potter, representing ancient Persian judicial wisdom and dating to 1896, towers over the Appellate Division Courthouse of New York State at East 25th Street and Madison Avenue in Manhattan. A sculpture of Zoroaster is included among other prominent religious figures in a procession representing major faith traditions on the south side of Rockefeller Memorial Chapel at the University of Chicago. It features figures from Abraham to the Reformation, illustrating a historical continuum of religious thought that includes the likes of Zoroaster, Moses, Plato and others.

### Section 4

Zarathustra, however, looked at the people and wondered. Then he spake
thus:

Man is a rope stretched between the animal and the Superman—a rope over
an abyss.

A dangerous crossing, a dangerous wayfaring, a dangerous looking-back, a
dangerous trembling and halting.

What is great in man is that he is a bridge and not a goal: what is
lovable in man is that he is an OVER-GOING and a DOWN-GOING.

I love those that know not how to live except as down-goers, for they
are the over-goers.

I love the great despisers, because they are the great adorers, and
arrows of longing for the other shore.

I love those who do not first seek a reason beyond the stars for going
down and being sacrifices, but sacrifice themselves to the earth, that
the earth of the Superman may hereafter arrive.

I love him who liveth in order to know, and seeketh to know in
order that the Superman may hereafter live. Thus seeketh he his own
down-going.
answer:
'사람은 짐승과 위버멘쉬 사이에 걸쳐진 줄'이라는 구절은 인간의 존재론적 위치와 그 역할에 대한 니체의 핵심적인 비유를 담고 있습니다. 이는 인간이 단순히 짐승(animal)과 궁극적인 이상인 위버멘쉬(Übermensch) 사이에서 놓여 있는 중개자, 즉 '다리(bridge)'와 같은 존재임을 의미합니다.

이 비유는 인간이 짐승적인 본능과 초인적인 이상 사이에서 끊임없이 긴장하고 균형을 잡아야 하는 불안정하고 위험한 상태에 있음을 시사합니다. 인간은 목표(goal)가 아니라 그 자체로 '다리'라는 점에서 위대하며, 이러한 중간적 위치 자체가 인간의 가치입니다.

결론적으로, 인간은 짐승의 영역에 머무르지 않고 위버멘쉬를 향해 나아가야 하는 과도기적 존재이며, 이 과정에서 발생하는 위험과 떨림(trembling and halting)이야말로 인간이 지녀야 할 역동성과 가치임을 나타냅니다.

### First part

The book begins with a prologue that sets up many of the themes that will be explored throughout the work. Zarathustra is introduced as a hermit who has lived ten years on a mountain with his two companions, an eagle and a serpent. One morning – inspired by the sun, which he describes as happy only when it shines upon others – Zarathustra returns to his human community to share his wisdom. Upon descending the mountain, he encounters an old holy man living in the forest, who spends his days praising God. Zarathustra marvels that the man has not yet heard that "God is dead".

Arriving at the nearest town, Zarathustra addresses a crowd that has gathered to watch a tightrope walker. He tells them that mankind's goal must be to create something superior to itself – a new type of human, the Übermensch*. All men, he says, must be prepared to will their own destruction in order to bring the Übermensch* into being. The crowd greets this speech with scorn and mockery, and the tightrope show begins, high above the audience.
answer:
'사람은 짐승과 위버멘쉬 사이에 걸쳐진 줄'이라는 구절은 인간의 존재론적 위치와 그 역할에 대한 니체의 핵심적인 비유를 담고 있습니다. 이는 인간이 단순히 짐승(animal)의 본능적인 영역에 머무르지도 않고, 궁극적인 이상인 위버멘쉬(Übermensch)의 영역에 도달하지도 못한 채 그 둘 사이에 놓여 있음을 의미합니다.

이 '줄(rope)'은 인간이 겪는 긴장감과 위험한 상태를 상징합니다. 인간은 짐승의 본능과 위버멘쉬라는 초인(Superman)의 이상 사이에서 아슬아슬하게 균형을 잡고 있는 존재이며, 이 과정은 '심연(abyss)' 위를 걷는 것과 같다고 묘사됩니다. 따라서 인간의 삶은 안정된 목표가 아니라 끊임없이 위험을 감수하며 나아가야 하는 역동적인 '다리(bridge)'의 역할에 있음을 시사합니다.

결론적으로, 이 비유는 인간이 현재의 상태에 만족하지 않고, 짐승적인 한계를 넘어서서 위버멘쉬라는 새로운 가치를 창조해내야 하는 과제를 안고 있음을 보여줍니다. 인간은 목적지 그 자체가 아니라, 그 목적지를 향해 나아가는 과정 자체, 즉 '넘어감(over-going)'과 '내려감(going under)'을 통해 가치를 지니는 존재라는 의미로 해석할 수 있습니다.
