answer:
'차라투스트라는 이렇게 말했다'의 구절에서 광대가 떨어진 사건은 매우 상징적인 의미를 지닙니다. 참고 자료 2에 따르면, 경쟁자(rival)가 승리하는 순간, 다른 인물이 극심한 충격이나 혼란을 겪으며 넘어지는 모습이 묘사됩니다. 이는 사회적 경쟁이나 기존 질서 속에서 발생하는 좌절, 패배, 혹은 기존 가치관의 붕괴를 상징할 수 있습니다.

이러한 사건은 혼돈(disorder)과 격변(storm)의 순간을 나타내며, 시장이나 사람들이 바다처럼 흩어지고 무질서해지는 상황과 비유됩니다. 이는 기존의 안정된 상태가 무너지고 새로운 변화가 시작되기 직전의 격렬한 혼란기를 의미합니다.

하지만 중요한 것은 차라투스트라(Zarathustra)의 태도입니다. 그는 이러한 혼란 속에서도 쓰러진 사람 곁에 꿇어앉아 있으며, "악마가 나를 걸리게 할 것이라고 오래전에 알았다"고 말합니다. 이는 그가 이러한 고통과 실패, 심지어 '악마'로 여겨질 수 있는 상황까지도 자신의 여정(journey)의 일부로 받아들이고 있음을 보여줍니다. 즉, 광대가 떨어진 사건은 파괴가 아닌, 더 높은 차원으로 나아가기 위한 필수적인 통과 의례(rite of passage)로 해석될 수 있습니다.

ground_truth_en: The marketplace symbolizes the realm of the crowd and the buffoons — a space of noise, flies, and mediocrity where genuine teaching cannot take root; Zarathustra learns he should not address the herd.
chunks:
### Section 1

When I came unto men for the first time, then did I commit the anchorite
folly, the great folly: I appeared on the market-place.

And when I spake unto all, I spake unto none. In the evening, however,
rope-dancers were my companions, and corpses; and I myself almost a
corpse.

With the new morning, however, there came unto me a new truth: then did
I learn to say: “Of what account to me are market-place and populace and
populace-noise and long populace-ears!”

Ye higher men, learn THIS from me: On the market-place no one believeth
in higher men. But if ye will speak there, very well! The populace,
however, blinketh: “We are all equal.”

“Ye higher men,”—so blinketh the populace—“there are no higher men, we
are all equal; man is man, before God—we are all equal!”

Before God!—Now, however, this God hath died. Before the populace,
however, we will not be equal. Ye higher men, away from the
market-place!

## 4. Freedom
### 4.2 Mediated Freedom

In *Being and Nothingness*, Sartre forwards an account of
"radical" or "absolute" freedom, an
unconditioned "freedom-in-consciousness" where we make or
create ourselves *ex nihilo*, through the sheer
"upsurge" of choice alone. But in the wake of Marxist
criticism in the 1940s and 1950s, his views changed; he realized that
this early account was far too abstract, interiorized, and influenced
by Cartesian
assumptions.[6]
It failed to engage the social, historical, and
material conditions that invariably limit and constrain our freedom. He
came to recognize that our choices and actions are always mediated by
the world, by the sociohistorical situation we've been thrown
into. He sees that the idea of radical, unconditioned freedom "is
nonsense. The truth is that existence 'is-in-society' as it
'is-in-the-world'" (Sartre 1952 [1963, 590]). Freedom
must be understood as "freedom-in-situation." It is true
that we are free to create ourselves, but it is also true that we are
already created by our situation. "Man," is best understood
as "a totally conditioned social being who does not render back
completely what his conditioning has given him" (Sartre 1972
[2008, 35]).

Sartre's Marxist inspired conception of situated or mediated
freedom is one that had already been forwarded and developed by
Beauvoir in her major treatises *The Second Sex* and *The
Coming of Age* and in her novels such as *The Blood of
Others* (1945 ) and *The Mandarins* (1954 ). The
view is also developed by her compatriot Merleau-Ponty. In
*Phenomenology of Perception*, for example, Merleau-Ponty makes
it clear that the options we choose to act on do not emerge out of
nothing. They are already embedded in a sociohistorical situation
"before any personal decision has been made." (1945 [1962,
449]) The ways in which we create or make ourselves, then, are always
circumscribed by the meanings of our situation. We are simultaneously
self-making and already made. "We exist in both ways at
once," writes Merleau-Ponty. "We choose the world, and the
world chooses us." (1945 [1962, 453–454]). As we will see in
section 6.3, the recognition of the extent to which freedom is mediated
by the material conditions of our situation opened existentialism to a
broader engagement with the social sphere and the structures of
oppression and violence that shape our experience and
self-understanding.

## XII. The Flies In The Market-Place

Flee, my friend, into thy solitude! I see thee deafened with the noise
of the great men, and stung all over with the stings of the little ones.

Admirably do forest and rock know how to be silent with thee. Resemble
again the tree which thou lovest, the broad-branched one—silently and
attentively it o’erhangeth the sea.

Where solitude endeth, there beginneth the market-place; and where the
market-place beginneth, there beginneth also the noise of the great
actors, and the buzzing of the poison-flies.

In the world even the best things are worthless without those who
represent them: those representers, the people call great men.

Little do the people understand what is great—that is to say, the
creating agency. But they have a taste for all representers and actors
of great things.

Around the devisers of new values revolveth the world:—invisibly it
revolveth. But around the actors revolve the people and the glory: such
is the course of things.

Spirit, hath the actor, but little conscience of the spirit. He
believeth always in that wherewith he maketh believe most strongly—in
HIMSELF!

To-morrow he hath a new belief, and the day after, one still newer. Sharp
perceptions hath he, like the people, and changeable humours.

To upset—that meaneth with him to prove. To drive mad—that meaneth
with him to convince. And blood is counted by him as the best of all
arguments.

A truth which only glideth into fine ears, he calleth falsehood and
trumpery. Verily, he believeth only in Gods that make a great noise in
the world!

Full of clattering buffoons is the market-place,—and the people glory
in their great men! These are for them the masters of the hour.

But the hour presseth them; so they press thee. And also from thee
they want Yea or Nay. Alas! thou wouldst set thy chair betwixt For and
Against?

On account of those absolute and impatient ones, be not jealous, thou
lover of truth! Never yet did truth cling to the arm of an absolute one.

On account of those abrupt ones, return into thy security: only in the
market-place is one assailed by Yea? or Nay?

Slow is the experience of all deep fountains: long have they to wait
until they know WHAT hath fallen into their depths.

Away from the market-place and from fame taketh place all that is great:
away from the market-place and from fame have ever dwelt the devisers of
new values.

Flee, my friend, into thy solitude: I see thee stung all over by the
poisonous flies. Flee thither, where a rough, strong breeze bloweth!

Flee into thy solitude! Thou hast lived too closely to the small and the
pitiable. Flee from their invisible vengeance! Towards thee they have
nothing but vengeance.

Raise no longer an arm against them! Innumerable are they, and it is not
thy lot to be a fly-flap.

Innumerable are the small and pitiable ones; and of many a proud
structure, rain-drops and weeds have been the ruin.

Thou art not stone; but already hast thou become hollow by the numerous
drops. Thou wilt yet break and burst by the numerous drops.

Exhausted I see thee, by poisonous flies; bleeding I see thee, and torn
at a hundred spots; and thy pride will not even upbraid.

Blood they would have from thee in all innocence; blood their bloodless
souls crave for—and they sting, therefore, in all innocence.

But thou, profound one, thou sufferest too profoundly even from small
wounds; and ere thou hadst recovered, the same poison-worm crawled over
thy hand.

Too proud art thou to kill these sweet-tooths. But take care lest it be
thy fate to suffer all their poisonous injustice!

They buzz around thee also with their praise: obtrusiveness, is their
praise. They want to be close to thy skin and thy blood.

They flatter thee, as one flattereth a God or devil; they whimper before
thee, as before a God or devil. What doth it come to! Flatterers are
they, and whimperers, and nothing more.

Often, also, do they show themselves to thee as amiable ones. But that
hath ever been the prudence of the cowardly. Yea! the cowardly are wise!

They think much about thee with their circumscribed souls—thou art
always suspected by them! Whatever is much thought about is at last
thought suspicious.

They punish thee for all thy virtues. They pardon thee in their inmost
hearts only—for thine errors.

Because thou art gentle and of upright character, thou sayest:
“Blameless are they for their small existence.” But their circumscribed
souls think: “Blamable is all great existence.”

Even when thou art gentle towards them, they still feel themselves
despised by thee; and they repay thy beneficence with secret
maleficence.

Thy silent pride is always counter to their taste; they rejoice if once
thou be humble enough to be frivolous.

What we recognise in a man, we also irritate in him. Therefore be on
your guard against the small ones!

In thy presence they feel themselves small, and their baseness gleameth
and gloweth against thee in invisible vengeance.

Sawest thou not how often they became dumb when thou approachedst them,
and how their energy left them like the smoke of an extinguishing fire?

Yea, my friend, the bad conscience art thou of thy neighbours; for they
are unworthy of thee. Therefore they hate thee, and would fain suck thy
blood.

Thy neighbours will always be poisonous flies; what is great in
thee—that itself must make them more poisonous, and always more
fly-like.

Flee, my friend, into thy solitude—and thither, where a rough strong
breeze bloweth. It is not thy lot to be a fly-flap.—

Thus spake Zarathustra.
answer:
'시장(market-place)'은 차라투스트라의 사상에서 매우 상징적인 공간으로 나타납니다. 참고 자료 3에 따르면, 시장은 '위대한 행위자들(great actors)'의 소음과 '독충(poison-flies)'의 윙윙거림이 시작되는 곳이며, 동시에 사람들이 '위대한 사람들(great men)'을 찬양하는 장소이기도 합니다.

이 공간은 '새로운 가치(new values)'를 만들어내는 존재들 주변으로 세상이 회전하는 곳이며, 사람들은 이 행위자들과 그들의 영광에 열광합니다. 하지만 차라투스트라는 이러한 시장의 소음과 군중 속에서 진정한 가치나 '위대한 것(great things)'을 인식하기 어렵다고 봅니다.

결론적으로, 시장은 대중의 환호와 칭송이 지배하는 곳이지만, 진정한 가치 창조자들(devisers of new values)은 이 혼잡함에서 멀리 떨어져 있으며, 진실을 추구하는 자들은 이 시장에서 벗어나 고독한 곳으로 피해야 한다고 역설하고 있습니다.

ground_truth_en: The marketplace symbolizes the realm of the crowd and the buffoons — a space of noise, flies, and mediocrity where genuine teaching cannot take root; Zarathustra learns he should not address the herd.
chunks:
### Section 1

When I came unto men for the first time, then did I commit the anchorite
folly, the great folly: I appeared on the market-place.

And when I spake unto all, I spake unto none. In the evening, however,
rope-dancers were my companions, and corpses; and I myself almost a
corpse.

With the new morning, however, there came unto me a new truth: then did
I learn to say: “Of what account to me are market-place and populace and
populace-noise and long populace-ears!”

Ye higher men, learn THIS from me: On the market-place no one believeth
in higher men. But if ye will speak there, very well! The populace,
however, blinketh: “We are all equal.”

“Ye higher men,”—so blinketh the populace—“there are no higher men, we
are all equal; man is man, before God—we are all equal!”

Before God!—Now, however, this God hath died. Before the populace,
however, we will not be equal. Ye higher men, away from the
market-place!

## 6. Reception and Influence

There has been considerable interest in German idealism in the last twenty years, as hostility waned in analytic philosophy, traditional assumptions faded in continental philosophy, and bridges were built between the two approaches. Philosophers like Richard Bernstein and Richard Rorty, inspired by Wilfrid Sellars, may be credited with re-introducing Hegel to analytic philosophy as an alternative to classical empiricism. Robert Pippin later defended a non-metaphysical Hegel, which has been a subject of intense debate, but which has also made Hegel relevant to contemporary debates about realism and anti-realism. More recently, Robert Brandom has championed the “normative” conception of rationality that he finds in Kant and Hegel, and which suggests that concepts function as rules regulating judgment rather than mere representations. Some, like Catherine Malabou, have even attempted to apply the insights of the German idealists to contemporary neuroscience. Finally, it would be remiss not to mention the extraordinary historical-philosophical scholarship, in both German and English, that has been produced on German idealism in recent years. The literature listed in the bibliography has not only enriched our understanding of German idealism with new editions, translations, and commentaries, it has also expanded the horizons of philosophical scholarship by identifying new problems and new solutions to problems arising in different traditions and contexts.

## 4. Freedom
### 4.2 Mediated Freedom

In *Being and Nothingness*, Sartre forwards an account of
"radical" or "absolute" freedom, an
unconditioned "freedom-in-consciousness" where we make or
create ourselves *ex nihilo*, through the sheer
"upsurge" of choice alone. But in the wake of Marxist
criticism in the 1940s and 1950s, his views changed; he realized that
this early account was far too abstract, interiorized, and influenced
by Cartesian
assumptions.[6]
It failed to engage the social, historical, and
material conditions that invariably limit and constrain our freedom. He
came to recognize that our choices and actions are always mediated by
the world, by the sociohistorical situation we've been thrown
into. He sees that the idea of radical, unconditioned freedom "is
nonsense. The truth is that existence 'is-in-society' as it
'is-in-the-world'" (Sartre 1952 [1963, 590]). Freedom
must be understood as "freedom-in-situation." It is true
that we are free to create ourselves, but it is also true that we are
already created by our situation. "Man," is best understood
as "a totally conditioned social being who does not render back
completely what his conditioning has given him" (Sartre 1972
[2008, 35]).

Sartre's Marxist inspired conception of situated or mediated
freedom is one that had already been forwarded and developed by
Beauvoir in her major treatises *The Second Sex* and *The
Coming of Age* and in her novels such as *The Blood of
Others* (1945 ) and *The Mandarins* (1954 ). The
view is also developed by her compatriot Merleau-Ponty. In
*Phenomenology of Perception*, for example, Merleau-Ponty makes
it clear that the options we choose to act on do not emerge out of
nothing. They are already embedded in a sociohistorical situation
"before any personal decision has been made." (1945 [1962,
449]) The ways in which we create or make ourselves, then, are always
circumscribed by the meanings of our situation. We are simultaneously
self-making and already made. "We exist in both ways at
once," writes Merleau-Ponty. "We choose the world, and the
world chooses us." (1945 [1962, 453–454]). As we will see in
section 6.3, the recognition of the extent to which freedom is mediated
by the material conditions of our situation opened existentialism to a
broader engagement with the social sphere and the structures of
oppression and violence that shape our experience and
self-understanding.
answer:
'시장(market-place)'은 니체가 『차라투스트라는 이렇게 말했다』에서 등장하는 중요한 공간으로, 화자가 처음으로 인간들에게 자신을 드러내고 소통을 시도하는 장소입니다. 처음에는 화자가 이 공간에 나타나지만, 사람들은 그를 인식하지 못합니다. 이는 '고차원적인 인간들(higher men)'에게는 시장에서 자신을 믿지 않는다는 현실을 보여줍니다.

이러한 시장의 모습은 인간들 사이의 위계와 가치 판단을 드러내는 장소이기도 합니다. 대중(populace)은 "우리는 모두 평등하다(We are all equal)"고 생각하지만, 화자는 "신 앞에서는(Before God)" 그들이 평등하지 않음을 지적하며, 시장이라는 공간을 통해 기존의 가치관과 평등주의에 도전하고 '고차원적인 인간들'을 구분 짓는 역할을 합니다.

결론적으로, 시장은 단순히 물건을 사고파는 공간을 넘어, 인간들이 서로를 평가하고, 가치에 대해 논쟁하며, 기존의 도덕적, 사회적 질서에 대해 의문을 제기하는 철학적 대립이 발생하는 현장이라고 할 수 있습니다.

ground_truth_en: The marketplace symbolizes the realm of the crowd and the buffoons — a space of noise, flies, and mediocrity where genuine teaching cannot take root; Zarathustra learns he should not address the herd.
chunks:
### Section 1

When I came unto men for the first time, then did I commit the anchorite
folly, the great folly: I appeared on the market-place.

And when I spake unto all, I spake unto none. In the evening, however,
rope-dancers were my companions, and corpses; and I myself almost a
corpse.

With the new morning, however, there came unto me a new truth: then did
I learn to say: “Of what account to me are market-place and populace and
populace-noise and long populace-ears!”

Ye higher men, learn THIS from me: On the market-place no one believeth
in higher men. But if ye will speak there, very well! The populace,
however, blinketh: “We are all equal.”

“Ye higher men,”—so blinketh the populace—“there are no higher men, we
are all equal; man is man, before God—we are all equal!”

Before God!—Now, however, this God hath died. Before the populace,
however, we will not be equal. Ye higher men, away from the
market-place!

## 4. Nietzsche's Lack of a Political Philosophy

Even in the early *Untimely Meditations*, this hostility is
already evident. So, for example, Nietzsche comments:

Every philosophy which believes that the problem of existence is
touched on, not to say solved, by a political event is a joke- and
pseudo-philosophy. Many states have been founded since the world
began; that is an old story. How should a political innovation suffice
to turn men once and for all into contented inhabitants of the earth?
[That people think the answer to existential questions might come from
politics shows] that we are experiencing the consequences of the
doctrine…that the state is the highest goal of mankind and that
a man has no higher duty than to serve the state: in which doctrine I
recognize a relapse not into paganism but into stupidity. It may be
that a man who sees his highest duty in serving the state really knows
no higher duties; but there are men and duties existing beyond this
— and one of the duties that seems, at least to me, to be higher
than serving the state demands that one destroys stupidity in every
form, and therefore in this form too. That is why I am concerned with
a species of man whose teleology extends somewhat beyond the welfare
of a state…, and with [this kind of man] only in relation to a
world which is again fairly independent of the welfare of a state,
that of culture. (U III:4)

The same, vaguely anarchistic attitude is apparent in *Thus Spoke
Zarathustra*, where Nietzsche calls the "state…the
coldest of all cold monsters" and remarks, aptly enough, that
"the state…whatever it says it lies…Everything
about it is false" (Z I:11). "Only where the state ends,
there begins the human being who is not superfluous" (Z I:11) Of
course, it is only the latter individual that *really*
interests Nietzsche. And who is that individual? The next section (Z
I:12) tells us: he is the one who values his "solitude,"
which is precisely what the "marketplace" of politics
violates, with its "showmen and actors of great [sic]
things." "Far from the market place and from fame happens
all that is great" (Z I:12): in other words, great things (and
great people) are to be found far from the realms of politics and
economics.

## XII. The Flies In The Market-Place

Flee, my friend, into thy solitude! I see thee deafened with the noise
of the great men, and stung all over with the stings of the little ones.

Admirably do forest and rock know how to be silent with thee. Resemble
again the tree which thou lovest, the broad-branched one—silently and
attentively it o’erhangeth the sea.

Where solitude endeth, there beginneth the market-place; and where the
market-place beginneth, there beginneth also the noise of the great
actors, and the buzzing of the poison-flies.

In the world even the best things are worthless without those who
represent them: those representers, the people call great men.

Little do the people understand what is great—that is to say, the
creating agency. But they have a taste for all representers and actors
of great things.

Around the devisers of new values revolveth the world:—invisibly it
revolveth. But around the actors revolve the people and the glory: such
is the course of things.

Spirit, hath the actor, but little conscience of the spirit. He
believeth always in that wherewith he maketh believe most strongly—in
HIMSELF!

To-morrow he hath a new belief, and the day after, one still newer. Sharp
perceptions hath he, like the people, and changeable humours.

To upset—that meaneth with him to prove. To drive mad—that meaneth
with him to convince. And blood is counted by him as the best of all
arguments.

A truth which only glideth into fine ears, he calleth falsehood and
trumpery. Verily, he believeth only in Gods that make a great noise in
the world!

Full of clattering buffoons is the market-place,—and the people glory
in their great men! These are for them the masters of the hour.

But the hour presseth them; so they press thee. And also from thee
they want Yea or Nay. Alas! thou wouldst set thy chair betwixt For and
Against?

On account of those absolute and impatient ones, be not jealous, thou
lover of truth! Never yet did truth cling to the arm of an absolute one.

On account of those abrupt ones, return into thy security: only in the
market-place is one assailed by Yea? or Nay?

Slow is the experience of all deep fountains: long have they to wait
until they know WHAT hath fallen into their depths.

Away from the market-place and from fame taketh place all that is great:
away from the market-place and from fame have ever dwelt the devisers of
new values.

Flee, my friend, into thy solitude: I see thee stung all over by the
poisonous flies. Flee thither, where a rough, strong breeze bloweth!

Flee into thy solitude! Thou hast lived too closely to the small and the
pitiable. Flee from their invisible vengeance! Towards thee they have
nothing but vengeance.

Raise no longer an arm against them! Innumerable are they, and it is not
thy lot to be a fly-flap.

Innumerable are the small and pitiable ones; and of many a proud
structure, rain-drops and weeds have been the ruin.

Thou art not stone; but already hast thou become hollow by the numerous
drops. Thou wilt yet break and burst by the numerous drops.

Exhausted I see thee, by poisonous flies; bleeding I see thee, and torn
at a hundred spots; and thy pride will not even upbraid.

Blood they would have from thee in all innocence; blood their bloodless
souls crave for—and they sting, therefore, in all innocence.

But thou, profound one, thou sufferest too profoundly even from small
wounds; and ere thou hadst recovered, the same poison-worm crawled over
thy hand.

Too proud art thou to kill these sweet-tooths. But take care lest it be
thy fate to suffer all their poisonous injustice!

They buzz around thee also with their praise: obtrusiveness, is their
praise. They want to be close to thy skin and thy blood.

They flatter thee, as one flattereth a God or devil; they whimper before
thee, as before a God or devil. What doth it come to! Flatterers are
they, and whimperers, and nothing more.

Often, also, do they show themselves to thee as amiable ones. But that
hath ever been the prudence of the cowardly. Yea! the cowardly are wise!

They think much about thee with their circumscribed souls—thou art
always suspected by them! Whatever is much thought about is at last
thought suspicious.

They punish thee for all thy virtues. They pardon thee in their inmost
hearts only—for thine errors.

Because thou art gentle and of upright character, thou sayest:
“Blameless are they for their small existence.” But their circumscribed
souls think: “Blamable is all great existence.”

Even when thou art gentle towards them, they still feel themselves
despised by thee; and they repay thy beneficence with secret
maleficence.

Thy silent pride is always counter to their taste; they rejoice if once
thou be humble enough to be frivolous.

What we recognise in a man, we also irritate in him. Therefore be on
your guard against the small ones!

In thy presence they feel themselves small, and their baseness gleameth
and gloweth against thee in invisible vengeance.

Sawest thou not how often they became dumb when thou approachedst them,
and how their energy left them like the smoke of an extinguishing fire?

Yea, my friend, the bad conscience art thou of thy neighbours; for they
are unworthy of thee. Therefore they hate thee, and would fain suck thy
blood.

Thy neighbours will always be poisonous flies; what is great in
thee—that itself must make them more poisonous, and always more
fly-like.

Flee, my friend, into thy solitude—and thither, where a rough strong
breeze bloweth. It is not thy lot to be a fly-flap.—

Thus spake Zarathustra.
answer:
'시장(market-place)'은 니체 철학에서 중요한 상징적 공간으로 나타납니다. 참고 자료에 따르면, 시장은 대중(populace)과 소음(populace-noise)이 지배하는 공간이며, 이곳에서는 '더 높은 인간(higher men)'들이 자신들의 가치를 증명하거나 인정받기 어렵습니다. 사람들은 시장에서 '우리는 모두 평등하다(We are all equal)'고 생각하며, 이는 '더 높은 인간'들이 자신들의 우월성을 주장하기 어려운 환경을 만듭니다.

더 나아가, 시장은 '위대한 행위자들(great actors)'과 그들을 숭배하는 대중이 모이는 장소이며, 이들은 '거대한 것들(great things)'을 대표하는 존재들입니다. 니체는 이러한 시장의 환경이 위대한 것들이 발생하는 장소와는 거리가 멀다고 보며, 진정한 가치와 새로운 가치(new values)는 정치나 경제의 영역이 아닌, 정치와 명성으로부터 멀리 떨어진 고독(solitude) 속에서 발생한다고 주장합니다.

They think much about thee with their circumscribed souls—thou art
always suspected by them! Whatever is much thought about is at last
thought suspicious.

They punish thee for all thy virtues. They pardon thee in their inmost
hearts only—for thine errors.

Because thou art gentle and of upright character, thou sayest:
“Blameless are they for their small existence.” But their circumscribed
souls think: “Blamable is all great existence.”

Even when thou art gentle towards them, they still feel themselves
despised by thee; and they repay thy beneficence with secret
maleficence.

Thy silent pride is always counter to their taste; they rejoice if once
thou be humble enough to be frivolous.

What we recognise in a man, we also irritate in him. Therefore be on
your guard against the small ones!

In thy presence they feel themselves small, and their baseness gleameth
and gloweth against thee in invisible vengeance.

Sawest thou not how often they became dumb when thou approachedst them,
and how their energy left them like the smoke of an extinguishing fire?

Yea, my friend, the bad conscience art thou of thy neighbours; for they
are unworthy of thee. Therefore they hate thee, and would fain suck thy
blood.

Thy neighbours will always be poisonous flies; what is great in
thee—that itself must make them more poisonous, and always more
fly-like.

Flee, my friend, into thy solitude—and thither, where a rough strong
breeze bloweth. It is not thy lot to be a fly-flap.—

Thus spake Zarathustra.
answer:
'시장(market-place)'은 니체의 사상에서 중요한 상징적 공간으로 나타납니다. 참고 자료에 따르면, 시장은 '대중(populace)'과 '대중의 소음(populace-noise)'이 존재하는 공간이며, 이곳에서는 사람들이 서로를 평등하게 여기며 "우리는 모두 같다(We are all equal)"고 생각하는 경향이 있습니다.

이러한 시장의 분위기는 '위대한 사람들(higher men)'에게는 부적합한 곳으로 묘사됩니다. 니체는 시장에서 '위대한 사람들'은 신뢰받지 못하며, 대중은 자신들이 평등하다고 여기기 때문에 그들에게는 위대한 존재로 인정받지 못한다고 지적합니다. 결국 시장은 '위대함(great things)'이나 진정한 가치가 발생하는 장소가 아니라, 오히려 '소음의 벌레(poison-flies)'와 '거짓말(falsehood)'이 가득한 곳으로 그려집니다.

따라서 니체에게 시장은 대중의 평등주의와 덧없는 명예, 그리고 진정한 가치 창조가 이루어지는 '고독(solitude)'과 대조되는 공간으로 이해됩니다. 진정한 가치와 위대함은 정치나 경제의 영역이 아닌, 시장과 명성으로부터 멀리 떨어진 곳에서 발견된다고 강조됩니다.

And who is that individual? The next section (Z
I:12) tells us: he is the one who values his "solitude,"
which is precisely what the "marketplace" of politics
violates, with its "showmen and actors of great [sic]
things." "Far from the market place and from fame happens
all that is great" (Z I:12): in other words, great things (and
great people) are to be found far from the realms of politics and
economics.

Passages like these seem to support the Anti-Politics View. On this
account, Nietzsche occasionally expresses views about political
matters, but, read in context, they do not add up to a theoretical
account of any of the questions of political philosophy. He is more
accurately read, in the end, as a kind of *esoteric moralist*,
i.e., someone who has views about human flourishing, views he wants to
communicate at least to a select few. "This book belongs to the
very few," he says of *The Antichrist*, though the point
holds more generally.

## XII. The Flies In The Market-Place

Flee, my friend, into thy solitude! I see thee deafened with the noise
of the great men, and stung all over with the stings of the little ones.

Admirably do forest and rock know how to be silent with thee. Resemble
again the tree which thou lovest, the broad-branched one—silently and
attentively it o’erhangeth the sea.

Where solitude endeth, there beginneth the market-place; and where the
market-place beginneth, there beginneth also the noise of the great
actors, and the buzzing of the poison-flies.

In the world even the best things are worthless without those who
represent them: those representers, the people call great men.

Little do the people understand what is great—that is to say, the
creating agency. But they have a taste for all representers and actors
of great things.

Around the devisers of new values revolveth the world:—invisibly it
revolveth. But around the actors revolve the people and the glory: such
is the course of things.

### Section 1

When I came unto men for the first time, then did I commit the anchorite
folly, the great folly: I appeared on the market-place.

And when I spake unto all, I spake unto none. In the evening, however,
rope-dancers were my companions, and corpses; and I myself almost a
corpse.

With the new morning, however, there came unto me a new truth: then did
I learn to say: “Of what account to me are market-place and populace and
populace-noise and long populace-ears!”

Ye higher men, learn THIS from me: On the market-place no one believeth
in higher men. But if ye will speak there, very well! The populace,
however, blinketh: “We are all equal.”

“Ye higher men,”—so blinketh the populace—“there are no higher men, we
are all equal; man is man, before God—we are all equal!”

Before God!—Now, however, this God hath died. Before the populace,
however, we will not be equal. Ye higher men, away from the
market-place!
answer:
'시장(marketplace)'은 니체의 철학에서 대중과 덧없는 가치들이 지배하는 공간으로 묘사됩니다. 참고 자료에 따르면, 시장은 '위대한 것들(great things)'이나 '위대한 사람들(great people)'이 발견되는 곳이 아니라, 오히려 그 반대라고 할 수 있습니다. 시장은 '쇼맨(showmen)'들과 '큰 것들의 배우들(actors of great things)'이 존재하는 곳이며, 이곳에서는 대중들이 숭배하고 주목하는 현상이 일어납니다.

참고 자료 2에서 언급하듯이, 시장은 '고대들의 소음(noise of the great actors)'과 '독이 든 파리들(poison-flies)'이 윙윙거리는 곳으로 비유됩니다. 이곳은 사람들이 '위대한 것들'을 대표하는 자들(representers)에게 열광하며, 세상이 그 배우들과 영광(glory)을 중심으로 회전하는 장소입니다.

결론적으로, 니체에게 시장은 진정한 가치나 위대함이 발생하는 곳이 아니라, 대중의 시선과 칭송을 얻기 위해 존재하며, '위대한 인간(higher men)'들이 멀리 떨어져 있어야 할 공간으로 그려집니다.

## Notable influence on modern Western culture


A sculpture of Zoroaster by Edward Clark Potter, representing ancient Persian judicial wisdom and dating to 1896, towers over the Appellate Division Courthouse of New York State at East 25th Street and Madison Avenue in Manhattan. A sculpture of Zoroaster is included among other prominent religious figures in a procession representing major faith traditions on the south side of Rockefeller Memorial Chapel at the University of Chicago. It features figures from Abraham to the Reformation, illustrating a historical continuum of religious thought that includes the likes of Zoroaster, Moses, Plato and others.

answer:
'시장(marketplace)'은 니체 철학에서 대중적이고 겉으로 드러나는 가치와 명성이 지배하는 공간을 상징합니다. 참고 자료 3에 따르면, 시장은 '고대인들의 정치와 경제'의 영역이며, '거대한 행위자들(great actors)'의 소음과 '독충(poison-flies)'의 윙윙거림이 존재하는 곳입니다.

이러한 시장 공간은 사람들이 좇는 것, 즉 '위대한 것(great things)'이 실제로 존재하는 곳이 아니라, 그 '대표자들(representers)'과 '행위자들(actors)'이 만들어내는 환상과 명성이 지배하는 장소입니다. 즉, 시장은 대중의 시선과 영광(glory)이 맴도는 곳이며, 진정한 가치 창조의 근원과는 거리가 먼 곳으로 묘사됩니다.

따라서 니체에게 있어 진정한 위대함은 이러한 정치적, 경제적 시장의 영역에서 멀리 떨어진 '고독(solitude)' 속에서 발견되어야 합니다. 시장은 대중의 관심과 칭송을 얻기 위해 존재하지만, 진정한 창조적 힘은 그 반대편, 즉 고독한 성찰과 자기 극복을 통해 발현된다고 볼 수 있습니다.

Spirit, hath the actor, but little conscience of the spirit. He
believeth always in that wherewith he maketh believe most strongly—in
HIMSELF!

To-morrow he hath a new belief, and the day after, one still newer. Sharp
perceptions hath he, like the people, and changeable humours.

To upset—that meaneth with him to prove. To drive mad—that meaneth
with him to convince. And blood is counted by him as the best of all
arguments.

A truth which only glideth into fine ears, he calleth falsehood and
trumpery. Verily, he believeth only in Gods that make a great noise in
the world!

Full of clattering buffoons is the market-place,—and the people glory
in their great men! These are for them the masters of the hour.

But the hour presseth them; so they press thee. And also from thee
they want Yea or Nay. Alas! thou wouldst set thy chair betwixt For and
Against?

however, blinketh: “We are all equal.”

“Ye higher men,”—so blinketh the populace—“there are no higher men, we
are all equal; man is man, before God—we are all equal!”

Before God!—Now, however, this God hath died. Before the populace,
however, we will not be equal. Ye higher men, away from the
market-place!
answer:
'시장(market-place)'은 니체의 사상에서 대립되는 두 가지 상반된 공간을 상징합니다. 첫째, 시장은 '위대한 인간들(great men)'의 소음과 그들이 만들어내는 현상들이 존재하는 공간입니다. 이곳은 사람들이 '위대한 것들(great things)'을 대표하는 사람들을 주목하고 찬양하는 장소이며, 사람들은 이러한 대표자들(representers)에게 열광합니다.

둘째, 시장은 고독(solitude)과 대조를 이루는 공간입니다. 고독은 소음과 잡음으로부터 벗어나 자연(숲이나 바위)과 같은 침묵 속에서 사유할 수 있는 공간을 의미합니다. 따라서 시장은 고독이 끝나는 지점에 도달하며, 그곳에서 '위대한 행위자들(great actors)'의 소음과 '해충(poison-flies)'의 윙윙거림이 시작되는 경계 지점이라고 볼 수 있습니다.

결론적으로, 시장은 대중과 숭배, 소음과 혼란, 그리고 '위대한 것'을 둘러싼 평가와 가치가 교차하는 역동적인 사회적 공간이며, 이는 고독한 사유의 공간과 명확히 구분되어야 할 영역입니다.

ground_truth_en: The marketplace symbolizes the realm of the crowd and the buffoons — a space of noise, flies, and mediocrity where genuine teaching cannot take root; Zarathustra learns he should not address the herd.
chunks:
## XII. The Flies In The Market-Place

Flee, my friend, into thy solitude! I see thee deafened with the noise
of the great men, and stung all over with the stings of the little ones.

Admirably do forest and rock know how to be silent with thee. Resemble
again the tree which thou lovest, the broad-branched one—silently and
attentively it o’erhangeth the sea.

Where solitude endeth, there beginneth the market-place; and where the
market-place beginneth, there beginneth also the noise of the great
actors, and the buzzing of the poison-flies.

In the world even the best things are worthless without those who
represent them: those representers, the people call great men.

Little do the people understand what is great—that is to say, the
creating agency. But they have a taste for all representers and actors
of great things.

Around the devisers of new values revolveth the world:—invisibly it
revolveth. But around the actors revolve the people and the glory: such
is the course of things.

## Notable influence on modern Western culture


A sculpture of Zoroaster by Edward Clark Potter, representing ancient Persian judicial wisdom and dating to 1896, towers over the Appellate Division Courthouse of New York State at East 25th Street and Madison Avenue in Manhattan. A sculpture of Zoroaster is included among other prominent religious figures in a procession representing major faith traditions on the south side of Rockefeller Memorial Chapel at the University of Chicago. It features figures from Abraham to the Reformation, illustrating a historical continuum of religious thought that includes the likes of Zoroaster, Moses, Plato and others.

But around the actors revolve the people and the glory: such
is the course of things.

Spirit, hath the actor, but little conscience of the spirit. He
believeth always in that wherewith he maketh believe most strongly—in
HIMSELF!

To-morrow he hath a new belief, and the day after, one still newer. Sharp
perceptions hath he, like the people, and changeable humours.

To upset—that meaneth with him to prove. To drive mad—that meaneth
with him to convince. And blood is counted by him as the best of all
arguments.

A truth which only glideth into fine ears, he calleth falsehood and
trumpery. Verily, he believeth only in Gods that make a great noise in
the world!

Full of clattering buffoons is the market-place,—and the people glory
in their great men! These are for them the masters of the hour.

But the hour presseth them; so they press thee. And also from thee
they want Yea or Nay. Alas! thou wouldst set thy chair betwixt For and
Against?
answer:
'시장(market-place)'은 니체의 사상에서 단순한 물리적 공간을 넘어선, 가치와 권력, 그리고 대중의 관심이 교차하는 역동적인 영역을 의미합니다. 참고 자료에 따르면, 시장은 고독과 고요함(solitude)이 끝나는 지점에 시작되며, 그곳에서 '위대한 행위자들(great actors)'의 소음과 '독(poison-flies)'의 윙윙거림이 발생합니다.

이러한 시장 공간은 새로운 가치를 창조하는 사람들(devisers of new values)을 중심으로 세상이 회전하는 곳이며, 사람들은 이러한 행위자들(actors)과 그들이 만들어내는 영광(glory)에 몰두합니다. 즉, 시장은 사람들이 주목하고 찬양하는 '위대한 사람들(great men)'이 활동하며, 그들의 영향력 아래에서 대중이 열광하는 장소입니다.

결론적으로, 시장은 사람들이 '위대한 사람들'을 통해 자신들의 믿음과 가치를 확인하고, 끊임없이 변화하는 믿음과 의견(changeable humours)에 휩쓸리는 곳이며, 그곳에서 사람들은 '예(Yea)' 또는 '아니오(Nay)'라는 선택을 강요받게 됩니다.

ground_truth_en: The marketplace symbolizes the realm of the crowd and the buffoons — a space of noise, flies, and mediocrity where genuine teaching cannot take root; Zarathustra learns he should not address the herd.
chunks:
And who is that individual? The next section (Z
I:12) tells us: he is the one who values his "solitude,"
which is precisely what the "marketplace" of politics
violates, with its "showmen and actors of great [sic]
things." "Far from the market place and from fame happens
all that is great" (Z I:12): in other words, great things (and
great people) are to be found far from the realms of politics and
economics.

Passages like these seem to support the Anti-Politics View. On this
account, Nietzsche occasionally expresses views about political
matters, but, read in context, they do not add up to a theoretical
account of any of the questions of political philosophy. He is more
accurately read, in the end, as a kind of *esoteric moralist*,
i.e., someone who has views about human flourishing, views he wants to
communicate at least to a select few. "This book belongs to the
very few," he says of *The Antichrist*, though the point
holds more generally.

## XII. The Flies In The Market-Place

Flee, my friend, into thy solitude! I see thee deafened with the noise
of the great men, and stung all over with the stings of the little ones.

Admirably do forest and rock know how to be silent with thee. Resemble
again the tree which thou lovest, the broad-branched one—silently and
attentively it o’erhangeth the sea.

market-place beginneth, there beginneth also the noise of the great

is the course of things.

But around the actors revolve the people and the glory: such
is the course of things.

Spirit, hath the actor, but little conscience of the spirit. He
believeth always in that wherewith he maketh believe most strongly—in
HIMSELF!

To-morrow he hath a new belief, and the day after, one still newer. Sharp
perceptions hath he, like the people, and changeable humours.

To upset—that meaneth with him to prove. To drive mad—that meaneth
with him to convince. And blood is counted by him as the best of all
arguments.

A truth which only glideth into fine ears, he calleth falsehood and
trumpery. Verily, he believeth only in Gods that make a great noise in
the world!

Full of clattering buffoons is the market-place,—and the people glory
in their great men! These are for them the masters of the hour.

But the hour presseth them; so they press thee. And also from thee
they want Yea or Nay. Alas! thou wouldst set thy chair betwixt For and
Against?
answer:
'시장(marketplace)'은 니체 철학에서 정치와 경제적 가치들이 지배하는 공간으로 묘사됩니다. 참고 자료에 따르면, 시장은 '쇼맨과 위대한 것들의 배우들(showmen and actors of great things)'이 존재하는 곳이며, 이곳은 진정한 위대한 것들이 발견되는 곳과는 거리가 멀다고 여겨집니다. 즉, 시장은 대중의 관심과 명성, 그리고 겉으로 드러나는 현상들이 지배하는 영역입니다.

이러한 시장의 공간은 '소외(solitude)'와 대조를 이룹니다. 참고 자료 2에서 언급하듯이, 고독(solitude)은 시장의 소음과 대중의 소란으로부터 벗어난 상태를 의미하며, 자연(숲과 바위)처럼 고요하고 집중할 수 있는 공간입니다. 반면, 시장은 끊임없이 움직이고 소란스러우며, '거짓말과 허황됨(falsehood and trumpery)'을 숭배하는 사람들의 소음으로 가득 차 있습니다.

결론적으로, 시장은 대중이 '위대한 사람들(great men)'을 숭배하고 그들의 명성에 열광하는 곳이며, 이곳에서 사람들은 끊임없이 새로운 믿음과 허황된 주장들을 쫓으며 시간의 압박 속에서 살아가게 됩니다.

이러한 시장의 공간은 '소외(solitude)'와 대조됩니다. 니체는 위대한 것들과 위대한 인물들은 정치나 경제의 영역과는 거리가 먼 곳에서 발견된다고 보았습니다. 반면, 시장은 사람들이 추구하는 명성과 영광(glory)을 중심으로 돌아가며, 사람들은 이러한 '위대한 인물들'을 그들의 시대의 지배자로 여기고 찬양합니다.

결론적으로, 시장은 대중이 쉽게 이해하고 소비하며 숭배하는 가치와 명성이 생성되고 순환하는 공간이며, 이는 진정한 위대함이나 창조적 행위(creating agency)가 발생하는 영역과는 분리되어 있다고 볼 수 있습니다.

ground_truth_en: The 'overgoers' (Übergehende) cross toward the Übermensch and the 'down-goers' (Untergehende) sacrifice themselves; both are loved by Zarathustra because going-under is necessary for going-over — destruction precedes creation.
chunks:
## Themes
### Übermensch*

It is allegedly "well-known that as a term, Nietzsche’s Übermensch derives from Lucian of Samosata's hyperanthropos". This hyperanthropos, or "overman," appears in Lucian's Menippean satire Κατάπλους ἢ Τύραννος*, usually translated Downward Journey or The Tyrant*. This hyperanthropos is "imagined to be superior to others of 'lesser' station in this-worldly life and the same tyrant after his (comically unwilling) transport into the underworld".
Nietzsche celebrated Goethe as an actualisation of the Übermensch.

## Philosophy
### Übermensch

Another concept important to understanding Nietzsche is the Übermensch* (Superman). Writing about nihilism in Also Sprach Zarathustra*, Nietzsche introduced an Übermensch*. According to Laurence Lampert, "the death of God must be followed by a long twilight of piety and nihilism (II. 19; III. 8). Zarathustra's gift of the overman is given to mankind not aware of the problem to which the overman is the solution." Zarathustra presents the Übermensch* as the creator of new values, and he appears as a solution to the problem of the death of God and nihilism. The Übermensch* does not follow the morality of common people since that favours mediocrity but rises above the notion of good and evil and above the "herd". In this way Zarathustra proclaims his ultimate goal as the journey towards the state of the Übermensch*. He wants a kind of spiritual evolution of self-awareness and overcoming of traditional views on morality and justice that stem from the superstitious beliefs still deeply rooted or related to the notion of God and Christianity.

From Thus Spoke Zarathustra* (Zarathustra's Prologue; pp. 9–11):

*I teach you the Übermensch*. Man is something that shall be overcome. What have you done to overcome him? All beings so far have created something beyond themselves: and you want to be the ebb of that great tide, and would rather go back to the beast than overcome man? What is the ape to man? A laughing-stock or a painful embarrassment. And just the same shall man be to the Übermensch: a laughing-stock or a painful embarrassment. You have made your way from worm to man, and much within you is still worm. Once you were apes, and even yet man is more of an ape than any ape. Even the wisest among you is only a conflict and hybrid of plant and ghost. But do I bid you become ghosts or plants? Behold, I teach you the Übermensch! The Übermensch is the meaning of the earth. Let your will say: The Übermensch *shall be* the meaning of the earth... Man is a rope stretched between the animal and the Übermensch—a rope over an abyss... What is great in man is that he is a bridge and not a goal: what is lovable in man is that he is an *over-going* and a *going under*.

Zarathustra contrasts the Übermensch* with the last man of egalitarian modernity (the most obvious example being democracy), an alternative goal humanity might set for itself. The last man is possible only by mankind's having bred an apathetic creature who has no great passion or commitment, who is unable to dream, who merely earns his living and keeps warm. This concept appears only in Thus Spoke Zarathustra*, and is presented as a condition that would render the creation of the Übermensch* impossible.[*better source needed*]

## 5. The Human Exemplar

How and why do nihilism and the pessimism of weakness prevail in modernity? Again, from the notebook of 1887 (*Will to Power, *aphorism 27), we find two conditions for this situation:

1. the higher species is lacking, i.e., those whose inexhaustible fertility and power keep up the faith in man….[and] 2. the lower species (‘herd,’ ‘mass,’ ‘society,’) unlearns modesty and blows up its needs into cosmic and metaphysical values. In this way the whole of existence is vulgarized: insofar as the mass is dominant it bullies the exceptions, so they lose their faith in themselves and become nihilists.

With the fulfillment of “European nihilism” (which is no doubt, for Nietzsche, endemic throughout the Western world and anyplace touched by “modernity”), and the death of otherworldly hopes for redemption, Nietzsche imagines two possible responses: the easy response, the way of the “herd” and “the last man,” or the difficult response, the way of the “exception,” and the *Übermensch. *

Ancillary to any discussion of the exception, *per se*, the compatibility of the *Übermensch *concept with other movements in Nietzsche’s thought, and even the significance that Nietzsche himself placed upon it, has been the subject of intense debate among Nietzsche scholars. The term’s appearance in Nietzsche’s corpus is limited primarily to *Thus Spoke Zarathustra* and works directly related to this text. Even here, moreover, the *Übermensch *is only briefly and very early announced in the narrative, albeit with a tremendous amount of fanfare, before fading from explicit consideration. In addition to these problems, there are debates concerning the basic nature of the *Übermensch *itself, whether “*Über-” *refers to a transitional movement or a transmogrified state of being, and whether Nietzsche envisioned the possibility of a community of *Übermenschen*, as opposed to a solitary figure among lesser types. So, what should be made of Nietzsche’s so-called “overman” (or even “superman”) called upon to arrive after the “death of God”?
answer:
'건너가는 자(Übergehende)'와 '몰락하는 자(Untergehende)'는 니체의 사상에서 인간의 존재 방식과 가치 창조의 방향성을 대조하는 핵심적인 개념입니다. '건너가는 자(Übergehende)'는 위버멘쉬(Übermensch, 초인)를 지향하는 존재로, 기존의 도덕이나 가치관을 넘어서 새로운 가치를 창조하고 스스로의 운명을 개척해 나가는 주체입니다. 이는 '신(God)의 죽음'과 '허무주의(nihilism)'라는 위기 속에서 새로운 의미를 부여하는 역할을 합니다.

반면, '몰락하는 자(Untergehende)'는 이러한 변화에 저항하거나 적응하지 못하고 기존의 가치 체계나 대중의 도덕에 순응하며 안주하는 존재를 의미합니다. 참고 자료에 따르면, '몰락하는 자'는 대중(herd)의 도덕을 따르거나, 열정이나 헌신이 없는 무기력한 '마지막 인간(last man)'의 상태와 연결됩니다. 이들은 스스로를 극복하지 못하고 기존의 틀에 머무르며, 결국 위버멘쉬의 탄생을 불가능하게 만드는 조건으로 제시됩니다.

결론적으로, 니체는 인간을 동물과 위버멘쉬 사이의 밧줄로 비유하며, 인간의 가치는 목표가 아니라 '넘어섬(over-going)'과 '아래로 내려감(going under)'의 과정 그 자체에 있다고 보았습니다. 즉, 위버멘쉬는 이 몰락의 과정을 극복하고 새로운 의미를 창조하는 존재이며, 몰락하는 자는 그 극복을 거부하고 안주하는 존재인 것입니다.

## 6. Reception and Influence

There has been considerable interest in German idealism in the last twenty years, as hostility waned in analytic philosophy, traditional assumptions faded in continental philosophy, and bridges were built between the two approaches. Philosophers like Richard Bernstein and Richard Rorty, inspired by Wilfrid Sellars, may be credited with re-introducing Hegel to analytic philosophy as an alternative to classical empiricism. Robert Pippin later defended a non-metaphysical Hegel, which has been a subject of intense debate, but which has also made Hegel relevant to contemporary debates about realism and anti-realism. More recently, Robert Brandom has championed the “normative” conception of rationality that he finds in Kant and Hegel, and which suggests that concepts function as rules regulating judgment rather than mere representations. Some, like Catherine Malabou, have even attempted to apply the insights of the German idealists to contemporary neuroscience. Finally, it would be remiss not to mention the extraordinary historical-philosophical scholarship, in both German and English, that has been produced on German idealism in recent years. The literature listed in the bibliography has not only enriched our understanding of German idealism with new editions, translations, and commentaries, it has also expanded the horizons of philosophical scholarship by identifying new problems and new solutions to problems arising in different traditions and contexts.

## Philosophy
### Übermensch

Another concept important to understanding Nietzsche is the Übermensch* (Superman). Writing about nihilism in Also Sprach Zarathustra*, Nietzsche introduced an Übermensch*. According to Laurence Lampert, "the death of God must be followed by a long twilight of piety and nihilism (II. 19; III. 8). Zarathustra's gift of the overman is given to mankind not aware of the problem to which the overman is the solution." Zarathustra presents the Übermensch* as the creator of new values, and he appears as a solution to the problem of the death of God and nihilism. The Übermensch* does not follow the morality of common people since that favours mediocrity but rises above the notion of good and evil and above the "herd". In this way Zarathustra proclaims his ultimate goal as the journey towards the state of the Übermensch*. He wants a kind of spiritual evolution of self-awareness and overcoming of traditional views on morality and justice that stem from the superstitious beliefs still deeply rooted or related to the notion of God and Christianity.

From Thus Spoke Zarathustra* (Zarathustra's Prologue; pp. 9–11):

*I teach you the Übermensch*. Man is something that shall be overcome. What have you done to overcome him? All beings so far have created something beyond themselves: and you want to be the ebb of that great tide, and would rather go back to the beast than overcome man? What is the ape to man? A laughing-stock or a painful embarrassment. And just the same shall man be to the Übermensch: a laughing-stock or a painful embarrassment. You have made your way from worm to man, and much within you is still worm. Once you were apes, and even yet man is more of an ape than any ape. Even the wisest among you is only a conflict and hybrid of plant and ghost. But do I bid you become ghosts or plants? Behold, I teach you the Übermensch! The Übermensch is the meaning of the earth. Let your will say: The Übermensch *shall be* the meaning of the earth... Man is a rope stretched between the animal and the Übermensch—a rope over an abyss... What is great in man is that he is a bridge and not a goal: what is lovable in man is that he is an *over-going* and a *going under*.

Zarathustra contrasts the Übermensch* with the last man of egalitarian modernity (the most obvious example being democracy), an alternative goal humanity might set for itself. The last man is possible only by mankind's having bred an apathetic creature who has no great passion or commitment, who is unable to dream, who merely earns his living and keeps warm. This concept appears only in Thus Spoke Zarathustra*, and is presented as a condition that would render the creation of the Übermensch* impossible.[*better source needed*]
answer:
'건너가는 자(Übergehende)'와 '몰락하는 자(Untergehende)'는 니체의 사상에서 인간의 존재 방식과 발전 단계를 대조하는 개념으로 이해할 수 있습니다. 참고 자료에 따르면, 인간은 동물(animal)과 '위버멘쉬(Übermensch)' 사이의 밧줄과 같은 존재로 묘사됩니다. 여기서 '건너가는 자(Übergehende)'는 이 밧줄을 넘어설 수 있는 잠재력이나 의지를 가진 존재를 의미하며, 이는 궁극적으로 '위버멘쉬(Übermensch)'로 나아가는 방향성을 내포합니다.

반면, '몰락하는 자(Untergehende)'는 이러한 초월적인 목표를 추구하지 않고, 오히려 짐승(beast)으로 돌아가거나 기존의 가치관에 안주하는 상태를 상징합니다. 니체는 인간이 스스로를 극복하고 더 높은 차원으로 나아가야 한다고 주장하며, 이러한 '몰락하는 자'의 상태는 대중적인 도덕이나 평범함(mediocrity)에 굴복하는 태도와 연결됩니다.

결론적으로, 니체에게 중요한 것은 인간이 단순히 생존하거나 기존의 틀에 머무르는 것이 아니라, 스스로를 극복하고 새로운 가치를 창조하며 '위버멘쉬(Übermensch)'가 되기 위해 '건너가는 자(Übergehende)'의 의지를 가져야 한다는 점입니다.

## Philosophy
### Apollonian and Dionysian

Nietzsche is adamant that the works of Aeschylus and Sophocles represent the apex of artistic creation, the true realisation of tragedy; it is with Euripides, that tragedy begins its Untergang* (literally 'going under' or 'downward-way;' meaning decline, deterioration, downfall, death, etc.). Nietzsche objects to Euripides' use of Socratic rationalism and morality in his tragedies, claiming that the infusion of ethics and reason robs tragedy of its foundation, namely the fragile balance of the Dionysian and Apollonian. Socrates emphasised reason to such a degree that he diffused the value of myth and suffering to human knowledge. Plato continued along this path in his dialogues, and the modern world eventually inherited reason at the expense of artistic impulses found in the Apollonian and Dionysian dichotomy. He notes that without the Apollonian, the Dionysian lacks the form and structure to make a coherent piece of art, and without the Dionysian, the Apollonian lacks the necessary vitality and passion. Only the fertile interplay of these two forces brought together as an art represented the best of Greek tragedy.

An example of the impact of this idea can be seen in the book Patterns of Culture*, where the anthropologist Ruth Benedict acknowledges Nietzschean opposites of "Apollonian" and "Dionysian" as the stimulus for her thoughts about Native American cultures. Carl Jung has written extensively on the dichotomy in Psychological Types*. Michel Foucault commented that his own book Madness and Civilization* should be read "under the sun of the great Nietzschean inquiry". Here Foucault referenced Nietzsche's description of the birth and death of tragedy and his explanation that the subsequent tragedy of the Western world was the refusal of the tragic and, with that, refusal of the sacred. The painter Mark Rothko was influenced by Nietzsche's view of tragedy presented in The Birth of Tragedy.*

## Nietzschean usage

In this state one enriches everything out of one's own fullness: whatever one sees, whatever wills is seen swelled, taut, strong, overloaded with strength. A man in this state transforms things until they mirror his power—until they are reflections of his perfection. This having to transform into perfection is—art.

### Section 3

The most careful ask to-day: “How is man to be maintained?” Zarathustra
however asketh, as the first and only one: “How is man to be SURPASSED?”

The Superman, I have at heart; THAT is the first and only thing to
me—and NOT man: not the neighbour, not the poorest, not the sorriest,
not the best.—

O my brethren, what I can love in man is that he is an over-going and a
down-going. And also in you there is much that maketh me love and hope.

In that ye have despised, ye higher men, that maketh me hope. For the
great despisers are the great reverers.

In that ye have despaired, there is much to honour. For ye have not
learned to submit yourselves, ye have not learned petty policy.

For to-day have the petty people become master: they all preach
submission and humility and policy and diligence and consideration and
the long et cetera of petty virtues.

Whatever is of the effeminate type, whatever originateth from the
servile type, and especially the populace-mishmash:—THAT wisheth now to
be master of all human destiny—O disgust! Disgust! Disgust!

THAT asketh and asketh and never tireth: “How is man to maintain himself
best, longest, most pleasantly?” Thereby—are they the masters of
to-day.

These masters of to-day—surpass them, O my brethren—these petty
people: THEY are the Superman’s greatest danger!

Surpass, ye higher men, the petty virtues, the petty policy, the
sand-grain considerateness, the ant-hill trumpery, the pitiable
comfortableness, the “happiness of the greatest number”—!

And rather despair than submit yourselves. And verily, I love you,
because ye know not to-day how to live, ye higher men! For thus do YE
live—best!
answer:
'건너가는 자(Übergehende)'와 '몰락하는 자(Untergehende)'는 니체의 사상에서 인간의 존재 양식을 설명하는 핵심적인 개념입니다. 참고 자료에 따르면, 니체는 인간을 '건너가는 자(Übergehende)'와 '몰락하는 자(Untergehende)'로 구분합니다. '건너가는 자'는 끊임없이 넘어서고 극복하려는 의지를 가진 존재를 의미하며, 이는 궁극적으로 '위버멘쉬(Übermensch)'의 이상과 연결됩니다.

반면, '몰락하는 자(Untergehende)'는 현재의 가치, 즉 '오늘날의 주인들'이 추구하는 사소한 미덕, 정책, 겸손, 근면함, 그리고 '최대 다수의 행복'과 같은 평범하고 덧없는 가치에 굴복하는 존재를 의미합니다. 니체는 이러한 '몰락하는 자'들이 현재의 세상을 지배하고 있으며, 이들이 바로 '위버멘쉬'에게 가장 큰 위험이 된다고 경고합니다.

결론적으로, 니체는 우리가 추구해야 할 것은 이러한 사소한 가치에 순응하는 것이 아니라, 끊임없이 자신을 넘어서고 극복하려는 '건너가는 자'의 정신을 갖추는 것이라고 주장합니다. 따라서 '건너가는 자'는 현재의 안락함이나 평범한 도덕률을 초월하여 스스로 가치를 창조하는 존재를 지향해야 함을 시사합니다.

### Section 4

Zarathustra, however, looked at the people and wondered. Then he spake
thus:

Man is a rope stretched between the animal and the Superman—a rope over
an abyss.

A dangerous crossing, a dangerous wayfaring, a dangerous looking-back, a
dangerous trembling and halting.

What is great in man is that he is a bridge and not a goal: what is
lovable in man is that he is an OVER-GOING and a DOWN-GOING.

I love those that know not how to live except as down-goers, for they
are the over-goers.

I love the great despisers, because they are the great adorers, and
arrows of longing for the other shore.

I love those who do not first seek a reason beyond the stars for going
down and being sacrifices, but sacrifice themselves to the earth, that
the earth of the Superman may hereafter arrive.

I love him who liveth in order to know, and seeketh to know in
order that the Superman may hereafter live. Thus seeketh he his own
down-going.

I love him who laboureth and inventeth, that he may build the house for
the Superman, and prepare for him earth, animal, and plant: for thus
seeketh he his own down-going.

I love him who loveth his virtue: for virtue is the will to down-going,
and an arrow of longing.

I love him who reserveth no share of spirit for himself, but wanteth to
be wholly the spirit of his virtue: thus walketh he as spirit over the
bridge.

I love him who maketh his virtue his inclination and destiny: thus, for
the sake of his virtue, he is willing to live on, or live no more.

I love him who desireth not too many virtues. One virtue is more of a
virtue than two, because it is more of a knot for one’s destiny to cling
to.

I love him whose soul is lavish, who wanteth no thanks and doth not give
back: for he always bestoweth, and desireth not to keep for himself.

I love him who is ashamed when the dice fall in his favour, and who then
asketh: “Am I a dishonest player?”—for he is willing to succumb.

I love him who scattereth golden words in advance of his deeds, and
always doeth more than he promiseth: for he seeketh his own down-going.

I love him who justifieth the future ones, and redeemeth the past ones:
for he is willing to succumb through the present ones.

I love him who chasteneth his God, because he loveth his God: for he
must succumb through the wrath of his God.

I love him whose soul is deep even in the wounding, and may succumb
through a small matter: thus goeth he willingly over the bridge.

I love him whose soul is so overfull that he forgetteth himself, and all
things are in him: thus all things become his down-going.

I love him who is of a free spirit and a free heart: thus is his
head only the bowels of his heart; his heart, however, causeth his
down-going.

I love all who are like heavy drops falling one by one out of the dark
cloud that lowereth over man: they herald the coming of the lightning,
and succumb as heralds.

Lo, I am a herald of the lightning, and a heavy drop out of the cloud:
the lightning, however, is the SUPERMAN.—

## Style

The nature of the text is musical and operatic. While working on it Nietzsche wrote "of his aim 'to become Wagner's heir'". Nietzsche thought of it as akin to a symphony or opera. Gustav Mahler, whose Third Symphony quotes a poem from the book, remarked that "Zarathustra* was born completely from the spirit of music, and is even 'symphonically constructed'". Nietzsche

"}},"i":0}}]}' id="mwAdA"/>

later draws special attention to "the tempo of Zarathustra's speeches" and their "delicate slowness" – "from an infinite fullness of light and depth of happiness drop falls after drop, word after word" – as well as the necessity of "*hearing* properly the tone that issues from his mouth, this halcyon tone".

The length of paragraphs and the punctuation and the repetitions all enhance the musicality.
The title is Thus Spoke Zarathustra*. Much of the book is what Zarathustra said. What Zarathustra says

Robert Pippin, \"Introduction\", in ''Thus Spoke Zarathustra'', trans. Adrian del Caro (Cambridge: Cambridge University Press. 2006)</ref>"}},"i":0}}]}' id="mwAd4"/>

is throughout so highly parabolic, metaphorical, and aphoristic. Rather than state various claims about virtues and the present age and religion and aspirations, Zarathustra speaks about stars, animals, trees, tarantulas, dreams, and so forth. Explanations and claims are almost always analogical and figurative.

Nietzsche would often appropriate masks and models to develop himself and his thoughts and ideas, and to find voices and names through which to communicate. While writing Zarathustra*, Nietzsche was particularly influenced by "the language of Luther and the poetic form of the Bible". But Zarathustra* also frequently alludes to or appropriates from Hölderlin's Hyperion* and Goethe's Faust* and Emerson's Essays*, among other things. It is generally agreed that the sorcerer is based on Wagner and the soothsayer is based on Schopenhauer.

The original text contains a great deal of word-play. For instance, words beginning with über* ('over, above') and unter* ('down, below') are often paired to emphasise the contrast, which is not always possible to bring out in translation, except by coinages. An example is untergang* (lit. 'down-going'), which is used in German to mean 'setting' (as in, of the sun), but also 'sinking', 'demise', 'downfall', or 'doom'. Nietzsche pairs this word with its opposite übergang* ('over-going'), used to mean 'transition'. Another example is übermensch* ('overman' or 'superman').

Nietzsche's style is figuratively and metaphorically rich with complex ideas that may let the reader find them hard to interpret. He does not follow a straightforward philosophical discourse; instead, he uses poetic, rhetorical narratives to convey Zarathustra's development of character. This richness of language can be ambiguous for readers to understand because it includes paradoxes, contradictions and parables, which requires "literary interpretations to unpack the language".
answer:
'건너가는 자(Übergehende)'와 '몰락하는 자(Untergehende)'는 차라투스트라의 사상에서 인간의 본질과 삶의 태도를 설명하는 핵심적인 대조 개념입니다. 차라투스트라는 인간을 "동물과 초인(Superman) 사이에 놓인 밧줄, 즉 심연 위의 밧줄"에 비유하며, 인간의 존재를 '건너가는 자'와 '몰락하는 자'라는 두 가지 움직임으로 설명합니다.

'건너가는 자(Übergehende)'는 이 밧줄 위를 넘어 나아가는 존재를 의미하며, 이는 진보, 극복, 그리고 미래를 향한 지향성을 나타냅니다. 차라투스트라는 이러한 '건너가는 자'를 사랑하는데, 그 이유는 그들이 '몰락하는 자(Down-going)'이기 때문이라고 말합니다. 즉, 현재의 상태나 기존의 질서에 얽매이지 않고 앞으로 나아가려는 역동적인 의지를 가진 자들을 긍정하는 것입니다.

반면, '몰락하는 자(Untergehende)'는 아래로 향하거나 하강하는 움직임을 나타냅니다. 이는 소멸, 희생, 혹은 현재의 상태에 침잠하는 것을 의미할 수 있습니다. 차라투스트라는 이러한 '몰락하는 자'들을 사랑하는데, 그들이 '건너가는 자'가 되기 위한 전제 조건이 되기 때문입니다. 이 둘의 역동적인 상호작용이야말로 인간이 초인(Superman)으로 나아가기 위한 과정, 즉 '다리(bridge)'로서의 인간의 역할을 보여줍니다.

### Übermensch*

It is allegedly "well-known that as a term, Nietzsche’s Übermensch derives from Lucian of Samosata's hyperanthropos". This hyperanthropos, or "overman," appears in Lucian's Menippean satire Κατάπλους ἢ Τύραννος*, usually translated Downward Journey or The Tyrant*. This hyperanthropos is "imagined to be superior to others of 'lesser' station in this-worldly life and the same tyrant after his (comically unwilling) transport into the underworld".
Nietzsche celebrated Goethe as an actualisation of the Übermensch.
answer:
'건너가는 자(Übergehende)'와 '몰락하는 자(Untergehende)'는 니체의 문체와 개념에서 대조를 이루는 중요한 개념으로 사용됩니다. 참고 자료에 따르면, 니체는 'über*' (위, 위에)와 'unter*' (아래, 아래에)로 시작하는 단어들을 짝지어 대조를 강조하는데, 이는 번역만으로는 완전히 전달하기 어려울 수 있는 미묘한 차이를 표현하기 위함입니다. 예를 들어, 'untergang*'는 문자적으로는 '내려가는 것'을 의미하지만, '몰락', '쇠퇴', '파멸', 또는 '죽음'과 같은 의미로 사용됩니다.

반면, 이와 대조되는 개념으로 'übergang*'가 제시되는데, 이는 '넘어가는 것' 또는 '전이(transition)'를 의미합니다. 이 두 단어의 대비는 삶의 긍정적인 변화나 초월적인 움직임을 암시하는 동시에, 'untergang*'가 나타내는 부정적인 종말이나 하강의 의미와 극명하게 대비됩니다.

이러한 대조는 니체가 제시하는 삶의 태도와 관련이 깊습니다. 즉, 긍정적이고 초월적인 'übergang*'을 지향하는 것이, 부정적이고 쇠퇴하는 'untergang*'에 빠지는 것과 대비되며, 이는 독자들에게 삶의 가치와 극복의 방향을 제시하는 수사적 장치로 기능합니다.

ground_truth_en: The 'overgoers' (Übergehende) cross toward the Übermensch and the 'down-goers' (Untergehende) sacrifice themselves; both are loved by Zarathustra because going-under is necessary for going-over — destruction precedes creation.
chunks:
It is generally agreed that the sorcerer is based on Wagner and the soothsayer is based on Schopenhauer.

The original text contains a great deal of word-play. For instance, words beginning with über* ('over, above') and unter* ('down, below') are often paired to emphasise the contrast, which is not always possible to bring out in translation, except by coinages. An example is untergang* (lit. 'down-going'), which is used in German to mean 'setting' (as in, of the sun), but also 'sinking', 'demise', 'downfall', or 'doom'. Nietzsche pairs this word with its opposite übergang* ('over-going'), used to mean 'transition'. Another example is übermensch* ('overman' or 'superman').

Nietzsche's style is figuratively and metaphorically rich with complex ideas that may let the reader find them hard to interpret. He does not follow a straightforward philosophical discourse; instead, he uses poetic, rhetorical narratives to convey Zarathustra's development of character.

## Notable influence on modern Western culture


A sculpture of Zoroaster by Edward Clark Potter, representing ancient Persian judicial wisdom and dating to 1896, towers over the Appellate Division Courthouse of New York State at East 25th Street and Madison Avenue in Manhattan. A sculpture of Zoroaster is included among other prominent religious figures in a procession representing major faith traditions on the south side of Rockefeller Memorial Chapel at the University of Chicago. It features figures from Abraham to the Reformation, illustrating a historical continuum of religious thought that includes the likes of Zoroaster, Moses, Plato and others.

A man in this state transforms things until they mirror his power—until they are reflections of his perfection. This having to transform into perfection is—art.

Nietzsche is adamant that the works of Aeschylus and Sophocles represent the apex of artistic creation, the true realisation of tragedy; it is with Euripides, that tragedy begins its *Untergang* (literally 'going under' or 'downward-way;' meaning decline, deterioration, downfall, death, etc.). Nietzsche objects to Euripides' use of Socratic rationalism and morality in his tragedies, claiming that the infusion of ethics and reason robs tragedy of its foundation, namely the fragile balance of the Dionysian and Apollonian. Socrates emphasised reason to such a degree that he diffused the value of myth and suffering to human knowledge. Plato continued along this path in his dialogues, and the modern world eventually inherited reason at the expense of artistic impulses found in the Apollonian and Dionysian dichotomy.

ground_truth_en: The 'overgoers' (Übergehende) cross toward the Übermensch and the 'down-goers' (Untergehende) sacrifice themselves; both are loved by Zarathustra because going-under is necessary for going-over — destruction precedes creation.
chunks:
### Section 4

Zarathustra, however, looked at the people and wondered. Then he spake
thus:

Man is a rope stretched between the animal and the Superman—a rope over
an abyss.

A dangerous crossing, a dangerous wayfaring, a dangerous looking-back, a
dangerous trembling and halting.

What is great in man is that he is a bridge and not a goal: what is
lovable in man is that he is an OVER-GOING and a DOWN-GOING.

I love those that know not how to live except as down-goers, for they
are the over-goers.

I love the great despisers, because they are the great adorers, and
arrows of longing for the other shore.

I love those who do not first seek a reason beyond the stars for going
down and being sacrifices, but sacrifice themselves to the earth, that
the earth of the Superman may hereafter arrive.

I love him who liveth in order to know, and seeketh to know in
order that the Superman may hereafter live. Thus seeketh he his own
down-going.

Behold, I teach you the Übermensch! The Übermensch is the meaning of the earth. Let your will say: The Übermensch *shall be* the meaning of the earth... Man is a rope stretched between the animal and the Übermensch—a rope over an abyss... What is great in man is that he is a bridge and not a goal: what is lovable in man is that he is an *over-going* and a *going under*.

Zarathustra contrasts the Übermensch* with the last man of egalitarian modernity (the most obvious example being democracy), an alternative goal humanity might set for itself. The last man is possible only by mankind's having bred an apathetic creature who has no great passion or commitment, who is unable to dream, who merely earns his living and keeps warm. This concept appears only in Thus Spoke Zarathustra*, and is presented as a condition that would render the creation of the Übermensch* impossible.

A man in this state transforms things until they mirror his power—until they are reflections of his perfection. This having to transform into perfection is—art.

Nietzsche objects to Euripides' use of Socratic rationalism and morality in his tragedies, claiming that the infusion of ethics and reason robs tragedy of its foundation, namely the fragile balance of the Dionysian and Apollonian. Socrates emphasised reason to such a degree that he diffused the value of myth and suffering to human knowledge. Plato continued along this path in his dialogues, and the modern world eventually inherited reason at the expense of artistic impulses found in the Apollonian and Dionysian dichotomy.

'건너가는 자(Übergehende)'는 이러한 위험한 경계를 넘어서려는 움직임, 즉 'OVER-GOING'을 의미합니다. 이는 끊임없이 앞으로 나아가고, 새로운 것을 추구하며, 초인(Übermensch)을 향해 나아가는 능동적인 의지를 상징합니다. 반면, '몰락하는 자(Untergehende)'는 'DOWN-GOING'으로, 자신을 희생하거나 땅에 자신을 바쳐 초인의 땅이 도래하기를 바라는 태도를 의미합니다. 이는 자기 파괴적이거나 혹은 현재의 상태를 내려놓고 어떤 근원적인 목적을 향해 하강하는 과정을 나타냅니다.

결론적으로, 니체에게 있어 인간의 가치 있는 측면은 목표(goal)가 되는 것이 아니라 다리(bridge)가 되는 데 있으며, 바로 이러한 '건너감(over-going)'과 '하강(going under)'이라는 양면적인 움직임 속에서 인간의 진정한 가치가 발현된다고 할 수 있습니다.

order that the Superman may hereafter live. Thus seeketh he his own
down-going.

Behold, I teach you the Übermensch! The Übermensch is the meaning of the earth. Let your will say: The Übermensch *shall be* the meaning of the earth... Man is a rope stretched between the animal and the Übermensch—a rope over an abyss... What is great in man is that he is a bridge and not a goal: what is lovable in man is that he is an *over-going* and a *going under*.

Zarathustra contrasts the Übermensch* with the last man of egalitarian modernity (the most obvious example being democracy), an alternative goal humanity might set for itself. The last man is possible only by mankind's having bred an apathetic creature who has no great passion or commitment, who is unable to dream, who merely earns his living and keeps warm. This concept appears only in Thus Spoke Zarathustra*, and is presented as a condition that would render the creation of the Übermensch* impossible.

A man in this state transforms things until they mirror his power—until they are reflections of his perfection. This having to transform into perfection is—art.

Nietzsche is adamant that the works of Aeschylus and Sophocles represent the apex of artistic creation, the true realisation of tragedy; it is with Euripides, that tragedy begins its *Untergang* (literally 'going under' or 'downward-way;' meaning decline, deterioration, downfall, death, etc.). Nietzsche objects to Euripides' use of Socratic rationalism and morality in his tragedies, claiming that the infusion of ethics and reason robs tragedy of its foundation, namely the fragile balance of the Dionysian and Apollonian. Socrates emphasised reason to such a degree that he diffused the value of myth and suffering to human knowledge. Plato continued along this path in his dialogues, and the modern world eventually inherited reason at the expense of artistic impulses found in the Apollonian and Dionysian dichotomy.
answer:
'건너가는 자(Übergehende)'와 '몰락하는 자(Untergehende)'는 인간 존재의 역동적인 양면성을 나타냅니다. 참고 자료에 따르면, 인간은 동물(animal)과 초인(Übermensch) 사이를 잇는 밧줄과 같으며, 이 밧줄은 심연(abyss) 위에 놓여 있어 위험한 행위임을 시사합니다.

'건너가는 자(Übergehende)'는 이러한 위험한 경계를 넘어서려는 움직임, 즉 앞으로 나아가려는 의지를 상징합니다. 반면, '몰락하는 자(Untergehende)'는 아래로 향하는 움직임, 즉 희생하거나 내려가는 행위를 의미합니다. 자라투스트라는 이러한 양면성 속에서 인간이 목표가 아니라 다리(bridge)라는 점을 강조하며, 인간의 가치 있는 모습이 바로 이러한 '건너감(over-going)'과 '내려감(going under)'에 있음을 보여줍니다.

결론적으로, 이 두 개념은 인간이 정체되어 머무는 것이 아니라, 끊임없이 상승하려는 의지와 동시에 현실에 자신을 바치거나 깊이 침잠하는 역동적인 과정을 통해 초인(Übermensch)을 향해 나아가는 존재 방식을 의미합니다.

ground_truth_en: The 'overgoers' (Übergehende) cross toward the Übermensch and the 'down-goers' (Untergehende) sacrifice themselves; both are loved by Zarathustra because going-under is necessary for going-over — destruction precedes creation.

It is generally agreed that the sorcerer is based on Wagner and the soothsayer is based on Schopenhauer.

The original text contains a great deal of word-play. For instance, words beginning with über* ('over, above') and unter* ('down, below') are often paired to emphasise the contrast, which is not always possible to bring out in translation, except by coinages. An example is untergang* (lit. 'down-going'), which is used in German to mean 'setting' (as in, of the sun), but also 'sinking', 'demise', 'downfall', or 'doom'. Nietzsche pairs this word with its opposite übergang* ('over-going'), used to mean 'transition'. Another example is übermensch* ('overman' or 'superman').

Nietzsche's style is figuratively and metaphorically rich with complex ideas that may let the reader find them hard to interpret. He does not follow a straightforward philosophical discourse; instead, he uses poetic, rhetorical narratives to convey Zarathustra's development of character.
answer:
'건너가는 자(Übergehende)'와 '몰락하는 자(Untergehende)'는 니체 철학에서 인간의 존재 방식과 관련하여 대조되는 개념으로 사용됩니다. 참고 자료에 따르면, 인간은 동물과 **위버멘쉬(Übermensch)** 사이를 잇는 밧줄과 같으며, 인간의 위대함은 목표(goal)가 되는 것이 아니라 다리(bridge)라는 점에 있습니다. 따라서 인간에게 사랑받는 것은 '건너가는 것(over-going)'과 '아래로 내려가는 것(going under)' 모두를 포함하는 존재 방식이라는 것입니다.

이러한 대조는 삶과 변화의 역동성을 나타냅니다. '건너가는 자(Übergehende)'는 긍정적이고 능동적인 '과정'이나 '전이(transition)'를 상징하며, 이는 자기 극복과 성장을 향한 움직임을 의미할 수 있습니다. 반면, '몰락하는 자(Untergehende)'는 쇠퇴, 몰락, 혹은 파멸('Untergang')과 같은 하강하는 과정을 나타냅니다.

결론적으로, 니체는 인간이 정체되어 안주하는 상태(무관심한 존재)와 대비하여, 끊임없이 변화하고 극복하며 나아가는 역동적인 존재 방식, 즉 '건너감'과 '몰락함'이라는 양면성을 통해 인간의 잠재력을 탐구하고 있습니다.
