ground_truth_en: Zarathustra's first teaching fails because he addressed the wrong audience — the marketplace crowd wants comfort, not self-overcoming; he learns he must seek companions, not preach to the herd.
chunks:
Zoroaster soon became aware of the existence of two primal spirits, the second being Angra Mainyu* (Destructive Spirit), with opposing concepts of Asha* (order) and Druj* (deception). Thus he decided to spend his life teaching people to seek Asha*. He received further revelations and saw a vision of the seven Amesha Spenta*, and his teachings were collected in the Gathas* and the Avesta*.

Eventually, at the age of about 42, Zoroaster received the patronage of queen Hutaosa and a ruler named Vishtaspa, an early adherent of Zoroastrianism (possibly from Bactria according to the Shahnameh).

According to the tradition, he lived for many years after Vishtaspa's conversion, managed to establish a faithful community, and married three times. His first two wives bore him three sons, Isat Vâstra, Urvatat Nara, and Hvare Chithra, and three daughters, Freni, Thriti, and Pouruchista. His third wife, Hvōvi, was childless.

Elisha's servant Gehazi in Jewish scripture). According to Ibn Kathir, Zoroaster came into conflict with Jeremiah which resulted in angry Jeremiah cast a curse upon Zoroaster, causing him to suffer Leprosy, and exiling him. Zoroaster later moved to a place of modern-day Azerbaijan which ruled by Bashtaasib (Vishtaspa), governor of Nebuchadnezzar, and spread his teaching of Zoroastrianism there. Bashtaasib then followed his teaching, forces the inhabitants of Persia to convert to Zoroastrianism and killed those who refused.

Ibn Kathir has quoted the original narrative was borrowed from Tabari's record of the "History of Jerusalem". He also mentioned that Zoroastrian was synonymous with Majus.

Sibt ibn al-Jawzi instead stated that some older narration said that Zoroaster was a former disciple of Uzair.

Al-Tabari (I, 681–683) recounts that Zaradusht accompanied a Jewish prophet to Bishtasb/Vishtaspa.

answer:
차라투스트라의 첫 가르침이 실패한 주요 원인은 그가 제시한 인간의 목표에 대한 대중의 반응 때문이었습니다. 차라투스트라는 인간의 목표는 자신보다 우월한 존재, 즉 **위버멘쉬(Übermensch)**를 창조하는 것이어야 한다고 주장했습니다. 그는 모든 인간이 위버멘쉬를 탄생시키기 위해 스스로의 파멸을 의지해야 한다고 말했습니다.

그러나 이러한 그의 급진적인 사상은 대중에게 즉각적인 수용을 얻지 못했습니다. 청중들은 그의 말을 조롱하고 비웃었으며, 이는 그의 가르침이 당대의 일반적인 사고방식이나 기대와 충돌했음을 보여줍니다. 즉, 인간의 본성과 사회적 관습에 익숙한 대중은 자신들의 현 상태를 넘어서는 극단적인 목표 설정에 대해 거부감을 드러낸 것입니다.

결론적으로, 차라투스트라의 가르침이 실패한 것은 그의 비전 자체가 너무나 혁명적이고 도전적이었기 때문이며, 이는 그가 제시한 '위버멘쉬'라는 이상향을 현실 세계에서 즉각적으로 받아들이기에는 대중의 준비 상태가 되지 않았음을 시사합니다.

## Notable influence on modern Western culture


A sculpture of Zoroaster by Edward Clark Potter, representing ancient Persian judicial wisdom and dating to 1896, towers over the Appellate Division Courthouse of New York State at East 25th Street and Madison Avenue in Manhattan. A sculpture of Zoroaster is included among other prominent religious figures in a procession representing major faith traditions on the south side of Rockefeller Memorial Chapel at the University of Chicago. It features figures from Abraham to the Reformation, illustrating a historical continuum of religious thought that includes the likes of Zoroaster, Moses, Plato and others.

answer:
제시된 참고 자료만으로는 차라투스트라의 첫 가르침이 구체적으로 '실패한 이유'가 무엇인지 명확하게 설명하고 있지는 않습니다. 자료들은 주로 조로아스터교의 신학적 배경, 그의 가르침의 내용(아샤, Asha), 그리고 그가 겪었던 갈등과 전파 과정에 초점을 맞추고 있습니다.

다만, 자료를 통해 유추할 수 있는 맥락은 다음과 같습니다. 조로아스터는 두 가지 근원적인 영적 존재, 즉 질서(Asha)와 기만(Druj)이라는 대립되는 개념을 인식한 후, 사람들에게 '아샤(Asha, 질서)'를 추구하도록 가르치는 길을 선택했습니다. 이러한 가르침이 성공적으로 받아들여지고 공동체를 형성하는 과정은 이후의 역사적 사건들과 연결되어 있습니다.

결론적으로, 자료는 그의 가르침의 실패 원인보다는, 그가 겪은 적대적인 반응(예: 예레미야와의 갈등으로 인한 저주와 추방)이나, 이후의 통치자(Bashtaasib)에 의한 강제적인 개종 시도 등 외부적 요인들을 제시하고 있습니다.

ground_truth_en: Zarathustra's first teaching fails because he addressed the wrong audience — the marketplace crowd wants comfort, not self-overcoming; he learns he must seek companions, not preach to the herd.
chunks:
## Appearance in Thus Spoke Zarathustra*


The last man's first appearance is in "Zarathustra's Prologue". According to Nietzsche, the last man is the goal that modern society and Western civilization have apparently set for themselves. After having unsuccessfully attempted to get the populace to accept the Übermensch* as the goal of society, Zarathustra confronts them with a goal so disgusting that he assumes that it will revolt them – a culture which seeks only passive comfort and routine, avoiding everything that could potentially bring risk, pain, or disappointment. Zarathustra fails in this attempt, and instead of repelling and manipulating the populace into pursuing the goal of the Übermensch, the populace take Zarathustra literally and choose the "disgusting" goal of becoming the last men. This decision leaves Zarathustra disheartened and disappointed.

Who still
wanteth to rule? Who still wanteth to obey? Both are too burdensome.

No shepherd, and one herd! Every one wanteth the same; every one is
equal: he who hath other sentiments goeth voluntarily into the madhouse.

“Formerly all the world was insane,”—say the subtlest of them, and
blink thereby.

They are clever and know all that hath happened: so there is
no end to their raillery. People still fall out, but are soon
reconciled—otherwise it spoileth their stomachs.

They have their little pleasures for the day, and their little pleasures
for the night, but they have a regard for health.

“We have discovered happiness,”—say the last men, and blink thereby.—

And here ended the first discourse of Zarathustra, which is also
called “The Prologue”: for at this point the shouting and mirth of the
multitude interrupted him. “Give us this last man, O Zarathustra,”—they
called out—“make us into these last men!

### Second part

Zarathustra retires to his mountain cave, and several years pass by. One night, he dreams that he looks into a mirror and sees the face of a devil instead of his own; he takes this as a sign that his doctrines are being distorted by his enemies, and joyfully descends the mountain to recover his lost disciples.

More discourses follow, which continue to develop the themes of the death of God and the rise of the Übermensch*, and also introduce the concept of the will to power. There are hints, however, that Zarathustra is holding something back. A series of dreams and visions prompt him to reveal this secret teaching, but he cannot bring himself to do so. He withdraws from his disciples once more, in order to perfect himself.
answer:
차라투스트라의 첫 가르침이 실패한 주된 이유는 현대 사회와 서구 문명이 스스로 목표로 삼은 '마지막 인간(the last man)'이라는 목표에 대한 대중의 반응 때문입니다. 차라투스트라는 사회가 수동적인 안락함과 일상만을 추구하며 위험, 고통, 실망을 피하려는 '역겨운' 목표를 제시했지만, 대중은 그를 물리치거나 조종하는 대신, 차라투스트라의 말을 문자 그대로 받아들여 '마지막 인간'이 되기를 선택했습니다.

이러한 결과는 차라투스트라에게 실망감과 낙담을 안겨주었습니다. 그는 대중을 위버멘쉬(Übermensch)로 이끌고자 했으나, 대중은 그 반대편에 있는 '마지막 인간'이라는 목표를 선택함으로써 그의 시도가 좌절된 것입니다. 참고 자료에 따르면, 대중은 통제와 평등을 추구하며, 모든 사람이 동일한 것을 원하기 때문에(every one wanteth the same), 차라투스트라의 도전적인 가르침을 거부하고 안락한 상태를 선택한 것입니다.

결론적으로, 차라투스트라의 첫 번째 담론(The Prologue)은 대중의 반응에 의해 중단되었습니다. 대중은 "이 마지막 인간을 우리에게 주라, 오 차라투스트라"라고 외치며 그를 마지막 인간으로 만들기를 요구했고, 이는 차라투스트라가 기대했던 위버멘쉬의 탄생과는 정반대의 결과로 나타났습니다.

ground_truth_en: Zarathustra's first teaching fails because he addressed the wrong audience — the marketplace crowd wants comfort, not self-overcoming; he learns he must seek companions, not preach to the herd.
chunks: In Thus Spoke Zarathustra* (1885), considered to be his seminal work, the philosopher Friedrich Nietzsche gives to the central character the native Iranian name "Zarathustra". Nietzsche explained the choice of the name in his autobiographical work Ecce Homo*: "Zarathustra was the first to see in the struggle of good and evil the true driving-wheel in the machinery of things [...] Zarathustra created* the disastrous error that is morality: thus he must also be the first to acknowledge* the mistake." Though the naming of Zarathustra is an acknowledgement of the lineage of moralism, Nietzsche advocates atheism and moral nihilism, directly contradicting the philosophy of Zoroaster, and makes subversive allusions to Zoroastrianism. Despite Nietzsche's iconoclasm, some admirers of pre-Islamic Persian culture see his use of the name "Zarathustra" as a mark of uncritical respect towards the historical figure.

called out Zarathustra, long have thy speech and thy
species disgusted me!

Why didst thou live so long by the swamp, that thou thyself hadst to
become a frog and a toad?

Floweth there not a tainted, frothy, swamp-blood in thine own veins,
when thou hast thus learned to croak and revile?

Why wentest thou not into the forest? Or why didst thou not till the
ground? Is the sea not full of green islands?

I despise thy contempt; and when thou warnedst me—why didst thou not
warn thyself?

Out of love alone shall my contempt and my warning bird take wing; but
not out of the swamp!—

They call thee mine ape, thou foaming fool: but I call thee my
grunting-pig,—by thy grunting, thou spoilest even my praise of folly.

What was it that first made thee grunt? Because no one sufficiently
FLATTERED thee:—therefore didst thou seat thyself beside this filth,
that thou mightest have cause for much grunting,—

—That thou mightest have cause for much VENGEANCE!

### First part

The book begins with a prologue that sets up many of the themes that will be explored throughout the work. Zarathustra is introduced as a hermit who has lived ten years on a mountain with his two companions, an eagle and a serpent. One morning – inspired by the sun, which he describes as happy only when it shines upon others – Zarathustra returns to his human community to share his wisdom. Upon descending the mountain, he encounters an old holy man living in the forest, who spends his days praising God. Zarathustra marvels that the man has not yet heard that "God is dead".

Arriving at the nearest town, Zarathustra addresses a crowd that has gathered to watch a tightrope walker. He tells them that mankind's goal must be to create something superior to itself – a new type of human, the Übermensch*. All men, he says, must be prepared to will their own destruction in order to bring the Übermensch* into being. The crowd greets this speech with scorn and mockery, and the tightrope show begins, high above the audience.
answer:
차라투스트라의 첫 가르침이 실패한 주된 이유는 그가 제시한 인간의 목표, 즉 자신보다 우월한 존재를 창조해야 한다는 사상이 대중에게 거부당했기 때문입니다. 차라투스트라는 인간의 목표가 스스로를 초월하는 새로운 인간 유형인 **위버멘쉬(Übermensch)**를 창조하는 것이어야 한다고 주장했습니다.

하지만 이 주장에 대해 군중은 조롱과 비웃음으로 반응했으며, 이는 그의 가르침이 당대의 일반적인 가치관이나 인간의 욕망과 충돌했음을 보여줍니다. 즉, 기존의 도덕이나 안주하려는 태도에 익숙했던 대중들은 자신들의 현 상태를 넘어서려는 급진적인 요구를 받아들이지 않았습니다.

결과적으로, 차라투스트라가 제시한 '자기 극복을 통한 위버멘쉬의 창조'라는 비전은 대중의 냉소적인 반응에 직면하며 그 영향력을 얻지 못하고 실패하게 됩니다.

ground_truth_en: Zarathustra's first teaching fails because he addressed the wrong audience — the marketplace crowd wants comfort, not self-overcoming; he learns he must seek companions, not preach to the herd.
chunks:
Zoroaster soon became aware of the existence of two primal spirits, the second being Angra Mainyu* (Destructive Spirit), with opposing concepts of Asha* (order) and Druj* (deception). Thus he decided to spend his life teaching people to seek Asha*. He received further revelations and saw a vision of the seven Amesha Spenta*, and his teachings were collected in the Gathas* and the Avesta*.

Eventually, at the age of about 42, Zoroaster received the patronage of queen Hutaosa and a ruler named Vishtaspa, an early adherent of Zoroastrianism (possibly from Bactria according to the Shahnameh).

According to the tradition, he lived for many years after Vishtaspa's conversion, managed to establish a faithful community, and married three times. His first two wives bore him three sons, Isat Vâstra, Urvatat Nara, and Hvare Chithra, and three daughters, Freni, Thriti, and Pouruchista. His third wife, Hvōvi, was childless.

Verily, all too well do I understand the dream’s portent and monition:
my DOCTRINE is in danger; tares want to be called wheat!

Mine enemies have grown powerful and have disfigured the likeness of
my doctrine, so that my dearest ones have to blush for the gifts that I
gave them.

Lost are my friends; the hour hath come for me to seek my lost ones!—

With these words Zarathustra started up, not however like a person in
anguish seeking relief, but rather like a seer and a singer whom the
spirit inspireth. With amazement did his eagle and serpent gaze upon
him: for a coming bliss overspread his countenance like the rosy dawn.

What hath happened unto me, mine animals?—said Zarathustra. Am I not
transformed? Hath not bliss come unto me like a whirlwind?

Foolish is my happiness, and foolish things will it speak: it is still
too young—so have patience with it!

Wounded am I by my happiness: all sufferers shall be physicians unto me!

To my friends can I again go down, and also to mine enemies!

### Section 3

I pass through this people and let fall many words: but they know
neither how to take nor how to retain them.

They wonder why I came not to revile venery and vice; and verily, I came
not to warn against pickpockets either!

They wonder why I am not ready to abet and whet their wisdom: as if they
had not yet enough of wiseacres, whose voices grate on mine ear like
slate-pencils!

And when I call out: “Curse all the cowardly devils in you, that
would fain whimper and fold the hands and adore”—then do they shout:
“Zarathustra is godless.”

And especially do their teachers of submission shout this;—but
precisely in their ears do I love to cry: “Yea! I AM Zarathustra, the
godless!”

Those teachers of submission! Wherever there is aught puny, or sickly,
or scabby, there do they creep like lice; and only my disgust preventeth
me from cracking them.

Well!
answer:
차라투스트라의 첫 가르침이 실패한 이유에 대해 참고 자료를 바탕으로 설명해 드리겠습니다.

차라투스트라가 가르침을 전하는 과정에서 겪는 어려움은 그의 가르침이 대중에게 제대로 받아들여지지 않았기 때문입니다. 참고 자료 3에 따르면, 사람들은 그가 왜 음란이나 악덕을 비난하지 않는지, 혹은 왜 소매치기를 경고하지 않는지 의아해합니다. 이는 사람들이 그가 제시하는 메시지를 이해하거나 받아들이는 데 어려움을 겪고 있음을 시사합니다.

더욱이, 차라투스트라는 자신을 '신(god)'으로 칭하는 것에 대해 사람들의 강한 반발에 직면합니다. 사람들이 그를 '신 없는 자(godless)'라고 외치며 거부하는 상황은 그의 가르침이 기존의 가치관이나 사회적 통념과 충돌했음을 보여줍니다. 특히 굴종을 가르치는 교사들이 반발하는 모습은 그의 급진적인 사상에 대한 사회적 저항이 거셌음을 나타냅니다.

ground_truth_en: Zarathustra's first teaching fails because he addressed the wrong audience — the marketplace crowd wants comfort, not self-overcoming; he learns he must seek companions, not preach to the herd.
chunks:
Zoroaster soon became aware of the existence of two primal spirits, the second being Angra Mainyu* (Destructive Spirit), with opposing concepts of Asha* (order) and Druj* (deception). Thus he decided to spend his life teaching people to seek Asha*. He received further revelations and saw a vision of the seven Amesha Spenta*, and his teachings were collected in the Gathas* and the Avesta*.

Eventually, at the age of about 42, Zoroaster received the patronage of queen Hutaosa and a ruler named Vishtaspa, an early adherent of Zoroastrianism (possibly from Bactria according to the Shahnameh).

According to the tradition, he lived for many years after Vishtaspa's conversion, managed to establish a faithful community, and married three times. His first two wives bore him three sons, Isat Vâstra, Urvatat Nara, and Hvare Chithra, and three daughters, Freni, Thriti, and Pouruchista. His third wife, Hvōvi, was childless.

## Notable influence on modern Western culture


A sculpture of Zoroaster by Edward Clark Potter, representing ancient Persian judicial wisdom and dating to 1896, towers over the Appellate Division Courthouse of New York State at East 25th Street and Madison Avenue in Manhattan. A sculpture of Zoroaster is included among other prominent religious figures in a procession representing major faith traditions on the south side of Rockefeller Memorial Chapel at the University of Chicago. It features figures from Abraham to the Reformation, illustrating a historical continuum of religious thought that includes the likes of Zoroaster, Moses, Plato and others.

Verily, all too well do I understand the dream’s portent and monition:
my DOCTRINE is in danger; tares want to be called wheat!

Mine enemies have grown powerful and have disfigured the likeness of
my doctrine, so that my dearest ones have to blush for the gifts that I
gave them.

Lost are my friends; the hour hath come for me to seek my lost ones!—

With these words Zarathustra started up, not however like a person in
anguish seeking relief, but rather like a seer and a singer whom the
spirit inspireth. With amazement did his eagle and serpent gaze upon
him: for a coming bliss overspread his countenance like the rosy dawn.

What hath happened unto me, mine animals?—said Zarathustra. Am I not
transformed? Hath not bliss come unto me like a whirlwind?

Foolish is my happiness, and foolish things will it speak: it is still
too young—so have patience with it!

Wounded am I by my happiness: all sufferers shall be physicians unto me!

To my friends can I again go down, and also to mine enemies!
answer:
차라투스트라의 첫 가르침이 실패한 이유는 그의 가르침을 대적하는 세력의 등장과 그로 인한 자신의 교리가 훼손되었기 때문입니다. 참고 자료 3에서 차라투스트라는 "나의 교리가 위험에 처했다(my DOCTRINE is in danger)"고 말하며, "나의 적들이 힘을 얻어 나의 교리의 모습을 훼손했기 때문에" 사랑하는 사람들이 자신이 준 선물들에 대해 부끄러워해야 한다고 토로합니다.

이러한 상황은 그의 가르침이 세상으로부터 인정받지 못하고 적대적인 힘에 의해 왜곡되면서 발생한 것으로 보입니다. 즉, 그의 이상적인 가르침(Asha*와 같은 질서)이 현실의 힘과 대립하면서 그 순수성이 위협받았고, 이로 인해 그는 자신의 가르침에 대한 위기감을 느끼게 된 것입니다.

ground_truth_en: Zarathustra's first teaching fails because he addressed the wrong audience — the marketplace crowd wants comfort, not self-overcoming; he learns he must seek companions, not preach to the herd.
chunks:
The crowd greets this speech with scorn and mockery, and the tightrope show begins, high above the audience. When the rope-dancer is halfway across, a clown comes up behind him, urging him to get out of the way. The clown then leaps over the rope-dancer, causing the latter to lose balance and plummet to the ground. The crowd scatters, but Zarathustra stays with the fatally injured tightrope performer and implies that he admires him for having chosen such a dangerous livelihood. Zarathustra later takes the corpse of the rope-dancer on his shoulders into the forest and lays it in a hollow tree. He decides that from this point on, he will no longer attempt to speak to the masses, but only to a few chosen disciples.

There follows a series of discourses in which Zarathustra overturns many of the precepts of Christian morality. He gathers a group of disciples, but ultimately abandons them, saying that he will not return until they have disowned him.

“How well ye know what consolation I devised for
myself in seven days!

That I have to sing once more—THAT consolation did I devise for myself,
and THIS convalescence: would ye also make another lyre-lay thereof?”

—“Do not talk further,” answered his animals once more; “rather, thou
convalescent, prepare for thyself first a lyre, a new lyre!

For behold, O Zarathustra! For thy new lays there are needed new lyres.

Sing and bubble over, O Zarathustra, heal thy soul with new lays: that
thou mayest bear thy great fate, which hath not yet been any one’s fate!

For thine animals know it well, O Zarathustra, who thou art and must
become: behold, THOU ART THE TEACHER OF THE ETERNAL RETURN,—that is now
THY fate!

That thou must be the first to teach this teaching—how could this great
fate not be thy greatest danger and infirmity!

## XLII. Redemption

When Zarathustra went one day over the great bridge, then did the
cripples and beggars surround him, and a hunchback spake thus unto him:

“Behold, Zarathustra! Even the people learn from thee, and acquire faith
in thy teaching: but for them to believe fully in thee, one thing is
still needful—thou must first of all convince us cripples! Here hast
thou now a fine selection, and verily, an opportunity with more than one
forelock! The blind canst thou heal, and make the lame run; and from
him who hath too much behind, couldst thou well, also, take away a
little;—that, I think, would be the right method to make the cripples
believe in Zarathustra!”

Zarathustra, however, answered thus unto him who so spake: When one
taketh his hump from the hunchback, then doth one take from him his
spirit—so do the people teach.
answer:
차라투스트라의 첫 가르침이 실패한 이유는 그가 대중에게 직접적으로 말하는 방식을 포기하고 소수의 선택받은 제자들에게만 집중하기로 결정했기 때문입니다. 그는 군중의 조롱과 비웃음을 경험한 후, 더 이상 대중에게 말하려 하지 않고 소수의 제자들에게만 가르침을 전하겠다고 선언합니다. 이는 대중의 수용을 기대했던 초기 방식이 좌절되었음을 시사합니다.

또한, 그는 기독교적 도덕률의 많은 부분을 뒤엎는 담론을 펼쳤습니다. 비록 그는 제자들을 모았지만, 결국 그들을 버리고 그들이 자신을 저주할 때까지 돌아오지 않겠다고 말합니다. 이는 그의 가르침이 보편적인 수용을 얻지 못하고, 오히려 기존의 도덕적 틀에 대한 거부와 갈등을 불러일으켰음을 보여줍니다.

결론적으로, 차라투스트라의 초기 시도는 대중의 지지를 얻는 데 실패했으며, 그는 이후 자신의 운명과 가르침의 본질에 대해 더 깊이 성찰하게 됩니다. 그는 자신의 운명, 즉 '영원 회귀(Eternal Return)'의 교사를 되기 위해 스스로를 시험하고 새로운 방식으로 접근해야 할 필요성을 깨닫게 됩니다.

ground_truth_en: Zarathustra's first teaching fails because he addressed the wrong audience — the marketplace crowd wants comfort, not self-overcoming; he learns he must seek companions, not preach to the herd.
chunks: In Thus Spoke Zarathustra* (1885), considered to be his seminal work, the philosopher Friedrich Nietzsche gives to the central character the native Iranian name "Zarathustra". Nietzsche explained the choice of the name in his autobiographical work Ecce Homo*: "Zarathustra was the first to see in the struggle of good and evil the true driving-wheel in the machinery of things [...] Zarathustra created* the disastrous error that is morality: thus he must also be the first to acknowledge* the mistake." Though the naming of Zarathustra is an acknowledgement of the lineage of moralism, Nietzsche advocates atheism and moral nihilism, directly contradicting the philosophy of Zoroaster, and makes subversive allusions to Zoroastrianism. Despite Nietzsche's iconoclasm, some admirers of pre-Islamic Persian culture see his use of the name "Zarathustra" as a mark of uncritical respect towards the historical figure.

The crowd greets this speech with scorn and mockery, and the tightrope show begins, high above the audience. When the rope-dancer is halfway across, a clown comes up behind him, urging him to get out of the way. The clown then leaps over the rope-dancer, causing the latter to lose balance and plummet to the ground. The crowd scatters, but Zarathustra stays with the fatally injured tightrope performer and implies that he admires him for having chosen such a dangerous livelihood. Zarathustra later takes the corpse of the rope-dancer on his shoulders into the forest and lays it in a hollow tree. He decides that from this point on, he will no longer attempt to speak to the masses, but only to a few chosen disciples.

There follows a series of discourses in which Zarathustra overturns many of the precepts of Christian morality. He gathers a group of disciples, but ultimately abandons them, saying that he will not return until they have disowned him.

“How well ye know what consolation I devised for
myself in seven days!

That I have to sing once more—THAT consolation did I devise for myself,
and THIS convalescence: would ye also make another lyre-lay thereof?”

—“Do not talk further,” answered his animals once more; “rather, thou
convalescent, prepare for thyself first a lyre, a new lyre!

For behold, O Zarathustra! For thy new lays there are needed new lyres.

Sing and bubble over, O Zarathustra, heal thy soul with new lays: that
thou mayest bear thy great fate, which hath not yet been any one’s fate!

For thine animals know it well, O Zarathustra, who thou art and must
become: behold, THOU ART THE TEACHER OF THE ETERNAL RETURN,—that is now
THY fate!

That thou must be the first to teach this teaching—how could this great
fate not be thy greatest danger and infirmity!
answer:
차라투스트라의 첫 가르침이 실패한 이유는 그가 기존의 도덕적 가르침, 특히 기독교적 도덕률을 전복시키려 했기 때문입니다. 그는 대중에게 호소하거나 많은 사람들을 이끌려는 시도 대신, 소수의 선택받은 제자들에게만 가르침을 전파하려 했습니다.

참고 자료에 따르면, 차라투스트라는 대중에게 말하는 것을 포기하고 소수의 제자들에게만 집중하기로 결정합니다. 이는 그가 기존의 도덕적 틀을 깨고 새로운 가치를 제시하려는 시도였으나, 결국 그가 제자들에게 자신을 거부당할 때까지 돌아오지 않겠다고 선언하면서 그 시도는 단절되었습니다.

결국, 그의 가르침이 실패한 것은 그가 '영원 회귀(Eternal Return)'라는 자신의 운명을 가르치는 첫 번째 사람이 되어야 한다는 엄청난 위험과 연약함을 직면했기 때문일 수 있습니다. 그는 이 위대한 운명을 가르치는 첫 번째 사람이 되어야 한다는 사실 자체가 그의 가장 큰 위험이자 연약함이었습니다.
